Example: a chess engine that plays against itself. > > **Labelled Block Diagram for a Goal-Based Agent:** > > ``` > Sensors --> State (Model) + Goal --> Search/Planning > | > v > Action --> Actuators > ``` > > *(1 mark per architecture × 5 = 5 marks, 3 marks for the block diagram and explanation)* > > **(b) Agent Function for the 2×1 Vacuum World:** > > The **agent function** $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ maps any percept sequence to an action. > > **Percepts** — The percept is the pair (Location, Status of current square). > - Location $\in \{A, B\}$: 2 options > - Status $\in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$: 2 options > - Total percepts: $2 \times 2 = 4$ > - Therefore $\vert \mathcal{P} \vert = 4$ and $\vert \mathcal{P}^* \vert$ is countably infinite (percept sequences). > > **Actions** — Left, Right, Suck, NoOp. > - Therefore $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = 4$. > > **Complete Agent Function Table:** > > | Percept (Location, Status) | Action | > |---|---| > | (A, Clean) | Right | > | (A, Dirty) | Suck | > | (B, Clean) | Left | > | (B, Dirty) | Suck | > > *(2 marks for percept/action size calculation, 2 marks for the table, 2 marks for the formal agent function definition)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Omitting the performance measure from agent definitions** — Examiners deduct **at least 1 mark** if the rational-agent definition lacks the expected-performance clause. > 2. **Confusing "omniscient" with "rational"** — A common error. Always distinguish: *omniscient* = knows actual outcomes; *rational* = maximizes *expected* performance. > 3. **Mixing up the agent function $f$ with the agent program** — The function is the abstract mathematical mapping; the program is its concrete implementation. > 4. **In PEAS questions, omitting Sensors and Actuators** — Both are mandatory columns. > 5. **In environment-classification questions, writing only the label without justification** — The justification carries **at least 1 mark per environment**. > 6. **In vacuum-world problems, forgetting the NoOp action** — Examiners frequently include NoOp in the action set to test attention to detail. > 7. **Misclassifying episodic vs. sequential** — A common error. Recall: *sequential* = current decision affects future decisions; *episodic* = decisions are independent. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Agent** = sensors + actuators + agent program; the *agent function* $f$ maps percept sequences to actions. - **Rationality** = selecting the action that *maximizes expected performance measure*, given the percept history and prior knowledge. - **Rational $\ne$ Omniscient** — an agent acts on what it perceives, not on unknowable future outcomes. - **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — the KTU-mandated task specification. - **Six environment dimensions**: Observability, Determinism, Episodicity, Staticness, Discreteness, Agent count. - **Five agent architectures** (in increasing capability): Simple Reflex $\rightarrow$ Model-Based Reflex $\rightarrow$ Goal-Based $\rightarrow$ Utility-Based $\rightarrow$ Learning Agent. - **Simple reflex** = current percept only; **Model-based** = internal state; **Goal-based** = planning; **Utility-based** = trade-offs via utility function; **Learning** = performance improves with experience. - **Learning agent** = Critic + Learning Element + Performance Element + Problem Generator. - **Vacuum world** is a 2-square, 2-status, 4-percept, 4-action toy environment often used to ground these concepts. - **The percept space $\mathcal{P}^*$** is the set of *all possible finite sequences* of percepts — not the percepts themselves. - **Performance is summed over time** — the rational agent maximizes the *cumulative* expected reward. - **Environment type dictates architecture** — always classify the environment first, then pick the matching agent. - **KTU hot-spot question** = "Classify the following environment along the six dimensions" — practice at least five such examples. - **KTU hot-spot question** = "Differentiate between two agent architectures" — use a comparison table; pure prose answers lose marks.

topics.title: Python for AI: Python Basic Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Python for AI: Python Basic Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — Python for AI: Python Basic Data Structures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Python Data Structures** are *specialized* container objects used to **store, organize, manage, and access** collections of related data items efficiently. In the context of Artificial Intelligence (AI), data structures are the **backbone** upon which datasets, feature vectors, model parameters, and knowledge representations are built.

The four primitive (built-in) data structures defined in the KTU OEITT613 Module 1 syllabus are:

| # | Data Structure | Syntax Symbol | Mutability | Order | Duplicates Allowed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **List** | `[ ]` | Mutable | Ordered | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | **Tuple** | `( )` | Immutable | Ordered | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | **Dictionary** | `{k:v}` | Mutable | Ordered (Py 3.7+) | Keys ❌ / Values ✅ |
| 4 | **Set** | `{ }` | Mutable | Unordered | ❌ No |

> [!NOTE]
> **AI Relevance:** Every AI algorithm — from a simple Linear Regression to a Deep Neural Network — depends on these structures. For instance, a **dataset** is a *list of dictionaries*, model **weights** are stored in *lists/tuples*, and **feature lookups** use *dictionaries*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine you are building an AI system that recognizes handwritten digits:

- **📋 List** → A *shopping list* where you can add, remove, or change items anytime. In AI, this is your **training dataset** (you keep adding new samples).
- **📦 Tuple** → A *sealed parcel* — once packed, you can't change it. In AI, this stores **fixed configuration** like `(learning_rate=0.01, epochs=50)`.
- **📖 Dictionary** → A *phone book* — you look up a name (key) to get a number (value). In AI, this maps **feature names → values** like `{"age": 25, "salary": 50000}`.
- **🎱 Set** → A *class attendance register* — only unique roll numbers, no duplicates. In AI, this holds **unique vocabulary tokens** or **unique class labels**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **Lists** as *flexible arrays*, **Tuples** as *read-only arrays*, **Dictionaries** as *hash maps*, and **Sets** as *mathematical sets* with O(1) membership testing.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Time Complexity** ranges from **O(1)** (direct access) to **O(n)** (linear scan) depending on the operation.
- **Space Complexity** is **O(n)** where **n** is the number of elements stored.
- **Hash Table Load Factor** for dictionaries/sets is maintained around **2/3** for optimal performance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Python Data Structures

### 2.1.1 📋 Lists — The Workhorse of AI

A **List** is an ordered, mutable, heterogeneous sequence. It is the most frequently used data structure in AI pipelines (e.g., storing feature vectors, batch samples, prediction outputs).

**Core Characteristics:**
- **Dynamic Sizing:** Grows/shrinks automatically (no fixed capacity).
- **Heterogeneous:** Can hold mixed types — `[1, "AI", 3.14, True]`.
- **Indexable:** Supports both **positive** (0-based) and **negative** indexing.
- **Slicing Support:** `list[start : stop : step]` enables sub-list extraction.

**Essential List Operations Table:**

| Operation | Syntax | Time Complexity | AI Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Append | `lst.append(x)` | **O(1)** amortized | Add training sample |
| Insert | `lst.insert(i, x)` | **O(n)** | Insert at specific position |
| Remove | `lst.remove(x)` | **O(n)** | Delete a data point |
| Pop | `lst.pop(i)` | **O(1)** for end | Pop batch element |
| Index | `lst[i]` | **O(1)** | Access pixel value |
| Slice | `lst[a:b]` | **O(k)** | Window of time-series data |
| Sort | `lst.sort()` | **O(n log n)** | Sort predictions by confidence |
| Length | `len(lst)` | **O(1)** | Get dataset size |

### 2.1.2 📦 Tuples — Immutable Sequences

A **Tuple** is an ordered, *immutable* sequence. Once created, elements cannot be modified, added, or removed.

**Why Tuples Matter in AI:**
- **Hashable** → can be used as **dictionary keys** and **set elements** (lists cannot).
- **Memory efficient** → uses less memory than a list of the same size.
- **Safe storage** → guarantees that critical parameters like `(image_height, image_width, channels)` are never accidentally modified.
- **Faster iteration** than lists in CPython.

**Tuple Unpacking (Critical for AI):**
```python
# Used heavily in ML libraries for returning multiple values
pred_class, confidence = model.predict(image)
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y)
```

### 2.1.3 📖 Dictionaries — The AI Feature Map

A **Dictionary** is an unordered (insertion-ordered since Python 3.7) collection of **key-value pairs** implemented as a **hash table**.

**Why Dictionaries are KING in AI:**
- **O(1) average lookup time** — critical when processing millions of features.
- Store **JSON-like data** (API responses, configuration files, dataset metadata).
- Form the basis of **hyperparameter dictionaries** in scikit-learn/TensorFlow/PyTorch.
- Used to implement **sparse representations** and **graph adjacency lists**.

**Dictionary Key Rules:**
- Keys must be **hashable** (immutable: strings, numbers, tuples).
- Keys must be **unique**.
- Values can be *anything* (even another dict or list).

### 2.1.4 🎱 Sets — Unordered Unique Collections

A **Set** is an unordered collection of **unique, hashable** elements. It is the Pythonic implementation of the mathematical *set theory*.

**Set Operations and AI Relevance:**

| Operation | Operator | Method | AI Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Union | `A ∣ B` | `A.union(B)` | Combine vocabularies |
| Intersection | `A & B` | `A.intersection(B)` | Common features |
| Difference | `A - B` | `A.difference(B)` | Stop-word removal |
| Symmetric Diff | `A ^ B` | `A.symmetric_difference(B)` | Disagreement set |
| Membership | — | `x in A` | **O(1)** lookup |
| Subset | `A ⊆ B` | `A.issubset(B)` | Vocabulary containment |

> [!NOTE]
> **AI Application — Text Preprocessing:** Stop-word removal in NLP is elegantly done using **set difference**:
> `meaningful_words = text_tokens - stopwords_set`

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| List Indexing | `lst[i]`, `lst[-i]` | Positive & negative indexing |
| Slicing | `lst[a:b:s]` | From index **a** to **b-1**, step **s** |
| List Length | $\text{len}(L) = n$ | Number of elements |
| Dict Lookup | $T_{lookup} = O(1)$ avg, $O(n)$ worst | Hash-table access |
| Set Membership | $T_{member} = O(1)$ avg | Hash-based search |
| List Membership | $T_{member} = O(n)$ | Linear scan |
| Sort Cost | $T_{sort} = O(n \log n)$ | Timsort algorithm |
| Hashing | $h(k) = k \bmod m$ | Index from key |
| Load Factor | $\alpha = n/m$ | Items / table size |
| Tuple Unpacking | $(a,b,c) = (1,2,3)$ | Multi-assignment |
| Dict Comprehension | `{k:v for k,v in iterable}` | Inline construction |
| List Comprehension | `[f(x) for x in iterable if cond]` | Inline list build |

> **Mathematical Notation Note:** In the table, $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown pipe-conflict. Use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Building a Mini AI Dataset

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of constructing a synthetic AI dataset using all four data structures, mirroring a real ML preprocessing pipeline.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Initialize the List of Records (Dataset)

**Conceptual Logic:** Each row of a dataset is a *sample*. We collect samples in a list because we need to **add, remove, and iterate** over them.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$\text{Dataset} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_n\}$$

where each $S_i$ is a dictionary (sample) containing feature-value pairs.

```python
# Step 1: Initialize dataset as an empty list
dataset = []
print(f"Initial dataset: {dataset}")
print(f"Type: {type(dataset)}")   # <class 'list'>
```

**Conversion Logic:** `[]` creates an empty list object in heap memory. Its reference is stored in the variable `dataset`.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Append Samples (Mutability in Action)

**Conceptual Logic:** Each new sample $S_i$ is represented as a **dictionary** with keys `name`, `age`, `salary`, `purchased` (label).

$$\text{Append Operation: } \text{len}(L_{n+1}) = \text{len}(L_n) + 1$$

```python
# Step 2: Create sample as a dictionary and append
sample1 = {"name": "Alice", "age": 25, "salary": 50000, "purchased": 1}
sample2 = {"name": "Bob",   "age": 40, "salary": 80000, "purchased": 0}
sample3 = {"name": "Carol", "age": 35, "salary": 65000, "purchased": 1}

dataset.append(sample1)
dataset.append(sample2)
dataset.append(sample3)

print(f"Dataset size: {len(dataset)}")   # 3
```

**Conversion Logic:** `append()` adds the dictionary reference at index `len(dataset)-1`. Time complexity: **O(1)** amortized.

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Feature Extraction Using List Comprehension

**Conceptual Logic:** We extract the `age` column as a separate list to feed into an AI model.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{age} = [S_i[\text{'age'}] \text{ for } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}]$$

$$\mathbf{y} = [S_i[\text{'purchased'}] \text{ for } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}]$$

```python
# Step 3: Extract features (X) and labels (y)
X_age    = [s["age"]      for s in dataset]
y_labels = [s["purchased"] for s in dataset]

print(f"X_age = {X_age}")        # [25, 40, 35]
print(f"y     = {y_labels}")    # [1, 0, 1]
```

**Conversion Logic:** List comprehension iterates over the dataset list. For each sample `s`, it retrieves the value at key `'age'` and builds a new list. Time complexity: **O(n)**.

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Tuple for Immutable Hyperparameters

**Conceptual Logic:** Hyperparameters must NOT change during training. Use a **tuple**.

$$\text{Config} = (lr, \text{epochs}, \text{batch\_size}) = (0.01, 50, 32)$$

```python
# Step 4: Hyperparameter tuple
config = (0.01, 50, 32)
learning_rate, epochs, batch_size = config   # Tuple unpacking
print(f"Learning Rate: {learning_rate}, Epochs: {epochs}")
```

**Conversion Logic:** Tuple packing creates an immutable sequence. Unpacking assigns each element to a variable based on positional index.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Set for Unique Vocabulary (NLP Use-Case)

**Conceptual Logic:** In NLP, we need the **unique vocabulary** from a corpus.

$$V = \{w \mid w \in \text{Corpus}\}$$

```python
# Step 5: Vocabulary set
corpus = ["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat", "the"]
vocab  = set(corpus)

print(f"Vocabulary: {vocab}")      # {'the', 'cat', 'sat', 'on', 'mat'}
print(f"Unique words: {len(vocab)}")  # 5
```

**Conversion Logic:** `set(corpus)` iterates over the list and inserts each element into a hash table; duplicates are automatically rejected.

---

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Stop-word Removal Using Set Difference

**Mathematical Operation:**

$$W_{filtered} = V - S_{stop}$$

where $S_{stop}$ is the set of stop-words.

```python
# Step 6: Stop-word removal
stopwords = {"the", "on", "a", "an", "is"}
meaningful = vocab - stopwords
print(f"Meaningful: {meaningful}")  # {'cat', 'sat', 'mat'}
```

**Conversion Logic:** The `-` operator calls `vocab.difference(stopwords)`, returning all elements in `vocab` not in `stopwords`.

---

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Dictionary for Model Configuration

**Conceptual Logic:** scikit-learn / TensorFlow models accept dictionaries of hyperparameters.

```python
# Step 7: Model config dictionary
model_params = {
    "learning_rate": 0.01,
    "optimizer":     "adam",
    "layers":        [128, 64, 10],   # List nested inside dict
    "dropout":       0.3
}
print(model_params["optimizer"])   # 'adam'
```

**Conversion Logic:** Dictionary access `d[k]` invokes the hash function $h(k) = \text{hash}(k) \bmod m$ where $m$ is the table size, achieving **O(1)** average lookup.

---

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Zip + Tuple Unpacking for Train-Test Split

**Mathematical Logic:**

$$(X_{train}, y_{train}), (X_{test}, y_{test}) = \text{split}(X, y, \text{ratio}=0.8)$$

```python
# Step 8: Manual split using zip & slicing
combined = list(zip(X_age, y_labels))   # [(25,1), (40,0), (35,1)]
train, test = combined[:2], combined[2:]
print(f"Train: {train}, Test: {test}")
```

**Conversion Logic:** `zip(X_age, y_labels)` pairs corresponding elements into tuples. Slicing `[:2]` and `[2:]` partitions the list. (Real libraries use `sklearn.model_selection.train_test_split`.)

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operation Complexity

| Data Structure | Indexing | Search | Insertion | Deletion | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **List** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ end / $O(n)$ mid | $O(n)$ | Moderate |
| **Tuple** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | ❌ | ❌ | Low |
| **Dict** | $O(1)$ key | $O(1)$ key | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | High |
| **Set** | ❌ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — AI Data Pipeline Using Python Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Data Source CSV JSON API] --> B0[Step 1 LIST dataset]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2 DICTIONARY per sample]

    D0 --> E0[Step 4 TUPLE for Hyperparameters]
```

```mermaid
    DC0[DICTIONARY Mutable Key Value Hashable Keys] -->|lookup feature| ML0

    L0 -.used for.-> ML0[AI Dataset Feature Vectors]

    style L0 fill:#FFE4B5
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need Key Value Lookup} -->|Yes| A1[Use DICTIONARY]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need Unique Elements Only}

    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to Modify After Creation}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Use LIST]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Use TUPLE for Safety and Speed]

    A1 --> USE1[Config JSON Model State]

    style A4 fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.4 ASCII Memory Layout — How a Dictionary is Stored Internally

```
+---------------------+      +-------------------+
|  dict_obj (header)  | ---> |  hash table array |
+---------------------+      +-------------------+
|  size  = 3          |      | idx | key -> val  |
|  used  = 3          |      |  0  | "age"->25   |
|  mask  = 7 (size-1) |      |  1  | "sal"->50k  |
+---------------------+      |  2  | "buy"->1    |
                             | ... empty slots   |
                             +-------------------+
   h("age")  = 203749184  ->  203749184 & 7  = 0  -> bucket 0
   h("sal")  =  59182381  ->   59182381 & 7  = 5  -> bucket 5
   h("buy")  = 998877665  ->  998877665 & 7  = 1  -> bucket 1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between Python Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries. Mention their mutability and the symbols used to define them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Structure | Symbol | Mutability | Order | Allows Duplicates |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **List** | `[ ]` | Mutable | Ordered | ✅ Yes |
| **Tuple** | `( )` | Immutable | Ordered | ✅ Yes |
| **Set** | `{ }` | Mutable | Unordered | ❌ No |
| **Dictionary** | `{k:v}` | Mutable (keys immutable) | Ordered (Py 3.7+) | Keys ❌, Values ✅ |

- Lists and tuples are **sequences**; sets and dictionaries are **non-sequence** collections. **(1 mark)**
- Tuples are **hashable** and can be used as dictionary keys; lists cannot. **(1 mark)**
- Sets provide **O(1)** membership testing, which is faster than lists. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Explain the significance of Python data structures in Artificial Intelligence applications. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Lists** are used to store **datasets, feature vectors, and prediction outputs** in AI models. Example: A list of pixel intensities of an image. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Dictionaries** are used for **hyperparameter configuration and JSON data handling** in ML libraries like scikit-learn and TensorFlow. Example: `{"lr": 0.01, "epochs": 50}`. **(1.5 marks)**

*(Bonus point: Sets are crucial in NLP for stop-word removal using set difference.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Don't confuse** mutability of *values* with mutability of *keys* in dictionaries.
> - **Don't claim** that tuples are completely useless because they are immutable — emphasize their **hashability, memory efficiency, and use as dict keys**.
> - **Don't forget** to mention **time complexity** when asked about operations — KTU examiners specifically test this.
> - **Don't write** Python code for a question that only asks for explanation — use **structured points/tables**.
> - **Common Mark Loss:** Forgetting to mention that **sets are unordered** (no indexing) — KTU deducts 1 mark for this.

---

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** — Lists & Tuples in AI

**Explain Python Lists and Tuples in detail. Discuss their properties, key operations, and how they are used in AI applications with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition & Syntax [2 marks]**
- A **List** is a mutable, ordered sequence defined using square brackets `[ ]`.
- A **Tuple** is an immutable, ordered sequence defined using parentheses `( )`.

**(ii) Properties [2 marks]**
- Lists support **append, insert, remove, sort, reverse, pop, extend**.
- Tuples support **count, index, len, min, max** but no modification methods.
- Lists are **heterogeneous**; tuples can also store mixed types.
- Tuples are **hashable** (if all elements are hashable) → can be dict keys / set members.

**(iii) AI Applications [3 marks]**
```python
# AI Use Case 1: Storing dataset
images = ["img1.jpg", "img2.jpg", "img3.jpg"]   # List of filenames

# AI Use Case 2: Hyperparameters (immutable)
config = (0.001, 100, 64)     # (learning_rate, epochs, batch_size)
lr, ep, bs = config           # tuple unpacking

# AI Use Case 3: RGB pixel value
pixel = (255, 128, 0)         # Orange pixel — never changes
```

---

#### **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** — Dictionaries & Sets in AI

**Explain Python Dictionaries and Sets with emphasis on their hash-table implementation and AI use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dictionary Definition [1.5 marks]**
A dictionary is a collection of **key-value pairs** implemented as a **hash table**. Lookup is **O(1)** on average. Defined as `{key: value}`.

**(ii) Hash Table Working [1.5 marks]**
- A hash function $h(k)$ converts a key into an integer index.
- The index is computed as $h(k) \bmod m$ where $m$ is the table size.
- Collisions are resolved using **open addressing** or **chaining**.

**(iii) Set Definition [1 mark]**
A set is an **unordered** collection of **unique, hashable** elements. It supports mathematical operations: union, intersection, difference.

**(iv) AI Applications [3 marks]**
```python
# Dictionary: feature lookup
patient = {"name": "John", "age": 45, "diagnosis": "diabetes"}
print(patient["age"])      # O(1) lookup

# Set: vocabulary in NLP
tokens   = ["the", "cat", "the", "sat"]
vocab    = set(tokens)     # {'the', 'cat', 'sat'}

# Set difference for stop-word removal
stopwords = {"the", "a", "an"}
clean     = vocab - stopwords   # {'cat', 'sat'}
```

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** — List Comprehensions & Slicing in AI

**Explain List Comprehensions and Slicing in Python. Show how they are used in feature engineering for AI.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) List Comprehension [3 marks]**
A list comprehension is a **concise way to create lists** using a single line of code.

**Syntax:**
$$[\text{expression} \text{ for } \text{item} \text{ in } \text{iterable} \text{ if } \text{condition}]$$

**Examples:**
```python
# Square of numbers (preprocessing)
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]

# Filter only even ages (feature selection)
ages = [25, 40, 35, 22, 60]
even_ages = [a for a in ages if a % 2 == 0]   # [40, 22, 60]

# Normalize salary column (feature scaling)
salaries = [50000, 80000, 65000]
normalized = [(s - min(salaries)) / (max(salaries) - min(salaries)) for s in salaries]
# [0.0, 1.0, 0.5]
```

**(ii) Slicing [2 marks]**
**Syntax:** `list[start : stop : step]`
```python
# Time-series windowing
data   = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60]
window = data[0:3]      # [10, 20, 30]  → first 3 samples
step   = data[::2]      # [10, 30, 50]  → every 2nd element
reverse= data[::-1]     # [60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10]
```

**(iii) AI Significance [2 marks]**
- Comprehensions enable **vectorized-style preprocessing** without explicit loops.
- Slicing is essential for **train-test split, batch creation, sliding window** in time-series AI.

---

#### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** — Set Operations & Dictionary Comprehensions in AI

**Demonstrate Python Set Operations (Union, Intersection, Difference) and Dictionary Comprehensions with AI-relevant examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Set Operations [3.5 marks]**

| Operation | Symbol | AI Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Union | $A \cup B$ | Merge two corpora |
| Intersection | $A \cap B$ | Common words between documents |
| Difference | $A - B$ | Remove stop-words |
| Symmetric Diff | $A \triangle B$ | Words unique to either document |

```python
# Example: Compare vocabularies of two documents
doc1_words = {"AI", "ML", "Python", "data"}
doc2_words = {"Python", "data", "deep", "learning"}

print(doc1_words & doc2_words)   # {'Python', 'data'}       → intersection
print(doc1_words | doc2_words)   # Union of all              → union
print(doc1_words - doc2_words)   # {'AI', 'ML'}              → unique to doc1
print(doc1_words ^ doc2_words)   # {'AI', 'ML', 'deep', 'learning'}
```

**(ii) Dictionary Comprehensions [2 marks]**

**Syntax:** `{key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable if condition}`

```python
# Create label-encoded mapping for AI classification
classes   = ["cat", "dog", "bird"]
label_map = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(classes)}
# {'cat': 0, 'dog': 1, 'bird': 2}

# One-hot encode small feature
one_hot = {c: [1 if i==j else 0 for j in range(len(classes))] 
           for i, c in enumerate(classes)}
```

**(iii) AI Pipeline Integration [1.5 marks]**
- Set operations → **deduplication, vocabulary building, stop-word removal, label uniqueness check**.
- Dict comprehensions → **feature encoding, label mapping, config generation**.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready Bullets)**

- ✅ Python has **4 built-in data structures**: `list`, `tuple`, `dict`, `set`.
- ✅ **Lists** `[ ]` are **mutable + ordered + allow duplicates** — used for datasets and feature vectors.
- ✅ **Tuples** `( )` are **immutable + ordered + hashable** — used for hyperparameters and dictionary keys.
- ✅ **Dictionaries** `{k:v}` provide **O(1) average lookup** via hash tables — used for config and feature maps.
- ✅ **Sets** `{ }` hold **unique, unordered, hashable** elements — used for vocabulary and stop-word removal.
- ✅ **Slicing syntax:** `list[start : stop : step]` with negative indices for reverse traversal.
- ✅ **List comprehensions** offer a **Pythonic, one-line** alternative to loops for creating new lists.
- ✅ **Dictionary comprehensions** use `{k:v for ...}` syntax.
- ✅ **Tuple unpacking** allows multi-variable assignment: `a, b, c = (1, 2, 3)`.
- ✅ **Set operations** (union, intersection, difference) use operators `|`, `&`, `-`.
- ✅ **Time Complexity Cheat-Sheet:** Indexing O(1), Search O(n) for lists, O(1) for dicts/sets.
- ✅ In AI, the typical pattern is: **List of Dicts** = Dataset, **Dict** = Config, **Set** = Vocabulary, **Tuple** = Hyperparameters.
- ✅ Remember the **hash table load factor** $\alpha = n/m$ for optimal dictionary performance.
- ✅ Strings in Python are **immutable sequences** (similar to tuples) and support slicing.
- ✅ **NumPy arrays** (covered later) extend lists for numerical AI but are **homogeneous** and **faster**.
- ✅ **Frozenset** is the **immutable version of a set** — also hashable.

> 🎓 **End of Module 1 Notes — Python Basic Data Structures for AI**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting Up Python Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Setting Up Python Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Python Environment (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Python Environment* is the complete collection of software components — the Python **interpreter**, the **standard library**, **third-party packages**, an **Integrated Development Environment (IDE) / text editor**, and the **system configuration (PATH variables and virtual environments)** — that together enable a developer to write, compile (byte-compile), execute, and debug Python programs on a computing platform.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on **"AI with Python" (OEITT613)**, the term *Setting Up Python Environment* explicitly refers to the sequence of installation and configuration tasks required before any AI/ML library (such as **NumPy**, **Pandas**, **Matplotlib**, **scikit-learn**, or **TensorFlow**) can be imported and used.

The three pillars of a working Python environment, as per the KTU 2024 module objectives, are:

1. **Python Interpreter** — the program that reads and executes Python source code (`.py` files).
2. **Package Manager** — a tool (typically `pip` or `conda`) used to install and manage external libraries.
3. **IDE / Code Editor** — a software application that provides syntax highlighting, code completion, debugging, and an integrated terminal.

## 1.2 Intuition — The Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Workshop Before the Carpenter Builds a Chair"**
>
> Imagine you want to become a carpenter. Before you can carve your first chair, you need:
> - A **workbench** (the IDE — where you work on your code)
> - A set of **tools** (Python interpreter, standard library)
> - A **toolbox for special tools** (package manager like `pip`)
> - A **dedicated corner of the workshop** for each project (virtual environment)
>
> Setting up your Python environment is exactly this — preparing the *workshop* before starting any AI project. Without it, even the most brilliant AI algorithm will not run on your machine.

## 1.3 Standard Components & Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Standard Tool / Value |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **Python Interpreter** | Translates Python code to machine-executable instructions | `python.exe` (Windows) / `python3` (Linux/macOS) |
| **Standard Library** | Built-in modules shipped with Python | `os`, `sys`, `math`, `datetime` |
| **pip** | Default Python package manager (since Python 3.4) | `pip --version` |
| **venv** | Built-in module to create isolated virtual environments | `python -m venv myenv` |
| **Anaconda** | Python distribution bundling 250+ pre-installed scientific packages | Includes `conda`, `Jupyter`, `Spyder` |
| **Jupyter Notebook** | Browser-based interactive environment (`.ipynb` files) | Launched via `jupyter notebook` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students are expected to be familiar with **both** the standard CPython distribution *and* the **Anaconda distribution**, because AI workflows (Module 2 onwards) rely heavily on Anaconda's bundled scientific stack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Python Execution Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

When a Python script is executed, the environment processes it through a well-defined pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Lexical Analysis:** The source code (`.py` file) is broken into **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals).
- **Step 2 — Parsing:** Tokens are arranged into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** that represents the program's grammatical structure.
- **Step 3 — Bytecode Compilation:** The AST is compiled into **bytecode** (`.pyc` files), which is a lower-level, platform-independent representation of the code.
- **Step 4 — Execution by PVM:** The **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** interprets the bytecode instruction by instruction and produces the final output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Understanding this pipeline is essential because debugging, performance tuning, and environment configuration all interact with these stages. For example, `.pyc` cache files live in the `__pycache__` directory inside your project folder.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Command / Path | Notes |
|---------|---------------|-------|
| Check Python version | `python --version` | Must be **3.8+** for AI libraries (TF 2.x requires ≥ 3.7) |
| Check pip version | `pip --version` | Bundled automatically with Python 3.4+ |
| Install a package | `pip install numpy` | Downloads from **PyPI** (Python Package Index) |
| Install specific version | `pip install numpy==1.24.0` | Pinning avoids version conflicts |
| List installed packages | `pip list` | Shows all packages in current env |
| Create virtual env | `python -m venv ai_env` | Creates folder `ai_env/` |
| Activate (Windows) | `ai_env\Scripts\activate` | Prompt shows `(ai_env)` prefix |
| Activate (Linux/macOS) | `source ai_env/bin/activate` | Same `(ai_env)` prefix |
| Deactivate | `deactivate` | Returns to global interpreter |
| Launch Jupyter | `jupyter notebook` | Opens browser at `localhost:8888` |
| Run script | `python script.py` | Executes from current directory |
| PATH variable | System environment variable | Tells OS where to find `python.exe` |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

In industry, a properly configured Python environment is the **first line of defense** against:
- **Dependency Hell** — when two projects need different versions of the same library (solved by virtual environments).
- **Cross-platform Inconsistencies** — code behaving differently on Windows vs Linux.
- **Reproducibility Issues** — AI experiments failing to reproduce because the package versions differ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application Note:** In Module 2 (NumPy & Pandas) and Module 4 (Machine Learning libraries), you will repeatedly be required to **activate the correct virtual environment** before running any AI code. This is a routine 2-mark short-answer question in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 Recommended Setup Workflow (KTU-Approved)

The KTU 2024 Scheme suggests the following **two-track** approach:

**Track A — Lightweight (Standard CPython + VS Code):**
1. Download Python from **python.org**.
2. During installation, **check "Add Python to PATH"** ✓.
3. Install **Visual Studio Code** with the Python extension.
4. Use `pip` to install NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib as needed.

**Track B — Heavyweight (Anaconda Distribution):**
1. Download **Anaconda Individual Edition** from anaconda.com.
2. Install with default options (≈ 3 GB disk space).
3. Launch **Anaconda Navigator** to access Jupyter, Spyder, VS Code.
4. Use `conda install` or `pip install` inside the Anaconda Prompt.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Setup Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Installation Walkthrough (Windows 10/11)

### Step 1 — Download the Python Installer

Navigate to the official Python download page and select the latest stable **Python 3.11.x** (or the version prescribed by your KTU lab manual). The installer file is named something like `python-3.11.9-amd64.exe`.

### Step 2 — Run the Installer with Critical Options

When the installer window appears, you will see two checkboxes at the bottom:

- **"Install launcher for all users (recommended)"** → Leave **checked** ✓
- **"Add Python 3.11 to PATH"** → **CHECK THIS BOX** ✓ (This is the most commonly missed step!)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If "Add to PATH" is **not** checked, every subsequent `python` command in Command Prompt will return the error `'python' is not recognized as an internal or external command`. Students often lose 2 marks in viva for this exact mistake.

Then click **"Install Now"** and wait for the progress bar to complete. A successful installation shows the message **"Setup was successful"**.

### Step 3 — Verify the Installation via Command Prompt

Open **Command Prompt** (`Win + R` → type `cmd` → Enter) and execute the following commands one by one:

```
python --version
```

Expected output: `Python 3.11.9`

```
python -m pip --version
```

Expected output: `pip 23.2.1 from C:\Users\YourName\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python311\lib\site-packages\pip (python 3.11)`

```
python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"
```

Expected output: `C:\Users\YourName\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python311\python.exe`

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The `sys.executable` path tells you *which* Python interpreter is being invoked. This becomes critical when you have multiple Python versions installed.

### Step 4 — Create and Activate a Virtual Environment

A virtual environment is a self-contained directory tree that contains a Python installation plus a number of additional packages. It isolates your project's dependencies from the global system Python.

```
mkdir C:\KTU_AI_Projects
cd C:\KTU_AI_Projects
python -m venv ai_env
ai_env\Scripts\activate
```

After activation, your Command Prompt prompt will be prefixed with `(ai_env)`, indicating that the virtual environment is active.

### Step 5 — Install AI/ML Packages Inside the Virtual Environment

With `(ai_env)` active in the prompt, install the foundational packages:

```
pip install numpy
pip install pandas
pip install matplotlib
pip install scikit-learn
```

Each command will:
1. Resolve the package and its **dependencies** from PyPI.
2. Download the `.whl` (wheel) files.
3. Install them into `ai_env\Lib\site-packages\`.

### Step 6 — Verify Package Installation

```
python -c "import numpy as np; import pandas as pd; import sklearn; print('AI Stack OK')"
```

Expected output: `AI Stack OK`

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Environment Variables (Linux/macOS Reference)

For KTU students using Linux lab systems, the following environment variables are essential:

$$PATH = \text{colon-separated list of directories searched for executables}$$

For Python to be invoked globally, the directory containing the `python3` binary must appear in `$PATH`. Verify using:

```
echo $PATH
```

The **PYTHONPATH** variable, if set, is prepended to `sys.path` and tells Python where to look for additional module imports:

$$sys.path = [PYTHONPATH] \cup [\text{script directory}] \cup [\text{default site-packages}]$$

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Which Setup Should I Choose?

| Criterion | Standard CPython + VS Code | Anaconda Distribution |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Disk Space Required | ≈ 200 MB | ≈ 3 GB |
| Pre-installed AI Libraries | None (manual `pip install`) | NumPy, Pandas, Jupyter, Spyder pre-installed |
| Package Manager | `pip` only | `conda` + `pip` |
| Beginner Friendly | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ |
| KTU Lab Availability | Common on CSE dept. machines | Often pre-installed in AI/ML labs |
| Best Use Case | Lightweight scripting, web dev | Data science, AI/ML coursework |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Recommendation:** Use **Anaconda** for Modules 2–5 (AI/ML heavy), and use **standard CPython** for Modules 1 and 6 (general Python programming).

## 3.4 First Program — The "Hello, AI" Sanity Check

Create a file named `hello_ai.py` in `C:\KTU_AI_Projects\` with the following content:

```python
# hello_ai.py
# A KTU-sanctioned sanity check for your Python environment.

import sys
import platform

print("=" * 50)
print(" KTU 2024 - Python Environment Self-Check ")
print("=" * 50)
print(f"Python Version : {sys.version}")
print(f"Platform       : {platform.system()} {platform.machine()}")
print(f"Executable Path: {sys.executable}")
print("=" * 50)
print(" Environment is READY for AI development! ")
print("=" * 50)
```

Run it from the activated virtual environment:

```
python hello_ai.py
```

A successful run confirms that your interpreter, standard library, and terminal are all correctly configured.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Python Environment — Layered Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **layered architecture** of a fully-configured Python environment, from the hardware up to the application code:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1 - HARDWARE"]:::hw
    L2["LAYER 2 - OPERATING SYSTEM"]:::os
    L3["LAYER 3 - PYTHON INTERPRETER CPYTHON 3.11"]:::core
    L4["LAYER 4 - STANDARD LIBRARY"]:::core
    L5["LAYER 5 - PACKAGE MANAGER PIP OR CONDA"]:::pkg
    L6["LAYER 6 - VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENT AI_ENV"]:::env
    L7["LAYER 7 - THIRD PARTY LIBRARIES NUMPY PANDAS SKLEARN"]:::lib
    L8["LAYER 8 - IDE OR EDITOR VS CODE JUPYTER PYCHARM"]:::ide
    L9["LAYER 9 - USER PYTHON CODE HELLO AI PY"]:::user

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> L8 --> L9

    classDef hw fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef os fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Setup Workflow — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START - Download Python 3.11 from python.org"]:::startNode
    B["Run Installer - Check Add Python to PATH"]:::setupNode
```

## 4.3 Virtual Environment Isolation — Conceptual Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL PYTHON - SYSTEM LEVEL"]
        G1["Python 3.11"]:::globalStyle
        G2["pip 23.2"]:::globalStyle
        G3["No AI packages"]:::globalStyle
    end

    subgraph PROJ1["PROJECT A - AI CHATBOT"]
        A1["Python 3.11 copy"]:::projAStyle
        A2["tensorflow 2.13"]:::projAStyle
        A3["numpy 1.24"]:::projAStyle
    end

    subgraph PROJ2["PROJECT B - DATA ANALYSIS"]
        B1["Python 3.11 copy"]:::projBStyle
        B2["pandas 2.0"]:::projBStyle
        B3["matplotlib 3.7"]:::projBStyle
    end

    GLOBAL --- PROJ1
    GLOBAL --- PROJ2
    PROJ1 -. "isolated" .- PROJ2

    classDef globalStyle fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    classDef projAStyle fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
```
