By averaging over the **uniform** distribution of blank positions: $$ b = \frac{1}{9}\bigl(4 \times 1 + 3 \times 4 + 2 \times 4\bigr) = \frac{4 + 12 + 8}{9} = \frac{24}{9} \approx 2.67 $$ ### 3.1.3 Estimating Search Complexity If we use **Breadth-First Search** with branching factor $b = 2.67$ and optimal solution depth $d = 22$ (well-known for 8-puzzle), the number of nodes expanded is bounded by $$ N_{\text{BFS}} = \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^{i} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} \approx \frac{(2.67)^{23} - 1}{1.67} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{9} $$ That is roughly **one billion nodes** — a strong argument for using **A$^*$ search with a heuristic** instead of uninformed BFS. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Vacuum World (Single-Cell Variant) Consider a one-cell world with a vacuum cleaner. There is a single cell, the cleaner can be in the cell or outside, and the cell can be dirty or clean. We derive the state space. ### 3.2.1 State Variables The state is determined by two binary variables: $$ s = (d, c), \quad d \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}, \quad c \in \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}\} $$ ### 3.2.2 The State Set By the **product rule** of counting, the total number of states is $$ \vert S \vert = \vert D_d \vert \times \vert D_c \vert = 2 \times 2 = 4 $$ The four states are: $$ S = \{(\text{Clean}, L), (\text{Clean}, R), (\text{Dirty}, L), (\text{Dirty}, R)\} $$ ### 3.2.3 Action Set and Transition Model The agent can perform three actions: $$ A = \{\text{Suck}, \text{MoveLeft}, \text{MoveRight}\} $$ The transition model is enumerated exhaustively: $$ T((\text{Dirty}, L), \text{Suck}) = (\text{Clean}, L) $$ $$ T((\text{Dirty}, R), \text{Suck}) = (\text{Clean}, R) $$ $$ T((d, L), \text{MoveLeft}) = (d, L) \quad \text{(no-op)} $$ $$ T((d, R), \text{MoveRight}) = (d, R) \quad \text{(no-op)} $$ $$ T((d, L), \text{MoveRight}) = (d, R) $$ $$ T((d, R), \text{MoveLeft}) = (d, L) $$ ### 3.2.4 Goal Set A clean cell irrespective of robot position is a goal: $$ G = \{(\text{Clean}, L), (\text{Clean}, R)\} $$ Equivalently, $\text{GoalTest}(s) = (s.d = \text{Clean})$. ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Missionaries and Cannibals A classic constraint-satisfaction problem: 3 missionaries and 3 cannibals must cross a river using a boat that carries at most 2. Cannibals must never outnumber missionaries on either bank. ### 3.3.1 State Representation $$ s = (M_l, C_l, B), \quad M_l, C_l \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}, \quad B \in \{L, R\} $$ where $M_l$ and $C_l$ are missionaries and cannibals on the **left** bank; the right-bank counts are $M_r = 3 - M_l$ and $C_r = 3 - C_l$. The boat's position $B$ tells us which side is currently holding the boat. ### 3.3.2 Total State Count (Upper Bound) $$ \vert S \vert_{\max} = 4 \times 4 \times 2 = 32 $$ But the safety constraint (no outnumbering) and the boat constraint reduce the *valid* states to about $\vert S \vert = 16$. ### 3.3.3 Action Set A boat trip $a$ is a tuple $(m, c)$ with $m + c \in \{1, 2\}$ and $m, c \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. There are six possible boat trips: $$ A = \{(1,0), (2,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,1), (2,1)\} $$ ### 3.3.4 Initial and Goal States $$ s_0 = (3, 3, L), \quad G = \{(0, 0, R)\} $$ ## 3.4 Constructing a Search Tree Given the transition model, we can grow a **search tree** rooted at $s_0$. Each node represents a *state reached during search*; each edge is labelled with an action. The same state may appear in many nodes because the tree duplicates states, whereas the **state-space graph** (a DAG) does not. > [!NOTE] > **Tree vs. Graph — Examiner's Favourite Trap** > A *search tree* expands every path independently and can contain the same state multiple times. A *state-space graph* is the underlying DAG, with each state appearing exactly once. A correct state-space representation uses a graph; a correct *search* data structure is a tree. ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Step-by-Step State Expansion for the 8-Puzzle Starting from $s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 6, 7, 5, 8)$, the four possible legal actions (blank at position 4 has 4 neighbours: 1, 3, 5, 7) give the four children: $$ s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 6, 7, 5, 8) \xrightarrow{\text{Up}} (1, 0, 3, 4, 2, 6, 7, 5, 8) $$ $$ s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 6, 7, 5, 8) \xrightarrow{\text{Down}} (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 8) $$ $$ s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 6, 7, 5, 8) \xrightarrow{\text{Left}} (1, 2, 3, 0, 4, 6, 7, 5, 8) $$ $$ s_0 = (1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 6, 7, 5, 8) \xrightarrow{\text{Right}} (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 0, 7, 5, 8) $$ Each of these four states is itself the root of a sub-tree of depth up to 22 from the goal. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — State-Space Graph of the 8-Puzzle (Partial View) The figure below is a *partial* state-space graph for the 8-puzzle. Each node is a state; each edge is an action. The graph is undirected because every action is reversible. ```mermaid graph LR s0["S0 initial 1 2 3 4 B 6 7 5 8"]:::start s1["S1 blank up 1 B 3 4 2 6 7 5 8"] s2["S2 blank down 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 B 8"] s3["S3 blank left 1 2 3 B 4 6 7 5 8"] s4["S4 blank right 1 2 3 4 6 B 7 5 8"] s5["S5 from S2 down 1 2 3 4 5 6 B 7 8"] goal["SG goal 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 B"]:::goal s0 -->|Up| s1 s0 -->|Down| s2 s0 -->|Left| s3 s0 -->|Right| s4 s2 -->|Down| s5 s5 -->|Right| goal classDef start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef goal fill:#9CE89C,stroke:#1A8A1A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram** > - The **orange** node $S_0$ is the *initial state*; the **green** node $S_G$ is the *goal state*. > - Each **edge** is labelled with an action name; the edge direction is the direction the *blank* moves. > - A *complete* state-space graph for the 8-puzzle has **181,440** nodes and roughly **1.2 million** edges. Only a tiny fraction is shown. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Search Tree (BFS) for the Same 8-Puzzle ```mermaid graph TD root["S0 initial"]:::start a1["S1 Up"] a2["S2 Down"] a3["S3 Left"] a4["S4 Right"] b1["S11 from S1 Right"] b2["S12 from S1 Down"] b3["S21 from S2 Up"] b4["S22 from S2 Left"] b5["S23 from S2 Right"] b6["S31 from S3 Up"] b7["S32 from S3 Right"] b8["S41 from S4 Up"] b9["S42 from S4 Left"] goal["SG goal"]:::goal root --> a1 root --> a2 root --> a3 root --> a4 a1 --> b1 a1 --> b2 a2 --> b3 a2 --> b4 a2 --> b5 a3 --> b6 a3 --> b7 a4 --> b8 a4 --> b9 b1 --> goal classDef start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef goal fill:#9CE89C,stroke:#1A8A1A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why is the search tree larger than the state-space graph?** > Because the *tree* duplicates states. In the tree, the goal state $S_G$ might appear under multiple branches, but in the *graph* it appears exactly once. A sound AI algorithm must therefore maintain a **closed set / visited list** to prune duplicates. ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Problem-Solving Agent ```mermaid graph TD perc["Perception Module
Sensors"]:::io form["Problem Formulation
S, A, s0, G"]:::think goalF["Goal Formulation
GoalTest"]:::think search["Search Algorithm
BFS, DFS, A star"]:::think exec["Action Executor
Apply a in world"]:::io env["Environment
World State"]:::env perf["Performance
Measure"]:::eval perc -->|raw input| form form -->|initial state| search goalF -->|goal set| search search -->|action a| exec exec -->|actuator signal| env env -->|new state| perc env -->|state outcome| perf perf -->|feedback| search classDef io fill:#A8C8FF,stroke:#1F4E9D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef think fill:#FFE680,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef env fill:#D4D4D4,stroke:#555,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef eval fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#2E8B2E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the block diagram** > The agent operates in a **perception → formulation → search → execution** loop. The *Problem Formulation* module is responsible for converting raw sensor data into the abstract state $s_0$ and the action set $A$. The *Search* module uses these to find a path to $G$. The *Action Executor* applies each action in the world, producing a new state that the cycle restarts. ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Missionaries and Cannibals State Graph ```mermaid graph LR n1["M3 C3 B left"]:::start n2["M3 C1 B left"] n3["M3 C2 B left"] n4["M2 C2 B left"] n5["M1 C1 B left"] n6["M0 C0 B right"]:::goal n7["M0 C1 B right"] n8["M0 C2 B right"] n9["M1 C2 B right"] n10["M2 C3 B right"] n1 -->|1C crosses| n2 n1 -->|2C cross| n3 n2 -->|2M return| n4 n3 -->|1M 1C return| n4 n4 -->|2C cross| n5 n5 -->|1C returns| n7 n4 -->|1M 1C cross| n9 n5 -->|2M cross| n9 n9 -->|1M 1C return| n10 n10 -->|2C cross| n6 classDef start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef goal fill:#9CE89C,stroke:#1A8A1A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram** > Only states that satisfy the safety constraint *($M_l \geq C_l$ or $M_l = 0$) and ($M_r \geq C_r$ or $M_r = 0$)* are kept. The bold *orange* node is the start, the *green* node is the goal, and each edge label is a boat trip. Notice how the state space is dramatically reduced from 32 raw states to 16 valid states by applying domain constraints — a key principle of *problem formulation*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks each, expects a precise 3–4 line answer with a clean definition or formula. No lengthy derivations. ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define state space representation. List its four components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** State space representation is a formal framework used in AI to define a problem mathematically. It consists of four components: the **state set $S$** (all possible configurations of the world), the **action set $A$** (all legal operators), the **initial state $s_0$** (the agent's starting configuration), and the **goal set $G$** (the desired target states). A problem is thus denoted $P = (S, A, s_0, G)$. (3 marks) ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between explicit goal set and goal-test predicate with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **explicit goal set** lists every goal state explicitly, e.g., $G = \{(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,0)\}$ for the 8-puzzle. A **goal-test predicate** is a Boolean function $\text{GoalTest}(s)$ that returns *True* iff $s$ is a goal, e.g., $\text{GoalTest}((a_1,\dots,a_9)) \equiv (a_1,\dots,a_9) = (1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,0)$. The two are mathematically equivalent; the predicate form is preferred when the goal set is large or infinite. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, has internal choice (a) and (b) sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR two 7-mark sub-parts from the same module. Sub-part (a) tests *concepts*; sub-part (b) tests *application / numerical*. ### **Question A** (14 Marks) #### **Q3 (a).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **With a neat diagram, explain the components of a state space representation. How is it different from a search tree? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **State Space Representation Components (4 marks):** A state space representation formally describes an AI problem as a quadruple $P = (S, A, s_0, G)$. 1. **State Set $S$** — the complete set of all possible configurations the world may be in. Example: for the 8-puzzle, $S$ contains all 181,440 valid permutations of 9 tiles. 2. **Action Set $A$** — the set of permissible operations, e.g., $A = \{\text{Up, Down, Left, Right}\}$ in the 8-puzzle. 3. **Initial State $s_0$** — the unique starting configuration, e.g., the scrambled puzzle at the start. 4. **Goal Set $G$** — the subset of states that satisfy the goal condition. A **transition model** $T(s, a) = s'$ defines the new state produced by applying action $a$ in state $s$. **State-Space Graph vs. Search Tree (3 marks):** | Aspect | State-Space Graph | Search Tree | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Node definition | Each *unique* state appears once | Each *occurrence* of a state is a separate node | | Edge definition | An action between two states | An action in a specific search path | | Duplicate states | Not allowed | Allowed and common | | Cycles | No directed cycles (it's a DAG) | Can contain back-edges if no closed list | | Use | *Problem definition* | *Algorithm execution* | The search tree is a *time-stamped expansion* of the state-space graph; the graph is the static underlying structure. #### **Q3 (b).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Formulate the 8-puzzle as a state space problem. Calculate the branching factor and the total number of valid states. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Formulation (4 marks):** - **State**: A 9-tuple $s = (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i)$ giving the row-major order of the board, with $0$ denoting the blank. - **State set $S$**: All valid permutations of $\{0, 1, \dots, 8\}$ such that the permutation parity matches a reachable parity. - **Action set $A$**: $\{\text{Up, Down, Left, Right}\}$ — sliding the blank one square in the named direction. - **Initial state $s_0$**: The scrambled starting configuration given in the problem. - **Goal set $G$**: $\{(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)\}$. - **Transition model $T$**: Swap the blank with the tile in the direction of the move; reject moves that cross the grid boundary. - **Step cost $c$**: $1$ per move. **Calculations (3 marks):** Total permutations: $9! = 362{,}880$. Because the blank must travel an even Manhattan distance to reach any position, the board splits into two *parity classes* of equal size. Hence: $$ \vert S \vert = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440 $$ Branching factor (averaging over 9 blank positions — 1 center with 4 neighbours, 4 edges with 3 neighbours, 4 corners with 2 neighbours): $$ b = \frac{1}{9}(1 \cdot 4 + 4 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot 2) = \frac{4 + 12 + 8}{9} = \frac{24}{9} \approx 2.67 $$ (7 marks) --- ### **Question B** (Alternative — 14 Marks) #### **Q4 (a).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain goal formulation and problem formulation with the example of a vacuum-cleaner world. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Problem Formulation (4 marks):** Problem formulation is the design decision of *which states and actions to include* in the AI model. We must balance expressiveness against tractability. For the **2-cell vacuum world**: - **States**: $S = \{(d, r) \mid d \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}, r \in \{L, R\}\}$, so $\vert S \vert = 4$. - **Actions**: $A = \{\text{Suck, MoveLeft, MoveRight}\}$. - **Initial state**: e.g., $s_0 = (\text{Dirty}, L)$. - **Goal test**: $\text{GoalTest}(s) \equiv (s.d = \text{Clean})$. **Goal Formulation (3 marks):** Goal formulation is the *declaration of what counts as success*. The choice of goal dramatically changes the agent's behaviour. For the same vacuum world, three possible goal formulations are: 1. **Single goal**: "*Clean both squares*" — $G = \{(\text{Clean}, L), (\text{Clean}, R)\}$. 2. **Sequence goal**: "*First clean left, then right*" — requires an *order*, used in planning. 3. **Utility-based**: Maximize cleanliness over time (no fixed goal; uses a reward function instead). Goal formulation depends on the *task environment*. A well-designed goal should be *achievable* (no impossible states) and *informative* (clearly distinguishes success from failure). #### **Q4 (b).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **For the missionaries-and-cannibals problem with 3 missionaries and 3 cannibals, derive the state space size and explain the safety constraint. Show the state transition for the first two moves. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **State Definition (2 marks):** A state is $s = (M_l, C_l, B)$ where $M_l, C_l \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ are the missionaries and cannibals on the left bank and $B \in \{L, R\}$ is the boat position. **Total States (2 marks):** Without constraints, $\vert S \vert = 4 \times 4 \times 2 = 32$. The **safety constraint** is: on *both* banks, missionaries must not be outnumbered by cannibals (unless no missionaries are present). $$ \text{Safety}(M, C) \iff (M = 0) \lor (M \geq C) $$ Applying this to *both* banks reduces the valid states to **16**. **State Transitions (3 marks):** Start: $s_0 = (3, 3, L)$. - **Move 1**: Two cannibals cross right. New state $s_1 = (3, 1, R)$. Check safety: Left = $(3, 1)$ OK; Right = $(0, 2)$ OK. - **Move 2**: One cannibal returns left. New state $s_2 = (3, 2, L)$. Check safety: Left = $(3, 2)$ OK; Right = $(0, 1)$ OK. These first two moves illustrate the standard opening sequence that all optimal solutions of the 3-3 problem follow. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write** > 1. **Do not confuse the search tree with the state-space graph.** Examiners award a *dedicated 2-mark band* for this distinction; missing it costs full marks on that sub-question. > 2. **Always quote the formula $P = (S, A, s_0, G)$** in the first line of any Part B answer. It earns an *easy 1 mark* and sets the academic tone. > 3. **State-space size for the 8-puzzle is $9!/2$, not $9!$**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting the parity factor. > 4. **For the missionaries-cannibals problem, you must state the safety constraint explicitly**; merely writing the state set without the constraint loses 1 mark. > 5. **In Part A, do not exceed 4 lines.** KTU examiners have a strict word-count rubric; verbosity is penalised. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Carry** - [ ] **State Space Definition** = quadruple $P = (S, A, s_0, G)$. Memorize this formula verbatim. - [ ] **State** = complete description of the world at one instant (use the *relevance principle*). - [ ] **Initial state $s_0$** = the unique starting configuration; deterministic problems have exactly one. - [ ] **Action $a$** = a transformation rule; legal actions only. - [ ] **Transition model $T(s, a) = s'$** = the result of action $a$ in state $s$. - [ ] **Successor function** $\text{Succ}(s)$ = set of all directly reachable states from $s$. - [ ] **Goal $G$** = either an explicit set or a Boolean predicate; both forms equivalent. - [ ] **Path cost $c(\pi)$** = sum of step costs; needed to compare solutions. - [ ] **State-space graph** = underlying DAG, each state appears once. - [ ] **Search tree** = expansion of the graph, may duplicate states. - [ ] **Branching factor $b$** = average $\vert \text{Succ}(s) \vert$; the 8-puzzle $b \approx 2.67$. - [ ] **8-puzzle size** = $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ valid states. - [ ] **8-puzzle actions** = $\{\text{Up, Down, Left, Right}\}$ moves of the blank. - [ ] **Missionaries-Cannibals safety**: $M \geq C$ or $M = 0$ on each bank. - [ ] **Problem formulation** = choice of states and actions; **Goal formulation** = choice of target. - [ ] **Continuous state spaces** must be discretized before classical search is applicable. - [ ] **Real-world analogy** — GPS navigation: state = location, action = turn, goal = destination. - [ ] **Engineering applications** — robotics, theorem proving, network routing, drug discovery, game AI. - [ ] **Visited list (closed set)** prevents redundant state revisits in search algorithms. - [ ] **Deterministic** transition returns one $s'$; **Stochastic** returns a probability distribution. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip** > When the question says *"Formulate X as a state space problem"*, immediately write the quadruple $P = (S, A, s_0, G)$, define each symbol, and (if applicable) compute the state-space size and branching factor. This single structure satisfies ~70% of the marks.

topics.title: Problem Characteristics: Problem Decomposability, Solution Steps Consideration, Problem Solution Predictability, Absolute or Relative Problem Solution, State or Path Solution, Role of Knowledge in Problem Solving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Problem Characteristics: Problem Decomposability, Solution Steps Consideration, Problem Solution Predictability, Absolute or Relative Problem Solution, State or Path Solution, Role of Knowledge in Problem Solving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Problem Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Problem characteristics* in Artificial Intelligence refer to a set of formal classification dimensions used to analyze and categorize a problem *before* designing a solution strategy. These dimensions dictate the choice of **search algorithm**, **knowledge representation scheme**, and **reasoning mechanism** that an AI agent will employ.

In classical AI (as described in foundational AI texts), the seven primary characteristics used to analyze a problem are:

1. **Decomposability** — Can the problem be broken into smaller, independent sub-problems?
2. **Solution Steps Consideration** — Can solution steps be ignored, recovered, or must they be preserved?
3. **Predictability** — Is the outcome of a solution step known with certainty?
4. **Absolute vs Relative Solution** — Is the solution a definite goal state or a comparative measure?
5. **State vs Path Solution** — Is only the final state important, or is the path significant?
6. **Role of Knowledge** — Does the problem require vast domain knowledge, or only raw computation?
7. **Nature of the Solution** — Is the solution a single answer or multiple acceptable answers?

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2) explicitly focuses on the **first six characteristics** as listed above. These were originally proposed by **George F. Luger** and **Stuart Russell & Peter Norvig** in their respective AI textbooks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Mental Model

Think of solving an AI problem as planning a **road trip from Calicut to Trivandrum**.

| Problem Characteristic | GPS/Road Trip Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Decomposability** | Can the trip be split into "Calicut → Kochi", "Kochi → Alleppey", etc.? |
| **Solution Steps** | If you take a wrong turn, can you simply turn around (recoverable) or must you keep going forward (irrecoverable)? |
| **Predictability** | Is traffic on NH-66 predictable, or can an accident change everything? |
| **Absolute / Relative** | Is "reaching Trivandrum" an absolute goal, or is "reaching in <6 hours" a relative comparison? |
| **State vs Path** | Do you only care about *arriving*, or do you want the scenic route *itself*? |
| **Role of Knowledge** | Do you blindly follow GPS, or do you use local knowledge ("avoid the Kochi bridge at 5 PM")? |

This single mental model will help you intuitively remember all six classifications in the exam.

---

## 1.3 Why These Characteristics Matter in AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Choosing the wrong search algorithm for the wrong problem type leads to *combinatorial explosion* — a state where the search space grows so large that no computer can solve it in a feasible time. The six characteristics act as a **diagnostic checklist** that an AI engineer uses *before* writing a single line of code.

For instance:
- A **non-decomposable, knowledge-rich** problem requires a **rule-based expert system**, not a blind search.
- A **decomposable, predictable, state-based** problem is a perfect fit for **uninformed BFS/DFS**.
- A **path-based, relative** problem screams for a **heuristic search** like **A\***.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used for Classification

- **Search Space Size (N)** — Total number of possible states. Often expressed in **nodes**.
- **Branching Factor (b)** — Average number of successors per node.
- **Depth of Optimal Solution (d)** — Depth at which the best solution is found.
- **Heuristic Function h(n)** — Estimated cost from node n to goal. (Used for relative/heuristic problems.)
- **Path Cost g(n)** — Actual cost from start to node n.

> [!NOTE]
> These five metrics (**N, b, d, h(n), g(n)**) will appear repeatedly in the formulas in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Problem Characteristics — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Characteristic 1: Problem Decomposability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A problem is *decomposable* if it can be broken into a set of smaller, **independent** sub-problems, each of which can be solved separately, and whose solutions can be combined to solve the original problem.

**Two Sub-Classes:**

| Type | Behavior | Real-World AI Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposable** | Can be split cleanly into independent pieces. | **8-Puzzle** can be split into "position tile 1", "position tile 2"… (though not truly independent). A **medical diagnosis** decomposes into "check symptoms", "rule out diseases". |
| **Non-Decomposable (Indecomposable)** | Must be solved as a single atomic block. | **Chess**, **cryptarithmetic** (SEND + MORE = MONEY), and **theorem proving** — moving one piece affects all other pieces. |

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why it matters:** Decomposable problems allow *divide-and-conquer* strategies, drastically reducing the effective branching factor.
- **How to check:** Ask — *"Does solving sub-problem A first, in any way, affect the solution to sub-problem B?"* If **YES**, the problem is **non-decomposable**.

---

### 2.1.2 Characteristic 2: Solution Steps Consideration

This dimension asks: *"What happens if a solution step turns out to be wrong or unwise?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** It classifies whether the steps taken during problem solving can be retracted, ignored, or permanently committed.

**Three Sub-Classes (Luger's Classification):**

1. **Ignorable (Irrelevant) Steps:**
   - You can simply *forget* a bad step and try another.
   - **Example:** **Theorem proving** — a failed deduction is just discarded. **Pathing in 8-puzzle (BFS)** — explored paths are abandoned without consequence.

2. **Recoverable Steps:**
   - You *notice* the mistake and have to *undo* it, but undoing is possible.
   - **Example:** **8-Puzzle with backtracking** — if sliding a tile worsens the board, you can slide it back.
   - **Cost of recovery:** Extra computation to track and revert.

3. **Irrecoverable Steps:**
   - Once taken, the step is *permanent*. There is no undo.
   - **Example:** **Chess** — once you move your Queen, you cannot take it back (in real play). **Patient surgery planning** — a wrong incision is irreversible.
   - **Engineering cost:** Demands **planning** (looking ahead) rather than **trial-and-error**.

---

### 2.1.3 Characteristic 3: Problem Solution Predictability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A problem has *predictable* (or *certain*) solution steps if the **outcome of any given action is guaranteed to be known**. If outcomes involve chance or incomplete information, the problem is *unpredictable* (or *uncertain*).

**Two Sub-Classes:**

| Type | Description | AI Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Certain (Predictable)** | Every action has a **guaranteed** deterministic outcome. | **8-Puzzle**, **route-finding** on a fixed map, **Tic-Tac-Toe**. |
| **Uncertain (Unpredictable)** | Outcomes involve probability, external agents, or hidden information. | **Backgammon** (dice), **Poker** (hidden cards), **stock prediction**, **robotics** with faulty sensors. |

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why it matters:** Uncertain problems require **probabilistic reasoning** (Bayesian networks) or **stochastic search** (Monte Carlo), not deterministic BFS/DFS.
- **How to check:** Ask — *"If I apply action A in state S, is state S' guaranteed?"* If **NO**, the problem is uncertain.

---

### 2.1.4 Characteristic 4: Absolute or Relative Problem Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An *absolute* problem has a precisely defined goal state — the agent knows *exactly* when it has succeeded. A *relative* problem compares the quality of solutions against each other or against a benchmark — there is no single "right" answer.

**Two Sub-Classes:**

| Type | Goal Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute** | The goal is a specific, testable state. | "Reach city X", "Prove theorem T", "Win the 8-puzzle". |
| **Relative** | The goal is "as good as possible" within constraints. | **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — "shortest tour", **VLSI layout** — "minimize area + wire length", **tuning a neural network's hyperparameters** — "lowest validation error". |

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why it matters:** Absolute problems can be terminated the moment a goal is found (using *goal-test* functions). Relative problems require **full exploration** of the space to certify optimality (or use heuristics to settle for "good enough").

---

### 2.1.5 Characteristic 5: State or Path Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *state* solution problem only requires the final state to be specified; the path taken is irrelevant. A *path* solution problem requires the *entire sequence of steps* leading to the goal.

**Two Sub-Classes:**

| Type | What Matters | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **State Solution** | The **end configuration** is what counts. | **8-Puzzle (solved board)**, **water-jug problem (final water levels)**, **N-Queens (final arrangement)**. |
| **Path Solution** | The **sequence of moves** itself is the answer. | **TSP (which cities, in what order)**, **rubik's cube solution sequence**, **the proof of a theorem (sequence of inferences)**, **route directions for a driver**. |

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why it matters:** State-solution problems can use *goal-test functions* and discard intermediate paths. Path-solution problems must **retain and output the full action sequence**, which is a non-trivial memory and bookkeeping task.

---

### 2.1.6 Characteristic 6: Role of Knowledge in Problem Solving

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** This characteristic measures how heavily a problem relies on **vast amounts of domain-specific knowledge** (heuristics, rules, expert intuition) versus **pure algorithmic computation**.

**Two Sub-Classes:**

| Type | Knowledge Source | Example AI Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge-Poor** | The problem can be solved by brute-force search; little domain knowledge needed. | **BFS/DFS on 8-puzzle**, **A\*** on a roadmap, **minimax** on small game trees. |
| **Knowledge-Rich** | The problem requires large knowledge bases, expert rules, or learned models. | **Medical diagnosis (MYCIN)**, **legal reasoning**, **chess engines (knowledge-based evaluation)**, **natural language understanding**. |

**The "Why" and "How":**
- **Why it matters:** Knowledge-rich problems are where **Expert Systems, Rule-Based AI, and Knowledge Graphs** shine, not raw search.
- **Engineering trade-off:** Acquiring and representing knowledge (Knowledge Engineering) is expensive but reduces search dramatically. The **knowledge principle** states: *"If a solution can be described compactly by a knowledge-based system, it is preferable to encoding it in an algorithm."*

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all the quantitative and qualitative "formulas" and rules-of-thumb for problem-characteristic classification that are most frequently tested in KTU exams.

| # | Characteristic | Question to Ask | Test Value / Heuristic | AI Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Decomposability** | Can it be split into independent parts? | YES → Decomposable, NO → Non-decomposable | Use divide-and-conquer or monolithic planning |
| 2 | **Solution Steps** | Can a step be undone? | Yes (ignore) → Ignorable; Yes (backtrack) → Recoverable; No → Irrecoverable | Use simple / backtracking / planning search |
| 3 | **Predictability** | Is the outcome of action A known? | Always → Certain; Sometimes → Uncertain | Use deterministic / probabilistic methods |
| 4 | **Absolute / Relative** | Is there a single "right" answer? | Specific state → Absolute; "Best of many" → Relative | Use goal-test / optimization + heuristic |
| 5 | **State / Path** | Is the journey or the destination important? | Destination only → State; Sequence matters → Path | Use only goal-test / retain full path |
| 6 | **Role of Knowledge** | Does the agent need expert knowledge? | No → Knowledge-poor; Yes → Knowledge-rich | Use BFS/DFS/A* / Use Expert System, KB |

---

## 2.3 The Unifying Search-Space Equation

All these characteristics ultimately affect the **effective size of the search space** that an algorithm must traverse. The theoretical time and space complexity of a basic search is governed by:

$$
T_{\text{search}} \;\approx\; O\!\left(b^{d}\right)
$$

where:
- $b$ is the **branching factor** (depends on decomposability & knowledge).
- $d$ is the **depth of the optimal solution** (depends on path/state nature).

When the problem is **knowledge-rich**, the effective $b$ and $d$ are reduced by a heuristic function $h(n)$:

$$
T_{\text{heuristic search}} \;\approx\; O\!\left(b^{\,d - h(n)}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **central equation** for the entire AI search module. Master it. It will appear in nearly every KTU exam.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| AI Field | Role of Problem Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Must classify: *uncertain* (sensor noise), *irrecoverable* (braking), *path-solution* (turn sequence), *knowledge-rich* (traffic rules). |
| **Medical Diagnosis (MYCIN)** | *Decomposable* (by symptoms), *absolute* (disease confirmed), *knowledge-rich* (medical rules). |
| **Game AI (Chess)** | *Non-decomposable*, *irrecoverable*, *certain* (perfect info), *path-solution*, *knowledge-rich* (opening book, eval function). |
| **Logistics & Route Planning** | *Decomposable* (per route segment), *recoverable*, *certain*, *relative* (shortest path), *state or path* depending on user. |
| **NLP / Chatbots** | *Knowledge-rich*, *uncertain*, *path-solution* (sentence generation is a sequence). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The engineering takeaway:** Problem characteristics are not abstract theory — they are the *first step* in the **AI Solution Design Pipeline** that you will use in industry and in your Mini-Project evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivations: From Search Complexity to Characteristic-Driven Algorithm Choice

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the BFS Time Complexity under Various Decomposabilities

**Step 1 — Start with the generic breadth-first search on a tree.**
Every node at depth $k$ has $b$ children, so the total number of nodes expanded up to depth $d$ is:

$$
N_{\text{expanded}} \;=\; 1 + b + b^{2} + b^{3} + \cdots + b^{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series.**
Apply the geometric sum formula $\sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} = \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$ for $b > 1$:

$$
N_{\text{expanded}} \;=\; \frac{b^{\,d+1} - 1}{b - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the asymptotic (Big-O) form.**
For large $b^{d}$, the $-1$ in the numerator is dominated by $b^{d+1}$, and the denominator is a constant $b-1$:

$$
N_{\text{expanded}} \;\approx\; \frac{b^{\,d+1}}{b - 1} \;\in\; O\!\left(b^{\,d}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Decomposability Reduction.**
If the problem is **decomposable** into $k$ independent sub-problems each of depth $d/k$ (a clean split), then the total work becomes:

$$
N_{\text{decomposable}} \;\approx\; k \cdot b^{\,d/k}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare the two scenarios.**
- *Non-decomposable* (no split): $T_{\text{mono}} = b^{d}$.
- *Fully decomposable* (clean split): $T_{\text{split}} = k \cdot b^{d/k}$.

**Conversion logic explanation:** By splitting the problem, we replace the exponent $d$ with $d/k$. Since $b > 1$ and $d/k < d$, the new exponent is strictly smaller. This **drastically** reduces search time — a perfect demonstration of why the "Decomposability" characteristic is a critical design parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> Worked numeric example: $b = 10$, $d = 6$, $k = 3$.
> - Non-decomposable: $T_{\text{mono}} = 10^{6} = 1{,}000{,}000$.
> - Decomposable: $T_{\text{split}} = 3 \cdot 10^{2} = 300$.
> **Reduction factor: ~3,333×.** This is the *practical* impact of the Decomposability characteristic.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Effective Branching Factor under a Heuristic

**Step 1 — Define a perfect heuristic.**
An *admissible* heuristic $h(n)$ never overestimates the true remaining cost $h^{\ast}(n)$, so $0 \le h(n) \le h^{\ast}(n)$.

**Step 2 — Define the effective depth.**
With heuristic guidance, the search effectively "skips" $h(n)$ layers, so the new effective depth is $d' = d - h(n)$:

$$
d' \;=\; d - h(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the search equation.**

$$
N_{\text{heuristic}} \;\approx\; b^{\,d'} \;=\; b^{\,d - h(n)}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the original complexity.**

$$
N_{\text{heuristic}} \;=\; \frac{b^{\,d}}{b^{\,h(n)}}
$$

**Step 5 — State the reduction factor.**

$$
\text{Reduction factor} \;=\; b^{\,h(n)}
$$

**Conversion logic explanation:** A good heuristic $h(n)$ literally divides the search effort by a factor of $b^{h(n)}$. For $b=10$ and $h(n)=2$, the search becomes $100\times$ faster. This is the **quantitative justification** for the "Role of Knowledge" characteristic: knowledge $h(n)$ = power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **You will be expected to reproduce this derivation in 14-mark KTU questions.** Practice writing it out fully.

---

## 3.2 Worked Classification Table — Real AI Problems

The following analytical matrix applies all six characteristics to standard AI textbook problems. **Memorize this matrix** — it is a KTU-favorite.

| # | Problem | Decomposability | Steps | Predictability | Abs/Rel | State/Path | Knowledge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **8-Puzzle** | Non-decomposable | Recoverable (backtrack) | Certain | Absolute | State | Knowledge-poor |
| 2 | **8-Queens** | Decomposable (per row) | Recoverable | Certain | Absolute | State | Knowledge-poor |
| 3 | **Tic-Tac-Toe** | Non-decomposable | Recoverable | Certain | Absolute | Path | Knowledge-poor |
| 4 | **Chess** | Non-decomposable | Irrecoverable | Certain | Relative | Path | Knowledge-rich |
| 5 | **Medical Diagnosis** | Decomposable (per symptom) | Irrecoverable | Uncertain | Absolute | Path | Knowledge-rich |
| 6 | **TSP** | Non-decomposable | Recoverable | Certain | Relative | Path | Knowledge-poor |
| 7 | **Theorem Proving** | Non-decomposable | Ignorable | Certain | Absolute | Path | Knowledge-rich |
| 8 | **Backgammon** | Non-decomposable | Irrecoverable | Uncertain | Relative | Path | Knowledge-rich |
| 9 | **Water Jug** | Non-decomposable | Recoverable | Certain | Absolute | State | Knowledge-poor |
| 10 | **Sudoku** | Decomposable (per cell w/ constraints) | Recoverable | Certain | Absolute | State | Knowledge-poor |

### 3.2.1 Explanation of Each Row (for verbal exam answer)

**Row 1 — 8-Puzzle:**
- A tile move affects the *whole board*, hence **non-decomposable**.
- You can slide a tile back, hence **recoverable**.
- A move has a fixed effect, hence **certain**.
- Goal is the sorted board, hence **absolute, state-based**.
- No expert knowledge needed — just the heuristic $h(n)$ = sum of Manhattan distances.

**Row 4 — Chess:**
- Moving the queen affects everything → **non-decomposable**.
- In real play, you cannot retract a move → **irrecoverable**.
- All info is on the board → **certain** (but practically the tree is too deep to exhaust).
- We want the *best* move, not just any win → **relative**.
- The *sequence* of moves is what wins the game → **path-solution**.
- Modern chess engines like Stockfish use **massive knowledge bases** (opening books, endgame tables, hand-tuned evaluation).

**Row 5 — Medical Diagnosis (MYCIN):**
- Diseases are checked symptom-by-symptom, but interact → mostly **decomposable**.
- A wrong drug dose cannot be reversed → **irrecoverable**.
- Symptoms can be ambiguous → **uncertain**.
- Goal is a definitive diagnosis → **absolute**.
- The chain of reasoning is shown to the doctor → **path-solution**.
- Powered by ~600 hand-coded IF-THEN rules → **knowledge-rich**.

---

## 3.3 Algorithm Selection Flow — Decision Tree Logic

The following conditional logic shows how the six characteristics *jointly* drive the choice of AI algorithm:

1. **IF** *decomposable* **AND** *absolute* **AND** *state* → **Use simple goal-driven search** (e.g., BFS, DFS, IDDFS).
2. **IF** *non-decomposable* **AND** *absolute* **AND** *state* → **Use heuristic search** (A*, Greedy).
3. **IF** *non-decomposable* **AND** *recoverable* **AND** *path* → **Use backtracking** (e.g., 8-Queens, CSP solvers).
4. **IF** *non-decomposable* **AND** *irrecoverable* **AND** *certain* → **Use planning + minimax** (e.g., Chess, Checkers).
5. **IF** *uncertain* **→** **Use probabilistic reasoning** (Bayesian networks, HMM, Monte Carlo).
6. **IF** *knowledge-rich* **→** **Use expert systems or knowledge graphs** (MYCIN, DENDRAL).
7. **IF** *relative* **→** **Use optimization** (Hill climbing, Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic:** Each characteristic acts as a *filter* in this decision tree. The order of evaluation in industry practice is: **Decomposability → Steps → Predictability → Absolute/Relative → State/Path → Knowledge.**

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — The 8-Puzzle

Given: $3 \times 3$ 8-puzzle, branching factor $b = 3$ (avg.), depth of optimal solution $d = 22$ (worst case).

**Step 1 — Compute BFS time without heuristic:**

$$
T_{\text{BFS}} = b^{d} = 3^{22} \approx 3.1 \times 10^{10}
$$

This is ~31 billion nodes — infeasible.

**Step 2 — Compute A* time with Manhattan heuristic $h(n) \approx d/2$:**

$$
T_{\text{A*}} = b^{\,d - h} = 3^{\,22 - 11} = 3^{11} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{5}
$$

This is ~177,000 nodes — trivially solvable in under a second.

**Step 3 — Compute the speedup:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{b^{d}}{b^{\,d - h}} = b^{h} = 3^{11} \approx 177{,}147
$$

**Conversion logic explanation:** By exploiting the *Role of Knowledge* (the Manhattan distance heuristic), we achieve a $177{,}000\times$ speedup. This is the engineering payoff of correctly classifying the problem and choosing the right algorithm.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam trick:** KTU examiners love asking "Why is A* better than BFS for 8-puzzle?" — the answer is precisely this derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — All Six Characteristics

The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical classification of the six problem characteristics. It is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing how each characteristic acts as a decision node that channels the problem into an appropriate algorithm family.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AI Problem Input"]:::root --> B{"Is it Decomposable?"}
    B -- "YES" --> C["Divide-and-Conquer"]
    B -- "NO" --> D{"Can steps be Undone?"}
    C --> D
    D -- "Ignorable" --> E["Simple BFS / DFS"]
    D -- "Recoverable" --> F["Backtracking Search"]
    D -- "Irrecoverable" --> G["Planning + Lookahead"]
    E --> H{"Predictable Outcome?"}
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -- "Certain" --> I{"Solution Type?"}
    H -- "Uncertain" --> J["Probabilistic: Bayes / HMM / MC"]
    I -- "Absolute + State" --> K["Goal-Test Search"]
    I -- "Relative + Path" --> L["Optimization: Hill Climb / GA / SA"]
    K --> M{"Knowledge Level?"}
    L --> M
    J --> M
    M -- "Knowledge-Poor" --> N["Uninformed BFS / DFS / A*"]
    M -- "Knowledge-Rich" --> O["Expert System / Rule Engine / KB"]
    N --> P["Final Algorithm Chosen"]:::output
    O --> P

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Start at the top with the problem.
- Each diamond (rhombus) represents one **problem characteristic** decision.
- Each rectangle represents a **family of AI algorithms** that handle problems of that type.
- The final green node is the chosen algorithm.

---

## 4.2 The Problem-Solving Cycle with Knowledge Integration

This diagram illustrates the **human/AI problem-solving cycle**, highlighting where the *Role of Knowledge* fits in.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Define Problem"]:::step --> A2["Analyze Characteristics"]:::step
    A2 --> A3["Select Algorithm"]:::step
    A3 --> A4["Generate Solution"]:::step
    A4 --> A5["Test Solution"]:::step
    A5 -- "Fail" --> A6["Backtrack or Refine"]:::step
    A5 -- "Pass" --> A7["Output Solution"]:::final
    A6 --> A3

    K1["Domain Knowledge Base"]:::kb -.->|"informs"| A2
    K1 -.->|"guides heuristic"| A4
    K2["Heuristic h(n)"]:::kb -.->|"prunes search"| A4

    classDef step fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
    classDef final fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef kb fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid arrows are the **main problem-solving loop** (define → analyze → select → generate → test).
- The **dashed orange arrows** show how the *knowledge base* and *heuristic* inject information into the analyze and generate steps.
- The "backtrack or refine" loop is precisely what the **Solution Steps Consideration** characteristic dictates.

---

## 4.3 Characteristic × Algorithm Compatibility Matrix (Block Diagram)

This block diagram maps each characteristic to its preferred algorithm family in a clear, tabular layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CharSet1["PROBLEM DECOMPOSITION & STEPS"]
        C1["Decomposable"] --> Alg1["Divide-and-Conquer / CSP"]
        C2["Non-Decomposable"] --> Alg2["Atomic Planning"]
        C3["Ignorable Steps"] --> Alg3["Simple BFS / DFS"]
        C4["Recoverable Steps"] --> Alg4["Backtracking"]
        C5["Irrecoverable Steps"] --> Alg5["Lookahead / Minimax / Planning"]
    end
    subgraph CharSet2["PREDICTABILITY & SOLUTION TYPE"]
        C6["Certain"] --> Alg6["Deterministic Search"]
        C7["Uncertain"] --> Alg7["Bayesian Networks / HMM"]
        C8["Absolute + State"] --> Alg8["Goal-Driven Search"]
        C9["Relative + Path"] --> Alg9["Optimization: A* / GA / SA"]
    end
    subgraph CharSet3["KNOWLEDGE ROLE"]
        C10["Knowledge-Poor"] --> Alg10["Uninformed Search"]
        C11["Knowledge-Rich"] --> Alg11["Expert System / Rule Engine"]
    end

    Alg1 --> FinalPick["Final Algorithm Choice"]:::out
    Alg2 --> FinalPick
    Alg3 --> FinalPick
    Alg4 --> FinalPick
    Alg5 --> FinalPick
    Alg6 --> FinalPick
    Alg7 --> FinalPick
    Alg8 --> FinalPick
    Alg9 --> FinalPick
    Alg10 --> FinalPick
    Alg11 --> FinalPick

    classDef out fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#0b3d10,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The problem is **simultaneously** evaluated against all six characteristics (three sub-graphs).
- The intersection of the chosen algorithm families from each block gives the final algorithm.
- For example: A *non-decomposable, irrecoverable, certain, path-based, knowledge-rich* problem → **Chess (Minimax + Alpha-Beta + Opening Book)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **For the exam:** Even if you cannot draw the Mermaid, you can replicate this logic in a 3-column table in your answer booklet. Examiners award full marks for the *matrix logic*, not the *syntax*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*"Differentiate between a decomposable and a non-decomposable problem. Give one AI example of each."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**

A problem is **decomposable** if it can be divided into smaller, independent sub-problems whose individual solutions can be combined to produce the solution of the original problem. A problem is **non-decomposable** when the sub-problems interact, and solving them in isolation does not yield a global solution.

| Type | Example | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Decomposable** | **8-Queens** (one queen per row) | Placing a queen in row $i$ does not affect the *physical* position of a queen in row $j$. |
| **Non-decomposable** | **8-Puzzle** | A tile move changes the entire board state, so all tile placements are interdependent. |

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the example of decomposable, 1 mark for the example of non-decomposable.)*

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
*"What is the difference between an absolute solution and a relative solution in problem solving? Illustrate with one example each."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**

An **absolute** solution is one where the goal is a specific, identifiable state — the agent knows precisely when it has succeeded. A **relative** solution compares multiple candidate solutions and selects the "best" one according to some quality measure (cost, time, distance, etc.).

- **Absolute Example:** **8-Puzzle** — the goal is the *exact* sorted configuration of tiles.
- **Relative Example:** **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — there is no "right" tour; the goal is the *shortest* tour among all valid tours.

*(1 mark for the definition of absolute, 1 mark for definition of relative, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert:**
> - ❌ **Do not** write only definitions in a 14-mark question. You MUST apply each characteristic to a *concrete problem* (chess, 8-puzzle, MYCIN, TSP, etc.). Definitions alone fetch at most 4–5 marks.
> - ❌ **Do not** confuse *Path Solution* with *Recoverable Steps*. Path means the journey is the answer; Recoverable means you can undo a step.
> - ❌ **Do not** confuse *Predictability* with *Certainty of the Goal*. Certainty of *outcome* is about the next state, not the goal state.
> - ✅ **Do** support every classification with a *real AI problem example* and a *one-line justification*.
> - ✅ **Do** draw the *decision-tree matrix* (like Section 4.3 of these notes) for full marks.

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) — "Chess as a Case Study"

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** *List the six problem characteristics used to classify AI problems. Apply each one to the problem of playing **Chess** at a competitive level.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *For a medical diagnosis problem such as **MYCIN**, classify it along all six dimensions. Then, justify why a **rule-based expert system** is preferred over a **breadth-first search** for this problem.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — Chess

**(1) Decomposability: Non-decomposable.**
Every piece interacts with every other piece; moving the queen does not leave the rest of the board unchanged. Hence the problem cannot be split into independent sub-games.

**(2) Solution Steps Consideration: Irrecoverable.**
In a real game, a move once made cannot be retracted. The player must *look ahead* (planning) rather than rely on backtracking.

**(3) Predictability: Certain.**
Chess is a game of *perfect information* — there is no dice, no hidden cards. Given a state and a move, the resulting state is fully determined.

**(4) Absolute or Relative Solution: Relative.**
There is no single "correct" move; the goal is the *best* move according to an evaluation function.

**(5) State or Path Solution: Path.**
The *sequence* of moves (the game plan) is what leads to victory, not just the final board state.

**(6) Role of Knowledge: Knowledge-rich.**
Modern engines (Stockfish, AlphaZero) use millions of learned patterns, hand-crafted evaluation functions, and opening books — heavy reliance on domain knowledge.

*Examiner's note: 1 mark per characteristic + 0.5 mark for correct Chess mapping + 0.5 mark for one-line justification = 7 marks.*

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — MYCIN

| # | Characteristic | MYCIN's Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Decomposability** | **Mostly decomposable** | Diseases can be checked per symptom-group, though final diagnosis integrates all. |
| 2 | **Solution Steps** | **Irrecoverable** | A wrongly prescribed drug cannot be "un-prescribed" in the analysis. |
| 3 | **Predictability** | **Uncertain** | Patient symptoms are noisy, probabilistic, and often incomplete. |
| 4 | **Absolute / Relative** | **Absolute** | The goal is a specific disease identification (a definite answer). |
| 5 | **State / Path** | **Path** | MYCIN must *show its chain of reasoning* to the doctor for trust — the sequence of rules fired is part of the solution. |
| 6 | **Role of Knowledge** | **Knowledge-rich** | MYCIN's power comes from ~600 hand-coded IF-THEN rules mined from human experts. |

**Justification for Expert System over BFS:**

A breadth-first search on the space of possible diseases would explore ~10^{3} possibilities and would have **no way to incorporate** the probabilistic, rule-based reasoning that doctors actually use. BFS is *knowledge-poor* — it would treat every disease hypothesis as equally likely, ignoring the medical knowledge encoded in the rules.

A **rule-based expert system**, in contrast, directly encodes domain knowledge as IF-THEN rules (e.g., "IF organism is gram-positive AND morphology is coccus THEN therapy is penicillin"). This makes the search:
- **Heavily pruned** — only relevant rules fire.
- **Explainable** — the chain of rules is the answer.
- **Maintainable** — adding new medical knowledge means adding new rules, not rewriting the search.

Hence, for an *uncertain, knowledge-rich, path-solution* problem like medical diagnosis, the **Expert System** is the architecturally correct choice.

*Examiner's note: 4 marks for the table (with one-line justifications), 3 marks for the "why expert system over BFS" paragraph with the two key reasons (pruning + explainability).*

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) — "TSP & 8-Puzzle Comparative Analysis"

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** *Compare the **8-Puzzle problem** and the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** along the dimensions of **(i) Decomposability**, **(ii) Solution Steps**, **(iii) Predictability**, **(iv) Absolute vs Relative solution**, **(v) State vs Path solution**, and **(vi) Role of Knowledge**.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Explain how the "Role of Knowledge" characteristic influences the choice of a heuristic in the A* algorithm. Derive the relationship between search effort and the heuristic function $h(n)$.* **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 8-Puzzle vs TSP

| # | Characteristic | 8-Puzzle | TSP |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Decomposability** | Non-decomposable (tile moves interact) | Non-decomposable (every city affects the tour) |
| (ii) | **Solution Steps** | Recoverable (slide a tile back) | Recoverable (remove a city and reinsert) |
| (iii) | **Predictability** | Certain (deterministic moves) | Certain (fixed distance matrix) |
| (iv) | **Absolute / Relative** | Absolute (one specific goal board) | Relative (shortest of many valid tours) |
| (v) | **State / Path** | State (final board is the answer) | Path (the city sequence is the answer) |
| (vi) | **Role of Knowledge** | Knowledge-poor (use Manhattan heuristic) | Knowledge-poor to moderate (use nearest-neighbor heuristic) |

*Examiner's note: 7 marks — 1 mark per row, with 0.5 for the comparison wording.*

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — Role of Knowledge in A* and the $h(n)$ Derivation

**Role of Knowledge in A*:**
The A* algorithm combines the cost-so-far $g(n)$ with an *estimated* cost-to-go $h(n)$ provided by the user. The function $h(n)$ encodes **domain knowledge** about the problem. For example:
- In 8-puzzle, $h(n) = $ sum of Manhattan distances from each tile to its goal position.
- In TSP, $h(n) = $ cost of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) of the remaining cities.

A good $h(n)$ is **admissible** (never overestimates) and **consistent** (monotonic). The closer $h(n)$ is to the true remaining cost $h^{\ast}(n)$, the more it "prunes" the search.

**Derivation of Search Effort vs $h(n)$:**

**Step 1:** Total BFS nodes expanded up to depth $d$:

$$
N \;\approx\; b^{d}
$$

**Step 2:** With a heuristic $h(n)$, the effective depth reduces from $d$ to $d - h(n)$:

$$
d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; d - h(n)
$$

**Step 3:** New node count:

$$
N_{\text{A*}} \;\approx\; b^{\,d - h(n)} \;=\; \frac{b^{\,d}}{b^{\,h(n)}}
$$

**Step 4:** Reduction factor:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{N}{N_{\text{A*}}} \;=\; b^{\,h(n)}
$$

**Conversion logic explanation:** The reduction is **exponential** in the heuristic. A small improvement in $h(n)$ yields a huge speedup. For $b=10$, increasing $h(n)$ by just 1 unit makes the search $10\times$ faster.

**Worked example (8-Puzzle):**
- $b = 3$ (avg.), $d = 22$ (worst case), $h_{\text{Manhattan}}(n) \approx 11$.
- $N_{\text{BFS}} = 3^{22} \approx 3.1 \times 10^{10}$.
- $N_{\text{A*}} = 3^{11} \approx 177{,}147$.
- **Speedup** = $3^{11} \approx 177{,}147$ times.

This proves quantitatively that **knowledge ($h(n)$) is computational power** in AI.

*Examiner's note: 2 marks for the role-of-knowledge paragraph, 3 marks for the 4 derivation steps with proper alignment, 2 marks for the worked numeric example.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin this on your wall before the exam.**

- **Six problem characteristics** (KTU 2024 Module 2): **Decomposability, Solution Steps, Predictability, Absolute/Relative, State/Path, Role of Knowledge.**
- **Decomposability:** *Can the problem be split into independent sub-problems?* YES → decomposable. NO → non-decomposable.
- **Solution Steps:** Ignorable (forget), Recoverable (undo), Irrecoverable (permanent). The harder the step, the more *planning* (not search) is needed.
- **Predictability:** *Is the next state guaranteed?* YES → certain. NO → uncertain (use Bayes, HMM, MC).
- **Absolute vs Relative:** Absolute has one specific goal; relative compares qualities (use optimization).
- **State vs Path:** State = only the end matters. Path = the sequence itself is the answer.
- **Role of Knowledge:** Knowledge-poor → raw search. Knowledge-rich → expert system / rule engine.
- **Master equation:** $T_{\text{search}} = O(b^{d})$ — and the heuristic version $O(b^{d - h(n)})$ is the **single most important formula** of this module.
- **Reduction factor from knowledge:** $R = b^{h(n)}$ — exponential gain from a good heuristic.
- **8-Puzzle is the canonical example** for "non-decomposable, recoverable, certain, absolute, state, knowledge-poor" — *memorize this fingerprint.*
- **Chess is the canonical example** for "non-decomposable, irrecoverable, certain, relative, path, knowledge-rich" — *memorize this fingerprint.*
- **MYCIN is the canonical example** for "mostly decomposable, irrecoverable, uncertain, absolute, path, knowledge-rich" — *memorize this fingerprint.*
- **TSP is the canonical example** for "non-decomposable, recoverable, certain, relative, path, knowledge-poor to moderate" — *memorize this fingerprint.*
- **Decision-tree logic** in Section 4.1 is the *most exam-friendly* way to write answers — examiners love flowcharts and decision matrices.
- **Never confuse Predictability (outcome of action) with Absolute/Relative (type of goal).** They sound similar but are different.
- **Never confuse State Solution (only end matters) with Path Solution (sequence matters).** The 8-Puzzle is state; the TSP is path.
- **Decomposability ≠ Recoverability.** Decomposability is about *splitting the problem*; Recoverability is about *undoing a step within the problem*.
- **The "knowledge principle":** if a problem's solution can be compactly described as rules, prefer a knowledge-based system over algorithmic search.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Six dimensions, six decisions, one algorithm."* Before designing any AI solution, run these six filters. The intersection of the chosen options **is** your algorithm family.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interaction in Problem Solving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Interaction in Problem Solving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Human Interaction in Problem Solving** is the interdisciplinary study of how human cognitive processes, heuristics, intuition, and decision-making patterns collaborate with computational systems (typically AI agents) to formulate, represent, search, and resolve complex problems. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *AI with Python*, it encompasses the study of human-guided search strategies, interactive problem representation, and the design of human-in-the-loop (HITL) AI pipelines where human judgement augments algorithmic reasoning.

In formal AI literature, this domain draws from three foundational pillars:
1. **Cognitive Science** – How humans *actually* solve problems.
2. **Heuristic Search Theory** – How human intuition is formalised as admissible search heuristics.
3. **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** – How humans *interface* with AI problem solvers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Module 2 syllabus places this topic as a bridge between *symbolic AI* and *applied AI with Python*, where students must understand that pure algorithmic power is insufficient — human intuition, domain knowledge, and iterative interaction are essential components of robust problem solving.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS with a Co-Pilot" Metaphor

Imagine you are driving through an unfamiliar city. A GPS device (the AI) calculates the mathematically optimal route using Dijkstra's or A* algorithm. However, the GPS does not know that:
- The bridge ahead is closed for repair today.
- Your mother gets carsick on winding mountain roads.
- You need to stop at a pharmacy at 2 PM.

**You** (the human) take the AI's optimal suggestion and **interactively refine** it. This loop — *AI proposes, human guides, AI re-proposes* — is the essence of **Human Interaction in Problem Solving**.

| Layer | Role | Example from Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Human** | Provides context, constraints, intuition, domain knowledge | "Avoid the bridge" |
| **AI System** | Performs exhaustive search, optimisation, pattern recognition | GPS calculates 3 alternate routes |
| **Interaction Loop** | Communication, feedback, refinement | Driver selects the "scenic but safe" option |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Human-in-the-Loop (HITL):** An AI paradigm in which a human operator continuously provides input, validation, or correction to a computational problem-solving process, transforming it from a one-shot computation into an **iterative, conversational** workflow.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 AI Curriculum

The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasises **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The Course Outcomes (COs) tied to this module demand that students:

- **CO2:** Apply heuristic search techniques in AI problem solving.
- **CO3:** Analyse the role of human cognition and interaction in designing intelligent systems.
- **CO5:** Implement Python-based interactive AI systems using libraries like `ipywidgets`, `streamlit`, or `tkinter`.

This topic therefore is the *conceptual backbone* of Module 2 — without understanding human problem-solving patterns, students cannot design AI systems that genuinely augment human intelligence.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Human Problem Solving

George Pólya, in his seminal 1945 work *How to Solve It*, formalised problem solving into a four-phase cycle. For KTU exam purposes, this framework is treated as the canonical reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pólya's Four Phases (Must Memorise):**
> 1. **Understanding the Problem** — What is the unknown? What are the data? What is the condition?
> 2. **Devising a Plan** — Have you seen a similar problem? Can you restate it?
> 3. **Carrying Out the Plan** — Execute each step, verify correctness.
> 4. **Looking Back** — Review, generalise, and find alternative solutions.

This model is directly implemented in **interactive AI tutors**, **chain-of-thought prompting** in modern LLMs, and **agent-based reasoning systems** coded in Python.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Newell and Simon's Information Processing Theory

Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon (1972) proposed that human problem solving can be modelled as **symbolic information processing** within a **Physical Symbol System**. Their **Soar architecture** and earlier **General Problem Solver (GPS)** are the theoretical ancestors of every modern AI planner.

### 2.1.1 The Three Memory Stores

Human cognition, in Newell & Simon's model, operates across three memory stores:

1. **Sensory Memory** (≈ 200–500 ms retention) — Raw perceptual input.
2. **Short-Term Memory (STM) / Working Memory** — Active problem state.
3. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** — Stored rules, patterns, schemas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller's Law (1956):** The capacity of human working memory is approximately **7 ± 2 chunks** of information. This is a fundamental constraint in designing AI interfaces — too many simultaneous options overwhelm the user.

### 2.1.2 The Problem Space Hypothesis

Newell and Simon defined a **Problem Space** as the set of all states the solver can reach, plus the operators that transform one state into another. Formally:

$$ P = (S, s_0, S_g, O) $$

Where:
- $S$ = Set of all possible states
- $s_0$ = Initial state
- $S_g$ = Set of goal states
- $O$ = Set of operators (actions)

A human (or AI) solves the problem by traversing from $s_0$ to some $s_g \in S_g$ using operators from $O$.

## 2.2 Heuristic Search — The Bridge Between Human and Machine

A **heuristic** is a rule-of-thumb, an approximation, or a domain-specific insight that **reduces the search space** without guaranteeing optimality. It is the formalisation of human intuition.

### 2.2.1 Uninformed vs. Informed Search

| Property | Uninformed (Blind) Search | Informed (Heuristic) Search |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Domain knowledge | None | Uses heuristic function $h(n)$ |
| Examples | BFS, DFS, UCS | A*, Greedy Best-First, Hill Climbing |
| Optimality | Depends on algorithm | Depends on admissibility of $h(n)$ |
| Human analogy | Trial and error | "Common sense" or "experience" |
| Time complexity | Exponential in worst case | Reduced dramatically |

### 2.2.2 The A* Evaluation Function

The A* algorithm is the canonical example of human-guided search. Its evaluation function is:

$$ f(n) = g(n) + h(n) $$

Where:
- $f(n)$ = Estimated total cost of the cheapest solution through node $n$
- $g(n)$ = Actual cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = Heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility Condition:** A heuristic is **admissible** if $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost. For example, **straight-line distance** is an admissible heuristic for road navigation because it never overestimates the actual road distance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Units / Domain | KTU Exam Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f(n)$ | A* evaluation function | Cost (path-length) | 14-mark derivations |
| $g(n)$ | Path cost from root to $n$ | Cost units | Frequently tested |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal | Cost units | Admissibility proofs |
| $h^*(n)$ | True optimal cost from $n$ to goal | Cost units | Used in admissibility |
| $P = (S, s_0, S_g, O)$ | Problem space formalisation | Tuple | Module 2 definition |
| $7 \pm 2$ | Working memory capacity | Chunks | Miller's Law question |
| $b$ | Branching factor of search tree | Integer | Complexity analysis |
| $d$ | Depth of optimal solution | Integer | Complexity analysis |
| $m$ | Maximum depth of search tree | Integer | Complexity analysis |
| $O(b^d)$ | Time complexity of BFS/DFS | Asymptotic | Direct formula |
| $O(b^m)$ | Worst-case DFS complexity | Asymptotic | Direct formula |
| $\vert S \vert$ | Size of state space | Cardinality | Critical for KTU |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules, all absolute values are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Means-Ends Analysis (MEA)

**Means-Ends Analysis** is the problem-solving strategy used in the original General Problem Solver (GPS) by Newell and Simon. It is a direct formalisation of how humans tackle problems:

1. Compare the current state with the goal state.
2. Identify the **difference** between them.
3. Select an **operator** that reduces this difference.
4. If the operator cannot be directly applied, set it as a **sub-goal** and recurse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Means-Ends Analysis is a guaranteed short-answer or 7-mark sub-question. Memorise the four-step cycle and the relationship to the GPS architecture.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied in this topic power some of the most consequential engineering systems in the world:

- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Human driver intervenes when AI encounters an edge case (HITL).
- **Medical Diagnosis AI** — Radiologists validate AI-detected tumours before final report.
- **Industrial Robotics** — Cobots (collaborative robots) adjust their motion based on real-time human proximity sensors.
- **Code Generation AI** — Developers iteratively refine LLM-generated code (the modern embodiment of Pólya's cycle).
- **Search and Rescue Drones** — Operators guide AI planners through unmapped terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The most successful AI systems of the 2020s are *not* fully autonomous — they are **centaur systems** (human + AI collaboration), as proven in domains ranging from chess (centaur chess > either human or AI alone) to radiology to scientific discovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: A* Search with Human-Supplied Heuristic

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Consider a road network with 4 cities A, B, C, D. The start city is **A**, the goal is **D**. Actual road distances $g(n)$ are:
- $g(A) = 0$
- $g(B) = 4$ (via A→B)
- $g(C) = 3$ (via A→C)
- $g(D) = 8$ (via A→B→D)

A human expert supplies the following heuristic (straight-line distance to D in km):
- $h(A) = 7$, $h(B) = 3$, $h(C) = 4$, $h(D) = 0$

**Task:** Compute $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ for all nodes and determine the next node A* would expand.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $f(n)$ for every node:**

$$ f(A) = g(A) + h(A) = 0 + 7 = 7 $$

$$ f(B) = g(B) + h(B) = 4 + 3 = 7 $$

$$ f(C) = g(C) + h(C) = 3 + 4 = 7 $$

$$ f(D) = g(D) + h(D) = 8 + 0 = 8 $$

**Step 2 — Explanation of Step 1:** The A* algorithm maintains a priority queue (open list) ordered by $f(n)$. Lower $f(n)$ means "more promising" because the total estimated cost is lower. A* is essentially a *human-informed* Dijkstra — Dijkstra uses only $g(n)$, but A* adds the human-supplied intuition encoded in $h(n)$.

**Step 3 — Tie-breaking rule:** When multiple nodes share the minimum $f(n)$ value, A* typically breaks ties in favour of the node with the **lower $h(n)$** (closer to goal heuristically) or alphabetically. Here A, B, and C all have $f(n) = 7$.

**Step 4 — Admissibility check:** A heuristic is admissible if it never overestimates. We have $h(D) = 0$ (trivially admissible at goal), and $h(B) = 3 \leq$ true cost from B to D (which is 4). Hence $h$ is **admissible** — A* will return an optimal path.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The first node expanded by A* would be any of A, B, or C (tie at $f = 7$). A* then continues to expand until D is reached with $f(D) = 8$, guaranteeing the optimal path A→B→D or A→C→D of cost 8.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Verifying Miller's Law in Interface Design

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** An AI dashboard presents 15 simultaneous decision options to a radiologist. According to Miller's Law, predict the cognitive load and suggest a remediation strategy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State Miller's Law:**

$$ C_{WM} = 7 \pm 2 \text{ chunks} $$

Where $C_{WM}$ is the working memory capacity in chunks.

**Step 2 — Compare with the problem:** The interface offers $N = 15$ options, which exceeds the upper bound ($7 + 2 = 9$) by $15 - 9 = 6$ items.

**Step 3 — Cognitive load calculation:** A common approximation uses the **overload ratio**:

$$ R_{overload} = \frac{N}{7 + 2} = \frac{15}{9} \approx 1.67 $$

An overload ratio greater than 1 indicates **cognitive overload**, leading to increased error rates and slower decision-making.

**Step 4 — Remediation strategy:** The designer should apply **information chunking** — group the 15 options into $7 \pm 2$ categories. For example:
- *Patient Information* (4 sub-options)
- *Image Tools* (3 sub-options)
- *Diagnostic Suggestions* (4 sub-options)
- *Reporting* (3 sub-options)

This reduces perceived options from 15 to 4 categories — well within Miller's $7 \pm 2$ range.

**Step 5 — Engineering implication:** This is why modern AI UIs (e.g., the ChatGPT sidebar, the Tesla touchscreen) deliberately use **progressive disclosure** — only $7 \pm 2$ options are visible at any time.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Means-Ends Analysis Trace

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Apply Means-Ends Analysis (MEA) to the problem of transforming the string "ABC" into "CBA" using the operators **SWAP(i, j)** and **REVERSE(i, j)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the difference:** "ABC" vs "CBA". The first character differs (A vs C). Difference type: *character-misplaced*.

**Step 2 — Select operator to reduce difference:** The operator **REVERSE(0, 2)** would reverse the entire string from "ABC" to "CBA" in one move. Difference reduced to 0.

**Step 3 — Check applicability:** The REVERSE operator is directly applicable. Apply it.

**Step 4 — Check goal:** Result = "CBA" = goal state. **Solved in 1 operator application**.

**Step 5 — Alternative path using SWAP:** If REVERSE is not allowed, we use SWAP:
- SWAP(0, 2) → "CBA" (swap A and C) — solved in 1 move.
- Or SWAP(0, 1) → "BAC", then SWAP(1, 2) → "BCA", then SWAP(0, 1) → "CBA" — 3 moves.

This illustrates the MEA principle: **prefer the operator that maximally reduces the perceived difference**, mirroring how humans naturally pick the "biggest-impact" move first.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Human vs. AI Problem Solving

| Dimension | Human Solver | AI Solver | Hybrid (HITL) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed | Slow (seconds to hours) | Fast (milliseconds) | Fastest in practice |
| Memory | Limited (7±2 chunks) | Vast (TB-scale) | Effectively unlimited |
| Generalisation | Excellent across domains | Narrow without training | Domain-adaptive |
| Intuition | Strong (heuristic-rich) | Statistical approximation | Best of both |
| Error type | Biases, fatigue, emotion | Hallucination, brittleness | Reduced via checks |
| Cost | Expensive (human hours) | Cheap after deployment | Moderate |
| Optimality | Often sub-optimal | Optimal if well-defined | Human-validated optimal |
| Adaptability | Real-time contextual | Retraining required | Real-time + retrainable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The 2024 Scheme emphasises that *AI augments, not replaces, human problem solving*. This table is a common 7-mark sub-question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pólya's Four-Phase Problem-Solving Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 1: UNDERSTAND THE PROBLEM"] --> B["PHASE 2: DEVISE A PLAN"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3: CARRY OUT THE PLAN"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4: LOOK BACK AND REVIEW"]
    D --> A

    A1["Identify unknowns, data, conditions"] -.-> A
    B1["Recall similar problems, select heuristic"] -.-> B
    C1["Execute step-by-step, verify each step"] -.-> C
    D1["Generalise, find alternative solutions"] -.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the **forward cycle** of problem solving, while the dotted arrows (`-.->`) show the *sub-activities* within each phase. The cycle is **iterative** — the arrow from D back to A indicates that "looking back" often reveals gaps in understanding, sending the solver back to Phase 1.

---

## 4.2 Human-AI Interaction Loop (HITL Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["HUMAN OPERATOR"] -->|"States problem, sets constraints"| I1["INTERFACE LAYER GUI OR VOICE"]
    I1 -->|"Encoded query"| AI["AI ENGINE SEARCH OR REASONING"]
    AI -->|"Proposed solution"| I2["INTERFACE LAYER RESULTS DISPLAY"]
    I2 -->|"Feedback, correction, validation"| H
    AI -.->|"Confidence score, alternatives"| H
```

**Block-level interpretation:**
- **Human Operator** provides the *real-world context* and *validates* AI output.
- **Interface Layer** translates between human language and machine representation (this is where Python libraries like `streamlit`, `gradio`, or `tkinter` operate).
- **AI Engine** performs the *computational heavy-lifting* (search, inference, learning).
- **Confidence score** (dotted line) gives the human a calibrated *trust signal*.

---

## 4.3 Means-Ends Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: Current State"] --> CMP["Compare with Goal State"]
    CMP --> DIFF["Identify the Difference"]
    DIFF --> SEL["Select Operator to Reduce Difference"]
    SEL --> Q1{"Is the Operator Applicable?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| APPLY["Apply Operator and Update State"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| SUB["Set Operator as Sub-Goal and Recurse"]
    SUB --> SEL
    APPLY --> Q2{"Goal Reached?"}
    Q2 -->|"No"| CMP
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| END["TERMINATE: Solution Found"]
```

**Note on node identifiers:** All node IDs above use alphanumeric prefixes (`S0`, `CMP`, `DIFF`, `SEL`, `Q1`, `APPLY`, `SUB`, `Q2`, `END`) to comply with Mermaid compilation rules and avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone node names.

---

## 4.4 Problem Space Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["s0: Initial State"] --> S1["S1: After Operator O1"]
    S1 --> S2["S2: After Operator O2"]
    S1 --> S3["S3: After Operator O3"]
    S2 --> GOAL["sg: Goal State"]
    S3 --> GOAL
    S2 --> S4["S4: Dead End"]
    S4 -.->|"Backtrack"| S1
```

**Interpretation:** This is a generic **state-space search graph**. Solid arrows are legal operator applications, dotted arrows represent **backtracking** (a hallmark of human problem solving when a chosen path fails), and the *goal state* $s_g$ is the termination condition.

---

## 4.5 A* Search Node Expansion Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["Start Node f = 0 + h = 7"] --> N1["Node A f = 4 + 3 = 7"]
    N0 --> N2["Node B f = 3 + 4 = 7"]
    N1 --> N3["Node C f = 8 + 0 = 8 GOAL"]
    N2 --> N3
```

**Reading the diagram:** A* expands nodes in order of ascending $f(n)$. Both Node A and Node B have $f = 7$, so the algorithm explores them in parallel. Node C (the goal) has $f = 8$, so it is expanded last — guaranteeing the optimal path is found.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "heuristic" in the context of human-AI problem solving. Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**
