    visited.add(node)
    print(node, end=" ")
    for neighbour in graph.get(node, []):
        if neighbour not in visited:
            dfs_recursive(graph, neighbour, visited)
    return visited


# ============================================================
# Part 2: Iterative DFS using an explicit Stack
# ============================================================
def dfs_iterative(graph, start):
    """
    Performs iterative DFS traversal using an explicit stack.
    """
    visited = set()
    stack = [start]

    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        if node not in visited:
            visited.add(node)
            print(node, end=" ")
            # Push neighbours in reverse so leftmost is processed first
            for neighbour in reversed(graph.get(node, [])):
                if neighbour not in visited:
                    stack.append(neighbour)
    return visited


# ============================================================
# Part 3: Cycle Detection in a Directed Graph using DFS
# ============================================================
def has_cycle_directed(graph):
    """
    Detects whether a cycle exists in a DIRECTED graph
    using DFS coloring: WHITE (0) = unvisited, GREY (1) = in stack, BLACK (2) = done.
    """
    WHITE, GREY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2
    color = {node: WHITE for node in graph}

    def dfs_visit(u):
        color[u] = GREY
        for v in graph.get(u, []):
            if color[v] == GREY:       # Back-edge found -> cycle
                return True
            if color[v] == WHITE and dfs_visit(v):
                return True
        color[u] = BLACK
        return False

    for node in graph:
        if color[node] == WHITE:
            if dfs_visit(node):
                return True
    return False


# ============================================================
# Driver Code
# ============================================================
if __name__ == "__main__":
    sample_graph = {
        'A': ['B', 'C'],
        'B': ['D', 'E'],
        'C': ['F'],
        'D': [],
        'E': ['F'],
        'F': []
    }

    print("Recursive DFS from A:")
    dfs_recursive(sample_graph, 'A')
    print("\n\nIterative DFS from A:")
    dfs_iterative(sample_graph, 'A')

    # ----- Cycle detection demo -----
    cyclic_graph = {
        1: [2],
        2: [3],
        3: [1]      # Back-edge 3 -> 1 forms a cycle
    }
    acyclic_graph = {
        1: [2, 3],
        2: [4],
        3: [4],
        4: []
    }

    print("\n\nCyclic graph has cycle?  ", has_cycle_directed(cyclic_graph))
    print("Acyclic graph has cycle? ", has_cycle_directed(acyclic_graph))
```

**Sample Output:**

```
Recursive DFS from A:
A B D E F C 

Iterative DFS from A:
A C F B D E 

Cyclic graph has cycle?   True
Acyclic graph has cycle?  False
```

**Explanation of the Code:**

- **Recursive DFS** uses Python's call stack; simple but limited by Python's default recursion limit (≈1000).
- **Iterative DFS** uses an explicit `list` as a stack with `.pop()`.
- **Cycle Detection** uses the standard *3-color DFS* algorithm. A **back-edge** (encountering a GREY node) definitively proves a cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Data Structure Mix-up (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing "BFS uses a Stack" or "DFS uses a Queue" is a *fatal* conceptual error that examiners immediately flag. Memorize: **BFS = Queue, DFS = Stack.**
> 2. **Confusing Completeness with Optimality (–1 mark):** A state-space may be *complete* (guaranteed to find *a* solution) but not *optimal* (guaranteed to find the *best* solution). These are *independent* properties — discuss them separately.
> 3. **Forgetting the Visited Set (–2 marks in code):** In cyclic graphs, BFS/DFS without a `visited` set will loop forever or return incorrect path lengths. Always include and justify it.
> 4. **Wrong Big-O Expression (–1 mark):** Writing $O(b^d)$ for DFS space is wrong; the correct expression is $O(b \cdot m)$.
> 5. **Not Recursing Properly (–2 marks in code):** Recursive DFS must pass the `visited` set as a parameter (or use a closure/inner function) — many students accidentally reset it on every call.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Uninformed Search** = no heuristic, only uses problem definition. **BFS and DFS** are the two primary strategies.
- **BFS uses a Queue (FIFO); DFS uses a Stack (LIFO).** This single difference determines all behavioral properties.
- **BFS is Complete and Optimal** (under uniform step costs); **DFS is Neither** in general infinite spaces.
- **Time Complexity:** BFS $\to O(b^d)$, DFS $\to O(b^m)$. **Space:** BFS $\to O(b^d)$, DFS $\to O(b \cdot m)$.
- **Always maintain a `visited`/`closed` set** when implementing BFS/DFS on graphs (not just trees) to avoid infinite loops on cycles.
- **BFS** = shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order tree traversal, BFS-based component labelling, bipartite testing.
- **DFS** = topological sort, cycle detection, SCC algorithms, solving mazes, N-Queens, Sudoku.
- **Python implementation:** Use `collections.deque` for BFS (O(1) popleft) and either a `list` as a stack or recursion for DFS.
- **3-color DFS** (WHITE/GREY/BLACK) is the standard technique for cycle detection in *directed* graphs.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, **always show the trace table, the code, and the complexity derivation** — a complete answer covers all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Informed Search Algorithms: Hill Climbing, A* Search Algorithm, and AO* Search Algorithm Description and Implementation Using Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Informed Search Algorithms: Hill Climbing, A* Search Algorithm, and AO* Search Algorithm Description and Implementation Using Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Informed (Heuristic) Search?

In **Artificial Intelligence**, an *informed search algorithm* is one that leverages **problem-specific knowledge** beyond the definition of the problem itself. This extra knowledge, called a **heuristic function** $h(n)$, guides the search toward the goal far more efficiently than uninformed (blind) strategies like BFS or DFS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A heuristic is a function $h(n)$ that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal state. The word "heuristic" comes from the Greek *heuriskein* — "to find" or "to discover."

### Conceptual Analogy — Finding Your Way in a Strange City

Imagine you are in **Kochi** and want to reach the Cochin International Airport. You have two strategies:
1. **Uninformed Search (BFS/DFS):** You wander through every possible street, including dead ends and rural detours, until you randomly find the airport.
2. **Informed Search (A*):** You look at a map, see the airport is to the **northeast**, and at every junction pick the road whose direction is "most aligned" with the airport. This $h(n)$ is the **straight-line distance** to the goal.

The informed strategy is dramatically faster because it never wastes steps going *away* from the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEITT613 — Module 3):** The three pillar algorithms to master are:
> 1. **Hill Climbing** — Local, greedy, evaluation-function driven.
> 2. **A* Search** — Global, optimal, best-first.
> 3. **AO* Search** — Extension of A* to **AND-OR graphs** (used for problem decomposition).

---

## 1.2 Hill Climbing — The Mountain Climber Analogy

**Formal Definition:** *Hill Climbing* is an **iterative algorithm** that starts from an arbitrary solution and continuously makes small changes to it, **accepting only changes that improve the objective (evaluation) function**. It terminates when no neighbor has a better value — i.e., it has reached a **peak**.

### Real-World Analogy

Picture a **blindfolded mountaineer** on a foggy mountain. The mountaineer can feel the ground beneath their feet. At every step, they take a stride in the direction where the slope is **steepest upward**. They never step downward because that would mean losing altitude. They stop when every direction around them is flat or sloping down — they are standing on a peak.

The mountaineer has no map, no memory of the full mountain — only local information. This perfectly describes Hill Climbing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Evaluation Function $f(n)$** in Hill Climbing is simply a measure of *how good* a state is. For the 8-Queens problem, $f$ could be the **number of non-attacking queen pairs** (higher = better). For finding a maximum of $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$, $f(n) = -n^2 + 4n$.

### Variants of Hill Climbing

| Variant | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Hill Climbing** | Examines one neighbor at a time; moves if it's better. | Fast, but may miss better neighbors. |
| **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** | Examines **all** neighbors; moves to the **best** one. | Slower per step, but better quality. |
| **Stochastic Hill Climbing** | Picks a random better neighbor with some probability. | Escapes some local maxima. |

---

## 1.3 A* Search Algorithm — The Smart GPS Analogy

**Formal Definition:** *A\** is a **best-first, graph-based search algorithm** that finds the **least-cost path** from a start node to a goal node. It combines:
- $g(n)$ — the actual cost from start to $n$ (the past)
- $h(n)$ — the heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal (the future)
- $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — the **total estimated cost** through $n$

### Real-World Analogy — Google Maps

Google Maps works exactly like A*. When planning a route from **Trivandrum to Kasaragod**:
- $g(n)$ = the actual distance already traveled from the start.
- $h(n)$ = the **straight-line ("as the crow flies") distance** from your current city to Kasaragod.
- $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ = "how promising is this current city as a waypoint?"

A* will never explore Kozhikode→Coimbatore→Kochi if it can directly see via the map that Kozhikode→Kannur is closer to the goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** A* is **optimal** (finds the lowest-cost path) if the heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** — i.e., $h(n) \leq \text{true cost from } n \text{ to goal}$ for all $n$. A common admissible heuristic is the **straight-line distance**.

---

## 1.4 AO* Search Algorithm — The Problem Decomposer

**Formal Definition:** *AO\** is the extension of A* to **AND-OR graphs**. It is used when a problem can be naturally broken into sub-problems that must *all* be solved (AND nodes) or *one of which* must be solved (OR nodes).

### Real-World Analogy — Planning a Wedding

To host a wedding, you must **(AND)**:
1. Book a venue
2. Order catering
3. Hire a photographer

For "Book a venue", you could **(OR)** pick: hall / beach / hotel / garden. AO* chooses the cheapest combination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from A\*:** A\* works on plain graphs where every node is an *OR* (one successor leads to a solution). AO\* handles **AND-nodes**, where *all* successors must be solved. The cost at an AND-node is the **sum** of children's costs plus the node's own cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often confuse A* with AO*. Remember — **A* is for OR-graphs, AO* is for AND-OR graphs.** The cost function at an AND node is a **sum**, not a min.

---

## 1.5 Module 3 Snapshot

| Algorithm | Type | Optimal? | Uses Heuristic? | Graph Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hill Climbing | Local search | ❌ (can get stuck) | ✅ (as evaluation fn) | State-space |
| A* | Global best-first | ✅ (if $h$ admissible) | ✅ $h(n)$ | OR-graph |
| AO* | Global best-first | ✅ (in AND-OR) | ✅ $h(n)$ | AND-OR graph |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hill Climbing — Algorithmic Mechanics

### Core Logic (Steepest-Ascent Variant)

The algorithm operates as a loop that maintains a **current state** and explores its **neighbors** (states reachable by one local move). At each iteration, it moves to the neighbor that **maximizes** the evaluation function $f$.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Initialize: pick a random starting state $s_0$. Evaluate $f(s_0)$.
2. **Generate neighbors:** Compute the set $N(s) = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\}$.
3. **Evaluate all neighbors:** Compute $f(s_i)$ for every $s_i \in N(s)$.
4. **Compare:** Find $s^* = \arg\max_{s_i \in N(s)} f(s_i)$.
5. **Move decision:**
   - If $f(s^*) > f(s)$ → move to $s^*$ and **GOTO step 2**.
   - Else → terminate and return $s$ as the local peak.
6. Stop when $f(s^*) \leq f(s)$ or maximum iterations reached.

### Why "Hill" Climbing?

In **maximization problems**, the algorithm climbs *up* the evaluation function landscape. In **minimization problems** (e.g., Travelling Salesman, path cost), the function is inverted, and the algorithm is called **gradient descent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Classic Failure Modes (KTU Repeatedly Tested):**
> 1. **Local Maxima** — A peak that is higher than all neighbors but lower than the true global maximum. Algorithm gets stuck.
> 2. **Plateau** — A flat region where neighbors have the same $f$ value. Algorithm cannot decide direction → random walk.
> 3. **Ridge** — A narrow elevation that slopes up, but the algorithm can only step in cardinal directions. It gets stuck on a slope that looks like a peak.

### Example — 8-Queens Problem

**State:** A board with 8 queens, one per column.
**Evaluation Function:** $f$ = number of attacking queen pairs (we want to **minimize**, so use $-f$ for climbing).
**Goal:** $f = 0$.

The hill climber moves one queen at a time within its column to reduce the number of attacks. It terminates when no move improves.

### The "Why" — Intuition Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Initialize):** Any feasible starting point works. Random restart later helps escape local maxima.
- **Step 2 (Neighbors):** Defines the *moves* available — small local perturbations.
- **Step 3 (Evaluate):** The heuristic $f$ encodes problem-specific goodness.
- **Step 4 (Compare):** Steepest-ascent picks the *best* neighbor — greedy.
- **Step 5 (Move/Stop):** The greedy acceptance is why the algorithm is **fast but not optimal**.

---

## 2.2 A* Search — Algorithmic Mechanics

### The Master Cost Function

A* ranks every node $n$ using:

$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost of the path from the start node $s$ to $n$.
- $h(n)$ = estimated cost of the cheapest path from $n$ to a goal node.
- $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the cheapest solution **through** $n$.

### Two Lists Maintained by A*

| List | Purpose | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **OPEN list** | Frontier — nodes discovered but not yet expanded. | Priority queue ordered by $f(n)$. |
| **CLOSED list** | Already expanded — nodes whose successors have been generated. | Hash set / boolean array. |

### Algorithmic Steps (Pseudocode Logic)

1. Place the start node $s$ in OPEN with $f(s) = g(s) + h(s) = 0 + h(s)$.
2. If OPEN is empty → **failure** (no path exists).
3. Pop node $n$ with the **lowest $f$** from OPEN.
4. If $n$ is a goal → **success**, return path.
5. Move $n$ to CLOSED.
6. For each successor $n'$ of $n$:
   - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
   - Compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
   - If $n'$ not in OPEN/CLOSED → add to OPEN.
   - If $n'$ already in OPEN with higher $f$ → replace.
   - If $n'$ already in CLOSED with higher $f$ → move back to OPEN.
7. **GOTO step 2.**

### Admissible vs. Consistent Heuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissible Heuristic:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost to goal. Never **overestimates**. Examples: straight-line distance, Manhattan distance on grid.
>
> **Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ — triangle inequality. Every consistent heuristic is admissible, but not vice versa.
>
> **KTU Theorem:** If $h$ is consistent, A* never re-expands a node — once a node is CLOSED, its $f$ is final.

### Why A* is "Best-First"

A* is called **best-first** because at every step it expands the most *promising* node — the one with the lowest $f(n)$. Promising means: "lowest estimated cost to a goal if I go through this node."

---

## 2.3 AO* Search — Algorithmic Mechanics

### The AND-OR Graph Concept

In a standard graph, expanding a node $n$ gives a set of successors $\{n_1, n_2, \ldots\}$. Solving the problem means finding *one* successor path to a goal. This is an **OR-node**.

In an AND-OR graph, a node may be an **AND-node**: to solve $n$, we must solve **all** its successors. The cost is the **sum**.

### Cost Computation Rules

For an OR-node $n$ with successors $\{n_i\}$:
$$
h(n) = \min_{i} \left[ \text{cost}(n, n_i) + h(n_i) \right]
$$

For an AND-node $n$ with successors $\{n_i\}$:
$$
h(n) = \text{cost}(n) + \sum_{i} h(n_i)
$$

### AO* Algorithm Steps

1. Initialize the start node $s$ and compute $h(s)$.
2. Expand a node on the **best path** (lowest $f$) marked as **unexpanded**.
3. Generate successors; for each successor, compute $h$ if unknown (use heuristic estimate).
4. **Propagate cost updates** upward:
   - If successor is AND → sum costs.
   - If successor is OR → take minimum.
5. Mark the best successor and re-mark ancestors.
6. If the start node is marked as **SOLVED** → return the solution graph.
7. Else GOTO 2.

### Key Differences Summary

| Feature | A* | AO* |
|---|---|---|
| Graph type | OR-graph | AND-OR graph |
| Cost at node | $g(n) + h(n)$ | Depends on AND/OR type |
| Successor semantics | One is enough | AND: all required |
| Backtracking | Not needed | Yes, can backtrack through graph |
| Solution form | Path | Solution graph (subgraph) |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Total estimated cost through $n$ | A*, AO* |
| 2 | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Admissibility condition | A* optimality proof |
| 3 | $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Consistency (triangle inequality) | A* monotonicity |
| 4 | $h_{\text{AND}}(n) = \text{cost}(n) + \sum_i h(n_i)$ | Cost at AND-node | AO* |
| 5 | $h_{\text{OR}}(n) = \min_i [\text{cost}(n, n_i) + h(n_i)]$ | Cost at OR-node | A*, AO* |
| 6 | $f_{\text{goal}} = g(\text{goal})$ | At goal, $h = 0$ | A* termination |
| 7 | $f(n) \leq f(\text{any goal})$ | A* never expands a non-optimal goal | Optimality proof |
| 8 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum (h_i - h_i^*)^2}$ | Heuristic quality metric | Heuristic evaluation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **A\*** powers GPS navigation, robotics path planning (ROS Nav Stack), game AI pathfinding, network packet routing.
> - **Hill Climbing** is used in neural network weight optimization, VLSI design, scheduling, the N-Queens solver.
> - **AO\*** is used in automated planning systems, theorem provers, expert systems, robotics task planning where a high-level task decomposes into mandatory sub-tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Hill Climbing for $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$

We want to **maximize** $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$ over integer $x$ in the range $[0, 5]$. Start at $x_0 = 0$ with step size 1.

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
- Current state $x = 0$, $f(0) = -(0)^2 + 4(0) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Generate neighbors of 0:** $\{1, -1\}$. We clip to domain: $\{1\}$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate neighbor $x=1$:**
$$
f(1) = -(1)^2 + 4(1) = -1 + 4 = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:** $f(1) = 3 > f(0) = 0$. **Move to 1.**

**Step 5 — Current $x = 1$, $f = 3$.** Neighbors: $\{0, 2\}$.

**Step 6 — Evaluate $x = 0$ and $x = 2$:**
$$
f(0) = 0, \quad f(2) = -(2)^2 + 4(2) = -4 + 8 = 4
$$

**Step 7 — Move to $x = 2$** (highest). $f = 4$.

**Step 8 — Current $x = 2$, $f = 4$.** Neighbors: $\{1, 3\}$.
$$
f(1) = 3, \quad f(3) = -(3)^2 + 4(3) = -9 + 12 = 3
$$

**Step 9 — No neighbor is better than 4.** **STOP.** Solution: $x^* = 2$, $f^* = 4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **True Global Max:** By calculus, $f'(x) = -2x + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2$, $f(2) = 4$. Hill Climbing found the global optimum. But this is not always the case — local maxima can trap it.

### Analytical Derivation — Why Local Max = Global Max Here

The function $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$ is a **concave parabola** — it has exactly one peak. Hill Climbing is guaranteed to find it. For non-convex functions (e.g., $f(x) = \sin(5x)\cdot x$ on a wide domain), local maxima abound.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — A* Search on a Graph

### Problem Graph

Nodes: $S, A, B, C, D, G$. Heuristic $h$ estimates distance to goal $G$.

| Node | $h(n)$ (straight-line) |
|---|---|
| S | 7 |
| A | 5 |
| B | 7 |
| C | 4 |
| D | 1 |
| G | 0 |

Edges (costs): $S\!-\!A=1$, $S\!-\!B=4$, $A\!-\!B=3$, $A\!-\!C=3$, $B\!-\!C=1$, $B\!-\!D=3$, $C\!-\!D=1$, $C\!-\!G=5$, $D\!-\!G=2$.

**Goal:** Find shortest path from $S$ to $G$.

### Iteration-by-Iteration Trace

**Initial:** OPEN = $\{S\}$ with $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + 7 = 7$.

**Iteration 1:** Pop $S$ (lowest $f = 7$). Expand.
- $A$: $g = 1$, $f = 1 + 5 = 6$
- $B$: $g = 4$, $f = 4 + 7 = 11$
- OPEN = $\{A:6, B:11\}$, CLOSED = $\{S\}$.

**Iteration 2:** Pop $A$ (lowest $f = 6$). Expand.
- $B$ (via $A$): $g = 1 + 3 = 4$, $f = 4 + 7 = 11$ — same as existing, skip (or replace, but no improvement).
- $C$: $g = 1 + 3 = 4$, $f = 4 + 4 = 8$
- OPEN = $\{C:8, B:11\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A\}$.

**Iteration 3:** Pop $C$ (lowest $f = 8$). Expand.
- $B$ (via $C$): $g = 4 + 1 = 5$, $f = 5 + 7 = 12$ — worse, skip.
- $D$ (via $C$): $g = 4 + 1 = 5$, $f = 5 + 1 = 6$
- $G$ (via $C$): $g = 4 + 5 = 9$, $f = 9 + 0 = 9$
- OPEN = $\{D:6, G:9, B:11\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A, C\}$.

**Iteration 4:** Pop $D$ (lowest $f = 6$). Expand.
- $G$ (via $D$): $g = 5 + 2 = 7$, $f = 7 + 0 = 7$ — **better** than existing $G:9$. **Update!**
- OPEN = $\{G:7, B:11\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A, C, D\}$.

**Iteration 5:** Pop $G$ (lowest $f = 7$). **GOAL reached!**

**Path reconstruction:** $G \to D \to C \to A \to S$ → Path = $S \to A \to C \to D \to G$ with total cost $1 + 3 + 1 + 2 = \mathbf{7}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimality Check:** The optimal path $S\!-\!A\!-\!C\!-\!D\!-\!G$ with cost **7** was found. This is the minimum. A* did **not** expand $B$ at all (or expanded it only later with a worse cost) — the heuristic saved exploration.

### Why Did A* Succeed?

- The heuristic $h$ is **admissible** (straight-line distance never overestimates).
- The heuristic is **consistent** (triangle inequality holds for Euclidean distance).
- A* always expands the node with the lowest $f$, so it always commits to the most promising path.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — AO* Search on a Simple AND-OR Graph

### Problem Setup

We want to find a solution from start node $A$. The graph has AND and OR nodes:

- $A$ is an **OR-node** with children $B$ and $C$.
- $B$ is an **AND-node** with children $D$ and $E$.
- $C$ is an **OR-node** with child $F$.
- $D, E, F$ are goal (terminal) nodes with $h(D)=2, h(E)=3, h(F)=1$.
- Edge costs: $A\!-\!B=1$, $A\!-\!C=2$, $B\!-\!D=2$, $B\!-\!E=3$, $C\!-\!F=1$.

### Cost Calculation

**Bottom-up evaluation:**

At $B$ (AND):
$$
h(B) = \text{cost}(B) + h(D) + h(E) = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6
$$

At $C$ (OR):
$$
h(C) = \text{cost}(C, F) + h(F) = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

At $A$ (OR):
$$
h(A) = \min\left[ \text{cost}(A,B) + h(B),\ \text{cost}(A,C) + h(C) \right]
$$

$$
= \min[1 + 6,\ 2 + 2] = \min[7, 4] = 4
$$

**Selected path at $A$:** go to $C$ (cheaper branch). At $C$, go to $F$. **Solution:** $A \to C \to F$ with cost **4**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why was the $A \to B$ branch rejected?** Because $B$ is an AND-node — you must pay for **both** $D$ and $E$. The cost 7 outweighs the simpler $A \to C \to F$ at cost 4. This is the **power of AND-OR reasoning** — it explicitly accounts for mandatory sub-tasks.

### Derivation of AO* Cost Formulas

For an **OR-node** $n$ with successors $\{n_1, \ldots, n_k\}$:
$$
h(n) = \min_{i=1}^{k} \left[ c(n, n_i) + h(n_i) \right]
$$

For an **AND-node** $n$ with successors $\{n_1, \ldots, n_k\}$:
$$
h(n) = c(n) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} h(n_i)
$$

These two rules, applied recursively from the leaves upward, give the cost of the cheapest solution subgraph rooted at any node.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Hill Climbing | A* | AO* |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search space | Local (neighbors) | Global (frontier) | Global (frontier) |
| Optimality | ❌ | ✅ (if $h$ admissible) | ✅ (in AND-OR sense) |
| Completeness | ❌ (can loop) | ✅ (in finite graphs) | ✅ (in finite graphs) |
| Time complexity | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Exponential worst case | Exponential worst case |
| Space complexity | $O(b)$ | $O(b^d)$ (all frontier) | $O(b^d)$ |
| Uses heuristic as | Evaluation $f$ | Estimated future cost $h$ | Estimated future cost $h$ |
| Handles AND-graphs | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Best for | Quick local optima | Single-agent shortest path | Multi-step mandatory sub-tasks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind AO\*'s Existence:** Standard A* assumes *any* single child path leads to a solution. But many real problems (e.g., robot assembly, circuit design, kitchen recipe) require **completing all** sub-steps. AO* extends A* to natively handle this via AND-nodes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hill Climbing State-Space Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start State s0]) --> evalA[Evaluate f s0]
    evalA --> genA[Generate Neighbors N s0]
    genA --> evalN[Evaluate all s in N s0]
    evalN --> compA{s star = argmax f s}
    compA -->|f s star > f s0| moveA[Move to s star]
    compA -->|f s star <= f s0| peakA([LOCAL PEAK Return s0])
    moveA --> evalA
    peakA -.Restart.-> startA
```

**Caption:** Steepest-ascent hill climbing loop. The dashed `Restart` arrow represents the **random restart** strategy used to escape local maxima.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — A* Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start S]) --> openB[OPEN: S with f h S]
    openB --> checkB{OPEN empty?}
    checkB -->|Yes| failB([FAILURE No path])
    checkB -->|No| popB[Pop node n with min f n]
    popB --> goalB{n is GOAL?}
    goalB -->|Yes| successB([SUCCESS Reconstruct path])
    goalB -->|No| moveB[Move n to CLOSED]
    moveB --> genB[Generate successors of n]
    genB --> compB[Compute g prime and f prime]
    compB --> openB
```

**Caption:** The A* main control loop. The OPEN list is a **priority queue** keyed by $f(n)$. The CLOSED list prevents re-expansion (assuming consistent $h$).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — A* Trace on the Sample Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((S f 7)) -->|1| A((A f 6))
    S -->|4| B((B f 11))
    A -->|3| C((C f 8))
    A -.->|3| B
    B -->|1| C
    B -->|3| D((D f 6))
    C -->|1| D
    C -->|5| G((G f 7 via D))
    D -->|2| G
    style S fill:#FFD700
    style G fill:#90EE90
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Caption:** Trace from Section 3.2. $B$ is shaded pink because A* never expanded it for a useful path — the heuristic told A* it was inefficient. Green $G$ marks the goal; yellow $S$ is the start.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — AND-OR Graph for AO* Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A((A OR cost 4)) -->|1| B([B AND cost 6])
    A -->|2| C((C OR cost 2))
    B -->|2| D((D GOAL h 2))
    B -->|3| E((E GOAL h 3))
    C -->|1| F((F GOAL h 1))
    style A fill:#87CEEB
    style B fill:#FFB6C1
    style C fill:#87CEEB
    style D fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#90EE90
    style F fill:#90EE90
```

**Caption:** The AND-OR graph from Section 3.3. $B$ is an AND-node (pink), $A$ and $C$ are OR-nodes (blue), and $D, E, F$ are goal nodes (green). The chosen solution subgraph is $A \to C \to F$ with cost 4, avoiding the expensive $A \to B \to \{D,E\}$ branch.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Algorithm Comparison Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HC[Hill Climbing]
        hc1[Start s0] --> hc2[Evaluate f]
        hc2 --> hc3{Better neighbor?}
        hc3 -->|Yes| hc1
        hc3 -->|No| hc4[Return peak]
    end
    subgraph AS[A Star]
        as1[OPEN with S] --> as2[Pop min f n]
        as2 --> as3{n is goal?}
        as3 -->|No| as4[Expand n update OPEN CLOSED]
        as4 --> as2
        as3 -->|Yes| as5[Return path]
    end
    subgraph AO[AO Star]
        ao1[Initialize h for all] --> ao2[Expand best unexpanded node]
        ao2 --> ao3[Propagate AND OR costs]
        ao3 --> ao4{Start SOLVED?}
        ao4 -->|No| ao2
        ao4 -->|Yes| ao5[Return solution graph]
    end
```

**Caption:** Functional block architecture comparing all three algorithms. Notice how Hill Climbing has the simplest loop (no OPEN/CLOSED lists), A* maintains a frontier, and AO* additionally propagates AND/OR cost rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define a **heuristic function**. What properties must it satisfy for A* to be optimal?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A heuristic function $h(n)$ is a function that **estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal state**. It encodes problem-specific knowledge to guide the search.
> For A* to be **optimal**, $h(n)$ must be:
> 1. **Admissible** — $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost. It must never overestimate.
> 2. (Sufficient) **Consistent** — $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$, ensuring A* does not re-expand nodes.
> Examples: straight-line distance, Manhattan distance on a grid.

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **Simple Hill Climbing** and **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** with one example each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing |
> |---|---|---|
> | Neighbor examination | One at a time | All neighbors |
> | Move selection | First better neighbor found | Best (steepest) neighbor |
> | Speed | Faster per step | Slower per step |
> | Quality | May settle on poor local peak | Higher quality local peak |
> | Example | 8-Queens moving first improvement queen | 8-Queens moving the queen that reduces attacks the most |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — A* Search Deep Dive

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`**
> **(a)** Explain the A* search algorithm with the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. State the conditions for the heuristic to be admissible and consistent. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Apply A* to the graph below and find the shortest path from $S$ to $G$. Show all iterations of OPEN and CLOSED lists. **(7 marks)**

**Graph Data:**

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| S → A | 3 |
| S → B | 5 |
| A → C | 2 |
| A → D | 4 |
| B → D | 1 |
| C → G | 6 |
| D → G | 2 |

| Node | $h(n)$ |
|---|---|
| S | 8 |
| A | 6 |
| B | 4 |
| C | 5 |
| D | 2 |
| G | 0 |

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**A\* Algorithm Definition (2 marks):**
A* is a best-first, graph-based search algorithm. It uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ where $g(n)$ is the path cost from start to $n$ and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal. A* maintains an OPEN list (priority queue ordered by $f$) and a CLOSED list of expanded nodes.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Initialize OPEN with start node $S$, set $f(S) = h(S)$.
2. While OPEN is not empty:
   - Pop node $n$ with the smallest $f(n)$.
   - If $n$ is goal → return path.
   - Move $n$ to CLOSED.
   - For each successor $n'$ of $n$:
     - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
     - Compute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$.
     - Add to OPEN if not present; update if a better path is found.
3. Failure if OPEN becomes empty.

**Admissibility and Consistency (2 marks):**
- **Admissible:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$. $h$ never overestimates the true cost.
- **Consistent:** $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every edge $(n, n')$. Implies admissibility. Ensures A* never re-expands a node.
- If $h$ is admissible, A* is **optimal**. If $h$ is consistent, A* is also **efficient** (no re-expansions).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Initial:** OPEN = $\{S: 0+8 = 8\}$, CLOSED = $\{\}$.

**Iteration 1:** Pop $S$ (f=8). Expand.
- $A$: $g=3$, $f=3+6=9$
- $B$: $g=5$, $f=5+4=9$

OPEN = $\{A:9, B:9\}$, CLOSED = $\{S\}$.

**Iteration 2:** Pop $A$ (f=9, ties broken arbitrarily; take $A$ first). Expand.
- $C$: $g=3+2=5$, $f=5+5=10$
- $D$: $g=3+4=7$, $f=7+2=9$

OPEN = $\{B:9, D:9, C:10\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A\}$.

**Iteration 3:** Pop $B$ (f=9). Expand.
- $D$ (via $B$): $g=5+1=6$, $f=6+2=8$ → **better** than $D:9$! **Update.**

OPEN = $\{D:8, C:10\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A, B\}$.

**Iteration 4:** Pop $D$ (f=8). Expand.
- $G$ (via $D$): $g=6+2=8$, $f=8+0=8$.

OPEN = $\{C:10, G:8\}$, CLOSED = $\{S, A, B, D\}$.

**Iteration 5:** Pop $G$ (f=8). **GOAL!**

**Path reconstruction:** $G \leftarrow D \leftarrow B \leftarrow S$ → **Path = $S \to B \to D \to G$**, total cost $= 5 + 1 + 2 = \mathbf{8}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Mark loss 1:** Students forget to **update** an existing node in OPEN when a better path is found. In iteration 3, $D$ must be updated from $f=9$ to $f=8$.
> - **Mark loss 2:** Students forget to include the **parent pointer** for path reconstruction, leaving the answer as just "cost = 8" without the path.
> - **Mark loss 3:** Students compute $g$ incorrectly — $g(n')$ is $g(n) + c(n, n')$, not just $c(n, n')$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Hill Climbing & AO* Combined

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`**
> **(a)** Explain the **Hill Climbing** algorithm. What are its limitations? How can they be overcome? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **AO\* algorithm** with a suitable example. Compare A* and AO*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Hill Climbing Definition (1 mark):**
Hill Climbing is a local search algorithm that iteratively moves from the current state to the best neighboring state as long as the evaluation function $f$ improves. It terminates when no neighbor has a higher $f$ — a local peak.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Start from an initial state $s_0$.
2. Generate neighbors $N(s) = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots\}$.
3. Evaluate $f$ for all neighbors.
4. Move to $s^* = \arg\max f(s_i)$ if $f(s^*) > f(s)$.
5. Else **terminate** and return $s$ as the local maximum.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Local Maxima** — the algorithm stops at a non-global peak.
2. **Plateaus** — flat regions cause random wandering.
3. **Ridges** — the algorithm gets stuck on slopes that look like peaks.

**Overcoming Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Random Restart:** Run hill climbing from many random initial states; keep the best.
- **Stochastic Hill Climbing:** Pick a random better neighbor instead of the best.
- **Simulated Annealing:** Allow occasional downhill moves with decreasing probability.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**AO\* Definition (1 mark):**
AO* is the extension of A* to **AND-OR graphs**. It is used when solving a problem requires solving **all** sub-problems of an AND-node or **any one** of an OR-node.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Initialize start node $s$ with heuristic $h(s)$.
2. Expand a node on the best path marked unexpanded.
3. For each successor, compute $h$ (use heuristic if leaf).
4. **Propagate costs upward**:
   - OR-node: $h(n) = \min_i [c(n, n_i) + h(n_i)]$.
   - AND-node: $h(n) = c(n) + \sum_i h(n_i)$.
5. Mark best successors/ancestors as SOLVED.
6. Stop when start is SOLVED.

**Example (2 marks):**
Start $A$ (OR) with children $B$ (AND) and $C$ (OR). $B$ has children $D, E$ (both goals). $C$ has child $F$ (goal). Costs: $A\to B = 2$, $B \to \{D,E\}$ summed $= 5$, $A\to C = 1$, $C \to F = 1$, $h(D)=h(E)=h(F)=0$.
- $h(B) = 2 + 0 + 0 = 2$
- $h(C) = 1 + 0 = 1$
- $h(A) = \min[2 + 2,\ 1 + 1] = \min[4, 2] = 2$
- **Solution:** $A \to C \to F$ with cost **2**.

**A\* vs AO\* Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | A* | AO* |
|---|---|---|
| Graph | OR only | AND-OR |
| Cost at AND | Not defined | Sum of children |
| Best for | Single path | Decomposable problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Mark loss 1:** Students confuse the **AND cost formula** with the OR cost formula. AND uses **sum**, OR uses **min**. This single error costs 2-3 marks.
> - **Mark loss 2:** Students forget to list **limitations and remedies** in the Hill Climbing question — KTU expects at least 3 limitations and 2 remedies.
> - **Mark loss 3:** For AO*, students provide only the algorithm but not a **worked example**. The example is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for Exam Eve):**

### Hill Climbing
- ☐ A **local search** algorithm — no memory of the path.
- ☐ Uses an **evaluation function** $f(n)$, not a cost function.
- ☐ Three variants: **Simple**, **Steepest-Ascent**, **Stochastic**.
- ☐ Three classic problems: **Local Maxima, Plateaus, Ridges**.
- ☐ Best fix for local maxima: **Random Restart**.
- ☐ Time: $O(b \cdot d)$; Space: $O(b)$.
- ☐ **Not optimal**, **not complete** in general.

### A* Search
- ☐ Uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- ☐ Maintains **OPEN (priority queue)** and **CLOSED (set)**.
- ☐ **Admissible heuristic** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ → A* is **optimal**.
- ☐ **Consistent heuristic** $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ → A* is **efficient** (no re-expansion).
- ☐ Manhattan distance = admissible on 4-connected grids.
- ☐ Straight-line (Euclidean) distance = admissible and consistent.
- ☐ At goal, $f = g$ (since $h = 0$).
- ☐ **Complete** in finite graphs with positive edge costs.

### AO* Search
- ☐ Extension of A* to **AND-OR graphs**.
- ☐ **OR-node cost** = min over successors.
- ☐ **AND-node cost** = own cost + sum of successors' costs.
- ☐ Used for **problem decomposition** where all sub-tasks are mandatory.
- ☐ Returns a **solution graph** (subgraph), not just a path.
- ☐ Applications: automated planning, theorem proving, expert systems.

### KTU Buzzwords to Memorize
- "Heuristic", "Admissible", "Consistent", "Monotonic", "Best-First"
- "Local Maxima", "Plateau", "Ridge", "Random Restart"
- "AND-OR graph", "Solution Subgraph", "Cost Propagation"
- "OPEN list", "CLOSED list", "Priority Queue", "$f$-value"

### Common Student Traps
- ❌ Treating **A\*** and **AO\*** as the same.
- ❌ Forgetting that Hill Climbing has **no global view**.
- ❌ Using $h$ that **overestimates** (breaks optimality).
- ❌ Confusing $g(n)$ (actual cost so far) with $h(n)$ (estimated remaining).
- ❌ Not updating parent pointers → cannot reconstruct the path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:** Always draw the **graph, OPEN, and CLOSED lists** explicitly in your A* solutions. Always show the **cost propagation arrows** in AO* diagrams. Always state the **heuristic admissibility** when claiming A* optimality. These three habits alone can fetch 3-4 extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Informed and Uninformed Search Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Informed and Uninformed Search Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and classical **search problem solving**, the term **Search Algorithm** refers to a systematic computational procedure that navigates through a state-space graph (or search tree) to locate a path from an **initial state** to one or more **goal states**. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies these algorithms into two broad families based on whether they use **problem-specific knowledge** (heuristics) or rely purely on the **structural topology** of the search space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uninformed Search (Blind Search):** A family of search strategies that possess **no additional information** about goal location beyond the problem definition itself. The algorithm can only distinguish a goal state from a non-goal state. It explores the state space using arbitrary, fixed ordering rules.
>
> **Informed Search (Heuristic Search):** A family of search strategies that employ **problem-specific heuristic functions** $h(n)$ to estimate the cost from any state $n$ to the nearest goal, thereby prioritizing exploration toward the most promising nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase **"informedness"** is formally defined as the *amount* of problem-specific knowledge available to the search beyond the basic problem formulation. KTU examiners specifically test this conceptual distinction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lost Tourist

Imagine you are **lost in a city** (a giant road network) and wish to reach the **central railway station** (goal state).

- **Uninformed Search = Walking in the dark with a blindfold.** You can only count intersections (nodes) and roads (edges), or simply walk straight down one street and backtrack. You have **no clue** where the station is. Strategies like BFS, DFS, and UCS are blind strategies.

- **Informed Search = Walking with a GPS device that always displays the straight-line distance to the station.** Even though roads are curved, the GPS gives you a useful **heuristic estimate** $h(n)$. This drastically reduces wandering. Strategies like Greedy Best-First Search and **A\*** are heuristic-driven.

The *stronger* the heuristic's quality (closer to the true cost), the *more informed* the search becomes — and the less nodes it must explore.

### 1.3 Syllabus-Highlight Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Coverage Map**
> 1. **Uninformed:** BFS, Uniform Cost Search (UCS), DFS, Depth-Limited Search (DLS), Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS), Bidirectional Search.
> 2. **Informed:** Greedy Best-First Search, **A\* Search**, Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*), Memory-Bounded A\* (MA\*, SMA\*).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used for Comparison

The following four metrics (referred to as the **"Big Four Evaluation Criteria"** in KTU valuation keys) are used throughout this module to grade any search algorithm:

| Metric | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Completeness** | Does the algorithm guarantee finding a solution if one exists? |
| **Optimality** | Does it guarantee the *least-cost* solution? |
| **Time Complexity** | Number of nodes generated/expanded in the worst case. |
| **Space Complexity** | Maximum number of nodes held in memory at any time. |

Where **b** = branching factor, **d** = depth of shallowest goal, and **m** = maximum depth of state space.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Uninformed Search

Uninformed algorithms operate on a generic **Tree-Search / Graph-Search** skeleton using a priority queue (or stack/queue depending on variant):

1. Insert the **initial state** $s_0$ into the frontier $F$ (a data structure: queue, stack, or priority queue).
2. If $F$ is empty, return **FAILURE** (no solution exists).
3. Pop the next node $n$ from $F$ according to the variant's ordering rule.
4. If $n$ is a **goal state**, return the corresponding solution path.
5. For each action $a$ applicable to $n$, generate the successor $n'$ with path cost $g(n')$.
6. Insert $n'$ into $F$ and go to step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> The *only* difference between the various uninformed strategies is the **ordering rule** used in Step 3. This single decision changes the algorithm's behaviour drastically — a key insight KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Informed Search

Informed algorithms augment Step 3 with an **evaluation function** $f(n)$ that biases node expansion toward goals:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from initial state to node $n$ (path cost so far).
- $h(n)$ = **heuristic estimate** of the cost from $n$ to the nearest goal.
- $f(n)$ = estimated cost of the *cheapest* solution passing through $n$.

Special cases yield the two most important informed algorithms:
- **Greedy Best-First Search:** $f(n) = h(n)$ → ignores $g(n)$, only chases the goal.
- **A\* Search:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ → balances path-so-far with goal-estimate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important comparison artefact** for Module 3. It is reproduced almost verbatim in KTU University Examinations and must be memorised.

| Algorithm | Type | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Complete? | Optimal? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** | Uninformed | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ | **Yes** | **Yes** (uniform step cost) |
| **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** | Uninformed | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^{\*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^{\*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Depth-First Search (DFS)** | Uninformed | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | No (finite $m$) | No |
| **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** | Uninformed | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b \cdot \ell)$ | No (if $\ell < d$) | No |
| **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** | Uninformed | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | **Yes** | **Yes** (uniform step cost) |
| **Bidirectional Search** | Uninformed | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | **Yes** (if both directions applicable) | **Yes** |
| **Greedy Best-First Search** | Informed | $O(b^{m})$ worst | $O(b^{m})$ worst | No | No |
| **A\* Search** | Informed | Exponential in path-error (polynomial if $\epsilon$ bounded) | Same as time | **Yes** (with tree-search) | **Yes** (admissible $h$) |

Where:
- $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of optimal solution, $m$ = maximum tree depth.
- $\ell$ = depth limit, $C^{\*}$ = optimal solution cost, $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost.
- The notation $\lfloor x \rfloor$ denotes the **greatest integer function** (floor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"BFS = Bread = a lot of RAM; DFS = Deep = little RAM but may get lost forever."*

### 2.4 Key Properties of Heuristic Functions

For the **informed search** family — particularly **A\*** — KTU examiners frequently test three critical heuristic properties:

| Property | Definition | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Admissibility** | $h(n) \le h^{\*}(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^{\*}$ is the true cost to goal. | Guarantees **A\* optimality**. |
| **Consistency (Monotonicity)** | $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every action. | Guarantees graph-search optimality. |
| **Dominance** | $h_{2}(n) \ge h_{1}(n)$ for all $n$ (with admissibility). | $h_{2}$ expands *no more* nodes than $h_{1}$. |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Uninformed Search** is used in **compiler design** (syntax tree traversal — BFS/DFS over AST), **garbage collection** (DFS mark-and-sweep), and **network routing fallbacks**.
- **Informed Search** powers **Google Maps** (A\* on road networks with Haversine heuristic), **game AI** (path-finding in chess/StarCraft), **robotics motion planning**, and **logistics** (Amazon/Uber route optimisation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why BFS is Optimal for Uniform Step Cost

Let $\epsilon$ be the **minimum positive step cost**. BFS expands nodes in **non-decreasing order of path depth**.

**Step 1 — BFS expands shallowest nodes first.**
At depth $k$, BFS expands all nodes of depth $k$ before any node of depth $k+1$.

**Step 2 — Uniform cost assumption.**
If every action has cost $\ge \epsilon$, then the path cost to a goal at depth $d$ satisfies:

$$g(\text{goal at depth } d) \ge d \cdot \epsilon$$

**Step 3 — Shallowness implies lower-bound cost.**
A goal at depth $d$ must have cost $\ge d \cdot \epsilon$. BFS, by expanding all shallower nodes first, finds this goal *before* any deeper, more expensive goal.

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{BFS goal}} \le \text{Cost}_{\text{any other goal}} \quad \text{(uniform step cost)}$$

Therefore, BFS is **optimal under uniform step cost**. End of derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Admissibility Condition for A\*

**Theorem (Hart, Nilsson, Raphael 1968):** *A\* using a tree-search and an admissible heuristic $h$ returns an optimal solution.*

**Proof Sketch:**

Let $C^{\*}$ be the optimal solution cost, and let $G$ be the goal finally returned by A\*. Suppose, for contradiction, $g(G) > C^{\*}$.

At the moment $G$ is chosen for expansion, A\* picks the node with minimum $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. There exists at least one **optimal-path node** $n^{\*}$ on the optimal path that is still in the frontier.

**Step 1 — Admissibility bound.**
Since $h$ is admissible, $h(n^{\*}) \le h^{\*}(n^{\*}) = \text{remaining optimal cost}$.

**Step 2 — Optimal path node value.**
For $n^{\*}$:

$$f(n^{\*}) = g(n^{\*}) + h(n^{\*}) \le g(n^{\*}) + h^{\*}(n^{\*}) = C^{\*}$$

**Step 3 — Contradiction.**
For the suboptimal goal $G$:

$$f(G) = g(G) + h(G) = g(G) + 0 = g(G) > C^{\*}$$

Thus $f(n^{\*}) < f(G)$, meaning A\* would have picked $n^{\*}$ before $G$ — contradiction. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation: IDDFS Time Complexity

IDDFS performs **DLS with limit $0, 1, 2, \ldots, d$** sequentially. The number of nodes generated in the $k$-th iteration is $b^{k}$ (root at level 0).

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} = b^{0} + b^{1} + b^{2} + \cdots + b^{d}$$

**Geometric series evaluation:**

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} = \frac{b \cdot (b^{d} - \frac{1}{b})}{b - 1}$$

**Step 1 — Asymptotic analysis:**
For $b \ge 2$, the dominant term is the *last* iteration $b^{d}$:

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} \le b^{d} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{b-1} \right) = b^{d} \cdot \frac{b}{b-1}$$

**Step 2 — Final bound:**

$$T_{\text{IDDFS}} = O(b^{d})$$

This matches BFS time but with the **memory of DFS** $O(bd)$, hence IDDFS is the optimal choice when the search depth is unknown and memory is constrained. End of derivation.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which?

| Scenario | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shallow solution, abundant memory | **BFS** | $O(b^{d})$ time and space; optimal and complete. |
| Deep solution, scarce memory, no goal-cost info | **IDDFS** | $O(bd)$ space, $O(b^{d})$ time; also optimal. |
| Variable step costs, cost-known actions | **UCS** | Dijkstra-like; optimal for $\epsilon$-bounded positive costs. |
| Goal far away, lots of dead-ends, no heuristic | **DFS** | Memory-efficient but risky. |
| Heuristic available, must find optimal path | **A\*** | $f = g + h$ with admissible $h$. |
| Heuristic available, path cost irrelevant | **Greedy Best-First** | Fastest, but may return suboptimal path. |
| Real-time, no offline compute | **Greedy / Hill Climbing** | Instantaneous decisions, may be locally optimal only. |
| Very large state space, low memory | **IDA\* / SMA\*** | Memory-bounded variants of A\*. |

### 3.5 Worked Comparison Example — The Romania Road Map

Consider the classical **Romania route-finding problem** (initial: Arad, goal: Bucharest).

| Algorithm | Nodes Expanded | Optimal Path Found? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BFS** | $\approx 1{,}000+$ (by depth) | Yes (if uniform cost) |
| **DFS** | $\approx 10$ (gets lucky) or $1{,}000+$ | No (may pick long route) |
| **UCS** | $\approx 11$ (only path-cost-aware) | Yes (418 km) |
| **Greedy (SLD heuristic)** | $\approx 7$ | No (450 km) |
| **A\* (SLD heuristic)** | $\approx 7$ | **Yes (418 km)** |

This is the **canonical KTU textbook example** — students are expected to reproduce these counts and conclude that **A\* is strictly superior** to its uninformed counterparts when a good heuristic exists.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Search Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["SEARCH ALGORITHMS"] --> rootB["UNINFORMED BLIND SEARCH"]
    rootA --> rootC["INFORMED HEURISTIC SEARCH"]
    rootB --> nodeU1["BREADTH FIRST SEARCH BFS"]
    rootB --> nodeU2["UNIFORM COST SEARCH UCS"]
    rootB --> nodeU3["DEPTH FIRST SEARCH DFS"]
    rootB --> nodeU4["DEPTH LIMITED SEARCH DLS"]
    rootB --> nodeU5["ITERATIVE DEEPENING DFS IDDFS"]
    rootB --> nodeU6["BIDIRECTIONAL SEARCH"]
    rootC --> nodeI1["GREEDY BEST FIRST SEARCH"]
    rootC --> nodeI2["A STAR SEARCH"]
    rootC --> nodeI3["ITERATIVE DEEPENING A STAR IDA"]
    rootC --> nodeI4["MEMORY BOUNDED A STAR SMA"]
    nodeU1 --> meta1["USES QUEUE FIFO"]
    nodeU3 --> meta2["USES STACK LIFO"]
    nodeU2 --> meta3["USES PRIORITY QUEUE BY G"]
    nodeI1 --> meta4["USES PRIORITY QUEUE BY H"]
    nodeI2 --> meta5["USES PRIORITY QUEUE BY G PLUS H"]
```

```mermaid

    stepA["START INITIAL NODE S0"] --> stepB["INSERT S0 INTO OPEN LIST"]
    stepB --> stepC{"IS OPEN LIST EMPTY"}
    stepC -- YES --> stepD["RETURN FAILURE"]
    stepC -- NO --> stepE["POP NODE N WITH MIN F N FROM OPEN"]
    stepE --> stepF{"IS N GOAL NODE"}
    stepF -- YES --> stepG["RETURN OPTIMAL PATH"]
    stepF -- NO --> stepH["MOVE N TO CLOSED LIST"]
    stepH --> stepI["GENERATE SUCCESSORS N PRIME"]
    stepI --> stepJ["COMPUTE G N PRIME H N PRIME F N PRIME"]

    stepK --> stepL["HEURISTIC DATABASE PROVIDES H VALUES"]
    stepL --> stepC
```

### 4.3 Comparative Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid

    axisA["UNINFORMED"] -- "FASTER, SMARTER" --> axisB["INFORMED"]
    axisA --> prop1["NO HEURISTIC NEEDED"]
    axisA --> prop2["GUARANTEED COMPLETE"]
    axisA --> prop3["DOMAIN INDEPENDENT"]
    axisB --> prop4["REQUIRES GOOD HEURISTIC"]
    axisB --> prop5["PROBLEM DEPENDENT"]
    axisB --> prop6["EXPONENTIALLY FASTER OFTEN"]
    axisA --> memoryA["MEMORY OK FOR BFS BUT BAD FOR DEEP"]
    axisB --> memoryB["MEMORY INTENSIVE BUT OFTEN OPTIMAL"]
```

```mermaid
