# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Q1.** Define the **Minimax algorithm** as used in adversarial game-playing. State the Minimax recurrence relation and explain the roles of **MAX** and **MIN** players.

**Model Answer:**

The Minimax algorithm is a recursive adversarial search procedure used in two-player, zero-sum, perfect-information games. It assumes that one player, **MAX**, tries to maximize the final utility, while the opponent, **MIN**, tries to minimize it. The algorithm explores the entire game tree to a fixed depth (or to terminal states), then back-propagates values. The Minimax value $V(s)$ of any state $s$ is given by the recurrence:

$$
V(s) = \begin{cases} U(s), & \text{if } s \text{ is terminal} \\ \max_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a)), & \text{if MAX's turn} \\ \min_{a} V(\text{Result}(s,a)), & \text{if MIN's turn} \end{cases}
$$

MAX represents the AI agent and seeks the highest value. MIN represents the opponent and seeks the lowest value. The algorithm is *complete* and *optimal* against an optimal opponent.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for recurrence, 1 for roles of MAX/MIN.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Q2.** What is **alpha-beta pruning**? Write down the pruning condition and explain what the parameters **α** and **β** represent.

**Model Answer:**

Alpha-beta pruning is an optimization of the Minimax algorithm that **eliminates branches of the game tree** which cannot influence the final decision. The two parameters maintained are:

- **α (alpha):** the highest utility value that MAX can guarantee so far along the current path. Initial value: $-\infty$.
- **β (beta):** the lowest utility value that MIN can guarantee so far along the current path. Initial value: $+\infty$.

**Pruning condition:**

$$
\alpha \ge \beta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{stop exploring remaining siblings}
$$

Alpha-beta pruning **does not change the final move chosen** by Minimax; it only reduces the number of nodes visited. In the best case (perfect move ordering), the effective branching factor reduces from $b$ to $\sqrt{b}$, giving time complexity $O(b^{d/2})$ instead of $O(b^d)$.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for α/β, 1 for pruning condition and complexity.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: *Module Internal Choice* — students answer **one** of two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Q(a).** Draw the complete game tree for Tic-Tac-Toe starting from the empty board down to a depth of **2 plies** (X plays one move, O plays one move). Label MAX and MIN nodes. Show the Minimax values of all leaves and back-propagate to the root. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b).** For the same tree from part (a), trace the **alpha-beta pruning** algorithm step-by-step. Identify which nodes are pruned and calculate the **percentage reduction** in nodes evaluated compared to plain Minimax. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

The empty Tic-Tac-Toe board gives X (MAX) **9 possible first moves**. For each, O (MIN) has **8 possible replies** (decreasing). We consider only depth 2 (i.e., one move by each side, plus root).

**Root (depth 0, MAX):** 9 children, one for each cell X may play.

**At depth 1 (MIN):** For each X move, O has 8 reply cells (any empty cell).

**Leaves at depth 2:** Terminal evaluation? At depth 2, no terminal state is reachable in Tic-Tac-Toe (need at least 5 X's or 4 O's in a row for a win). So we must either (i) expand to full depth, or (ii) apply a **heuristic evaluation function** $H(s)$ for non-terminal states.

A common heuristic for Tic-Tac-Toe at non-terminal states:

$$
H(s) = (\text{rows/cols/diags open for X}) - (\text{rows/cols/diags open for O})
$$

For brevity in an exam, students typically:

1. Show the **root with 9 children** (one for each X move).
2. Show that each child has 8 grandchildren.
3. State that the full expansion to depth 2 has $1 + 9 + 72 = 82$ nodes visited.
4. Back-propagate values upward (the exact leaf heuristic values depend on the board layout, so a representative illustration suffices).

For each of X's 9 first moves, by symmetry, the Minimax value at depth 2 is **0** (draw) for all — except the **center** move, which by symmetry also evaluates to 0, and the **corner** moves which also evaluate to 0. In Tic-Tac-Toe with perfect play, all opening moves lead to a draw, so $V(\text{root}) = 0$. Students should note this symmetry argument to save time.

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct tree structure with MAX/MIN labels: **2 marks**
- Leaf evaluation strategy: **2 marks**
- Back-propagation: **2 marks**
- Final root value and chosen move (any center or corner): **1 mark**

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

Starting $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$ at the root.

**Move-ordering heuristic:** explore center first, then corners, then edges (best-first).

1. **MAX explores center move** (X in middle):
   - State reaches depth 1. $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.
   - **MIN explores reply 1**: heuristic value $h_1$. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, h_1) = h_1$.
   - Check $\alpha \ge \beta$? $-\infty \ge h_1$? No (assuming $h_1 > -\infty$). Continue.
   - **MIN explores reply 2**: $h_2$. $\beta = \min(h_1, h_2)$. Continue.
   - ... all 8 replies evaluated, no pruning possible at depth 1.
   - Final value of center-move subtree: $V_c$. Update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, V_c) = V_c$.
2. **MAX explores corner move** (X in top-left):
   - Call MIN with current $\alpha = V_c$, $\beta = +\infty$.
   - **MIN explores reply 1**: heuristic $h_1'$. $\beta = \min(+\infty, h_1') = h_1'$.
   - Check $\alpha \ge \beta$? $V_c \ge h_1'$? If **yes** (because $V_c$ is already the best MAX can guarantee and the new branch is worse), then **prune the remaining 7 replies** of this corner subtree.
3. This pattern repeats: most replies at MIN level are pruned once $\beta \le \alpha$.

**Quantitative reduction:**
- Plain Minimax visits all $1 + 9 + 72 = 82$ nodes.
- With best-case α-β, only about $1 + 9 + 9 \cdot \sqrt{8} \approx 1 + 9 + 25.5 \approx 36$ nodes are visited (approximately).
- The **percentage reduction** is roughly:

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{82 - 36}{82} \times 100\% \approx 56\%
$$

Exact numbers depend on the heuristic used. Students should show a **specific numerical table** of nodes explored vs. pruned for full credit.

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct α/β initialization and update logic: **2 marks**
- Correct identification of pruned branches: **3 marks**
- Correct percentage calculation: **2 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing α and β**: α is updated at MAX nodes; β is updated at MIN nodes. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to reset α, β for each subtree**: the cutoff check must occur *after* every child evaluation, not just at the end.
> 3. **Stating the pruning condition as α > β instead of α ≥ β**: both work mathematically, but KTU textbooks use **α ≥ β** as the canonical cutoff.
> 4. **Forgetting that pruning preserves optimality**: students often incorrectly claim α-β "approximates" Minimax — it is *exactly* equivalent in value, only faster.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Q(a).** Explain why Tic-Tac-Toe is considered a **zero-sum, perfect-information, deterministic** game. Define the **utility function** for the game and list all possible terminal outcomes. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b).** Implement the **Minimax algorithm in Python** for a Tic-Tac-Toe AI. Your code should (i) represent the board, (ii) check for terminal states, (iii) recursively compute Minimax values, and (iv) return the best move. Explain each section of the code with comments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Why Tic-Tac-Toe fits the formal game model:**

- **Zero-sum:** The sum of utilities for both players is always zero. If X wins ($U = +1$), then O loses ($U = -1$), and $1 + (-1) = 0$. Draws give both players $U = 0$, again summing to $0$. One player's gain is exactly the other's loss.

- **Perfect information:** Both players can see the entire board at all times. There are no hidden cards, no dice, no concealed information. Every player knows everything relevant to the game state.

- **Deterministic:** There is no randomness. Given a board state and a chosen move, the next state is fully determined — no dice rolls, no shuffled cards, no probabilistic elements.

- **Two-player, turn-based, sequential:** The game proceeds in alternating turns, with each player making exactly one move per turn.

**Utility function:**

$$
U(s) = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{if } s \text{ is a terminal state where X (MAX) has three in a row} \\ -1, & \text{if } s \text{ is a terminal state where O (MIN) has three in a row} \\ \phantom{-}0, & \text{if } s \text{ is a terminal state with no three in a row (draw)} \end{cases}
$$

**Terminal outcomes:** There are exactly **three** terminal outcomes from the perspective of MAX:
1. **Win** ($U = +1$): three X's in a line (row, column, or diagonal).
2. **Loss** ($U = -1$): three O's in a line.
3. **Draw** ($U = 0$): all 9 cells filled, no three in a line.

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Three property justifications (zero-sum, perfect-info, deterministic): **3 marks**
- Utility function with all three cases: **2 marks**
- List of terminal outcomes: **2 marks**

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

A clean, well-commented Python implementation:

```python
import math

# ---------- 1. Board Representation ----------
# The board is a list of 9 strings: indices 0..8 mapping to the 3x3 grid:
#  0 | 1 | 2
# -----------
#  3 | 4 | 5
# -----------
#  6 | 7 | 8
EMPTY = " "
HUMAN = "O"     # MIN player
AI    = "X"     # MAX player

def print_board(board):
    print(f"{board[0]} | {board[1]} | {board[2]}")
    print("--+---+--")
    print(f"{board[3]} | {board[4]} | {board[5]}")
    print("--+---+--")
    print(f"{board[6]} | {board[7]} | {board[8]}")

# ---------- 2. Terminal State Checker ----------
def is_terminal(board):
    wins = [(0,1,2),(3,4,5),(6,7,8),
            (0,3,6),(1,4,7),(2,5,8),
            (0,4,8),(2,4,6)]
    for a,b,c in wins:
        if board[a] == board[b] == board[c] != EMPTY:
            return board[a]          # returns the winner symbol
    if EMPTY not in board:
        return "draw"
    return None                     # not terminal

def utility(state):
    # state is the return value of is_terminal
    if state == AI:    return  1
    if state == HUMAN: return -1
    return 0                         # draw

# ---------- 3. Minimax Recursion ----------
def minimax(board, is_max_turn):
    term = is_terminal(board)
    if term is not None:
        return utility(term), None   # no move needed at a terminal state

    if is_max_turn:                  # AI (X)
        best_score = -math.inf
        best_move  = None
        for i in range(9):
            if board[i] == EMPTY:
                board[i] = AI
                score, _ = minimax(board, False)
                board[i] = EMPTY
                if score > best_score:
                    best_score = score
                    best_move  = i
        return best_score, best_move
    else:                            # HUMAN (O)
        best_score = math.inf
        best_move  = None
        for i in range(9):
            if board[i] == EMPTY:
                board[i] = HUMAN
                score, _ = minimax(board, True)
                board[i] = EMPTY
                if score < best_score:
                    best_score = score
                    best_move  = i
        return best_score, best_move

# ---------- 4. Best Move Selection ----------
def best_move_for_AI(board):
    _, move = minimax(board, is_max_turn=True)
    return move

# ---------- 5. Example Driver ----------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    b = [EMPTY]*9
    print("Empty board:"); print_board(b)
    move = best_move_for_AI(b)
    print(f"\nAI (X) plays cell index: {move}")
```

**Explanation of each section:**

- **(1) Board representation:** A flat list of 9 strings, where each index corresponds to a cell. `EMPTY`, `HUMAN`, `AI` are constants for clarity.
- **(2) Terminal state checker:** The function `is_terminal` examines all 8 winning lines (3 rows, 3 columns, 2 diagonals). If any is filled by the same non-empty player, that player wins. If no empty cell remains, it's a draw. Otherwise, the game continues.
- **(3) Minimax recursion:** The function `minimax` returns a tuple `(score, move)`. At MAX (AI) nodes it picks the move that maximizes the eventual score; at MIN (HUMAN) nodes, the move that minimizes it. Recursion bottoms out at terminal states.
- **(4) Best-move selection:** The driver calls `minimax` from the empty board with `is_max_turn=True` and extracts the move.
- **(5) Example driver:** Demonstrates usage.

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Correct board representation: **1 mark**
- Correct terminal check: **2 marks**
- Correct recursive Minimax with MAX/MIN branches: **3 marks**
- Correct best-move return and overall integration: **1 mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q(b):**
> 1. **In-place mutation without undo:** Forgetting to reset `board[i] = EMPTY` after the recursive call corrupts the game tree and produces wrong values. This is the most common coding bug — **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing MAX with which symbol:** Some students assign MAX to "O" by mistake. State explicitly that **AI = X = MAX** and **HUMAN = O = MIN**.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle the draw case:** If `is_terminal` returns `"draw"`, the utility function must return 0, not `None`.
> 4. **No depth limit explanation:** In Tic-Tac-Toe the full game tree is small (9 plies), so depth limit is not strictly needed — but students should mention this. For deeper games like chess, a depth limit plus heuristic evaluation function is required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Game-playing in AI** = adversarial search under the assumption of a rational opponent.
- A game is described by: **initial state**, **players**, **actions**, **terminal test**, and **utility function**.
- **Tic-Tac-Toe** is *zero-sum*, *perfect-information*, *deterministic*, two-player.
- The **Minimax algorithm** computes the optimal move by recursively evaluating all terminal outcomes and back-propagating MAX/MIN values.
- **Minimax recurrence:** $V(s) = U(s)$ if terminal; else $\max/\min$ over child values.
- **Minimax complexity:** Time $O(b^d)$, Space $O(bd)$.
- **Alpha-beta pruning** is an optimization, not a different algorithm. It uses two parameters, $\alpha$ and $\beta$, to skip subtrees that cannot affect the final decision.
- **α** = best value MAX can guarantee so far (init $-\infty$).
- **β** = best value MIN can guarantee so far (init $+\infty$).
- **Pruning condition:** $\alpha \ge \beta$ — stop exploring remaining siblings.
- **Alpha-beta best case:** Time $O(b^{d/2})$ with perfect move ordering.
- **Move ordering** (best-first) is critical to maximize pruning — explore promising moves first.
- Alpha-beta pruning **preserves the optimal move and value**; it only reduces nodes evaluated.
- The Tic-Tac-Toe game tree has $\le 9! = 362{,}880$ terminal sequences, but only **255,168 unique games** (symmetry-reduced).
- With perfect play, Tic-Tac-Toe always ends in a **draw**.
- The Minimax algorithm is the conceptual basis for more advanced algorithms like **expectimax** (with chance nodes) and **MCTS** (Monte Carlo Tree Search) used in modern game AI.
- Python implementation requires careful **backtracking** (undo moves on a mutable board) to avoid corrupting the search.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to CSP: Definitions and Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to CSP: Definitions and Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Introduction to CSP: Definitions and Basic Concepts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a mathematical framework used in Artificial Intelligence to represent and solve problems where the objective is to find values for a set of variables that satisfy a collection of specified constraints. Formally, a CSP is defined as a triple:

$$\langle X, D, C \rangle$$

where:
- **X = {X₁, X₂, ..., Xₙ}** is a finite set of **variables**.
- **D = {D₁, D₂, ..., Dₙ}** is a set of **domains**, where each **Dᵢ** is the finite set of allowable values for variable **Xᵢ**.
- **C = {C₁, C₂, ..., Cₘ}** is a set of **constraints**, where each **Cⱼ** specifies allowed combinations of values for a subset of variables.

A **solution** (or **consistent assignment**) to a CSP is an assignment of a value to every variable, drawn from its respective domain, such that **every constraint is satisfied simultaneously**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEITT613, Module 4):** Students must be able to define the three components of a CSP, classify constraint types, draw constraint graphs, and articulate the difference between a *complete* and *partial* assignment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wedding Seating Arrangement

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are organizing a wedding reception. You have 5 tables (variables: T₁, T₂, T₃, T₄, T₅), each must seat exactly one guest from a fixed invitee list (domain), but with the rule that two particular guests who are feuding must NOT be seated at the same table (constraint), and an elderly uncle must be seated next to a family member (another constraint). Your job is to **assign** every guest to a table such that **all** the rules (constraints) hold true. That is exactly a CSP!

In this analogy:
- **Variables** → Tables (or guests to be placed).
- **Domains** → List of possible guests (or table numbers).
- **Constraints** → Social rules (feuding guests, age requirements, etc.).
- **Solution** → A complete seating chart that respects every rule.

### 1.3 Why CSPs Matter in AI

CSPs provide a declarative representation — you **state** what the constraints are rather than how to solve them. This makes them ideal for problems such as:

| Real-World Application | Variables | Domain | Constraints |
|------------------------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Map Coloring | Regions of a map | Set of colors | Adjacent regions differ in color |
| Sudoku | 81 cells | {1,2,...,9} | Row, column, 3×3 box uniqueness |
| N-Queens | N board positions | {1,...,N} | No two queens share row, column, or diagonal |
| Timetabling | Exam slots, rooms, invigilators | Available slots | No student has overlapping exams |
| Cryptarithmetic | Letters in a puzzle | {0,1,...,9} | Leading digit non-zero, arithmetic holds |
| Job Shop Scheduling | Machines, jobs | Time intervals | No machine overlap, precedence rules |

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Frequently Tested Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Assignment:** A mapping from a subset of variables to values in their domains.
- **Complete Assignment:** Every variable has been assigned a value.
- **Partial Assignment:** Only some variables have been assigned.
- **Consistent Assignment:** An assignment that does not violate any constraint.
- **Solution:** A complete and consistent assignment.
- **Constraint Graph (or Hypergraph):** A graph where each node is a variable and each edge (or hyperedge) represents a constraint relating those variables.
- **Constraint Scope (or Arity):** The number of variables involved in a single constraint.
- **Tuple:** An ordered set of values for the variables in a constraint's scope.
- **Relation:** The set of satisfying tuples of a constraint.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a CSP — Deconstructed

A CSP is built upon three rigorously defined components. Let us analyze each in depth.

#### 2.1.1 Variables (X)

Variables are the *unknowns* of the problem. In a CSP, they are typically **discrete** and **finite**. Examples include:
- The cells of a Sudoku grid.
- The regions of a map.
- The letters of a word puzzle (e.g., SEND + MORE = MONEY).

#### 2.1.2 Domains (D)

The domain **Dᵢ** of variable **Xᵢ** is the set of allowable values that variable can take. Domains can be:
- **Finite Discrete:** {Red, Green, Blue} for coloring.
- **Infinite Discrete:** The set of all integers.
- **Continuous:** Real-valued variables (these lead to **Constraint Optimization Problems**, COP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** Module 4 explicitly deals with **finite discrete domains**. Infinite and continuous domains are out of scope for the introductory module.

#### 2.1.3 Constraints (C)

A constraint **Cⱼ** is a relation **Rⱼ ⊆ D₁ × D₂ × ... × Dₖ** over the variables in its scope, defining the allowed combinations of values.

### 2.2 Classification of Constraints

#### 2.2.1 By Arity (Number of Variables Involved)

| Type | Scope | Example | KTU Relevance |
|------|-------|---------|---------------|
| **Unary** | 1 variable | X₁ ≠ 2 | Rarely tested alone |
| **Binary** | 2 variables | X₁ ≠ X₂ (map coloring) | **Most common in KTU board papers** |
| **Ternary (Higher-order)** | 3+ variables | X + Y = Z (cryptarithmetic) | Tested in Part B 14-mark questions |

#### 2.2.2 By Mathematical Nature

- **Absolute (Hard) Constraints:** Must be satisfied. E.g., "no two adjacent regions share a color."
- **Preference (Soft) Constraints:** Nice to satisfy but optional. These lead to **Constraint Optimization Problems (COP)**, which use a *cost function* instead of a strict relation.

#### 2.2.3 By Representation Form

- **Intentional Constraints:** Defined by a formula or property (e.g., "X₁ < X₂").
- **Extensional Constraints:** Defined by enumerating all allowed tuples.

### 2.3 Formal Representation of Constraints

A binary constraint between **Xᵢ** and **Xⱼ** can be represented as a relation:

$$R_{ij} \subseteq D_i \times D_j$$

A tuple **<a, b>** belongs to **Rᵢⱼ** if and only if the assignment **(Xᵢ = a, Xⱼ = b)** is permitted.

**Logical Representation (Standard KTU Form):**

$$\text{Constraint } C_{ij}: \quad (X_i, X_j) \in R_{ij}$$

For example, for map coloring of Australia with 3 colors {R, G, B}:

$$R_{WA,NT} = \{ (R,G), (R,B), (G,R), (G,B), (B,R), (B,G) \}$$

This excludes **(R, R), (G, G), (B, B)** since adjacent regions must differ.

### 2.4 Constraint Graph (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Constraint Graph):** A graph **G = (V, E)** in which:
> - Each **node v ∈ V** corresponds to a CSP variable.
> - Each **edge (Xᵢ, Xⱼ) ∈ E** corresponds to a binary constraint between variables **Xᵢ** and **Xⱼ**.

For **higher-order constraints** (arity > 2), the structure is called a **constraint hypergraph**, where hyperedges can connect 3 or more nodes.

**Why constraint graphs matter:**
- They allow graphical algorithms to exploit problem structure.
- A CSP is called a **binary CSP** if all constraints have arity ≤ 2; otherwise, it is **non-binary**.
- *Any* non-binary CSP can be converted into an equivalent binary CSP using the **hidden variable transformation** (out of scope for Module 4 introductory, but worth knowing).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet for the Board Exam — Memorize These!**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | CSP Triple | $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ | Definition of a CSP |
| 2 | Domain size product | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i \vert$ | Total number of complete assignments (search space) |
| 3 | Arity of constraint | $k = \vert \text{scope}(C_j) \vert$ | Number of variables in a constraint |
| 4 | Constraint relation | $R_j \subseteq D_{i_1} \times \cdots \times D_{i_k}$ | Allowed tuples for constraint $C_j$ |
| 5 | Number of constraints | $m = \vert C \vert$ | Total number of constraints in the CSP |
| 6 | Binary CSP size | $n(n-1)/2$ | Maximum possible binary constraints on $n$ variables |
| 7 | Arc Consistency (X→Y) | $\forall x \in D_X, \exists y \in D_Y$ such that $(x,y) \in R_{XY}$ | Every value in $D_X$ has a supporting value in $D_Y$ |
| 8 | Node Consistency | $\forall x \in D_X$, $x$ satisfies all unary constraints on $X$ | All unary constraints are satisfied |
| 9 | Constraint graph density | $\rho = 2E \big/ (n(n-1))$ | Ratio of actual to maximum binary edges |
| 10 | Number of solutions | $N_s \le \prod \vert D_i \vert$ | Upper bound on distinct solutions |

**Engineering Utility:** CSP frameworks power **circuit design verification**, **resource allocation in cloud computing**, **logistics planning (vehicle routing)**, and **bio-informatics (DNA sequencing alignment)** — making it one of the most industrially relevant AI paradigms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Australia Map Coloring (Full CSP Formulation)

The **Australia map coloring** problem is the *canonical* CSP example in AI textbooks. Let us fully derive its representation.

**Step 1: Identify the Variables.**

The Australian mainland (excluding Tasmania for simplicity) has 7 regions:

$$X = \{WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T\}$$

**Step 2: Identify the Domains.**

Every region must be assigned one of three colors:

$$D_i = \{\text{Red}, \text{Green}, \text{Blue}\} \quad \text{for every } i$$

So all seven domains are identical: **D = {Red, Green, Blue}**.

**Step 3: Identify the Constraints.**

Two regions sharing a border cannot have the same color. The borders are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Borders:} \quad & (WA, NT), (WA, SA), (NT, SA), (NT, Q), \\
& (SA, Q), (SA, NSW), (SA, V), (Q, NSW), (NSW, V)
\end{aligned}
$$

Each border gives rise to a binary constraint: *"the two regions must have different colors."* Total binary constraints: **9**.

**Step 4: Mathematical Form of One Constraint.**

For example, the constraint between Western Australia and Northern Territory:

$$C_{WA,NT}: \quad X_{WA} \neq X_{NT}$$

In relational form:

$$R_{WA,NT} = \{ (R,G), (R,B), (G,R), (G,B), (B,R), (B,G) \}$$

This relation has cardinality $\vert R_{WA,NT} \vert = 6$ allowed tuples out of $\vert D_{WA} \vert \times \vert D_{NT} \vert = 9$ possible tuples.

**Step 5: Search Space Calculation.**

Total number of complete assignments (without any constraints) is:

$$\prod_{i=1}^{7} \vert D_i \vert = 3^7 = 2187$$

After enforcing the 9 inequality constraints, only **18** valid complete assignments remain. (This is a verified result from standard AI literature.)

**Step 6: Verify Node Consistency.**

Each variable's domain — {Red, Green, Blue} — has no unary constraint attached, so all variables are trivially node consistent.

**Step 7: Identify the Constraint Graph.**

A graph with 7 nodes and 9 undirected edges representing the borders. (Drawn in Mermaid in Section 4.)

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — N-Queens as a CSP (n = 4)

**Step 1: Variables.**

$$X = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4\}$$

where **Qᵢ** denotes the column position of the queen in row **i**.

**Step 2: Domains.**

$$D_i = \{1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Step 3: Constraints.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Different columns: } Q_i \neq Q_j \quad \text{for } i \neq j \\
&\text{(ii) Not on same diagonal: } \vert Q_i - Q_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert \quad \text{for } i \neq j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Search Space Size.**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{4} \vert D_i \vert = 4^4 = 256$$

But due to constraints, only **2** solutions exist: {Q₁=2, Q₂=4, Q₃=1, Q₄=3} and {Q₁=3, Q₂=1, Q₃=4, Q₄=2}.

**Step 5: Binary CSP Verification.**

Both constraints involve exactly 2 variables (rows), so this is a **binary CSP** with arity = 2 throughout. The constraint graph is the **complete graph K₄** (4 nodes, 6 edges), because every pair of queens interacts.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CSP Variants Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU 14-Mark Questions:**

| Property | Map Coloring | N-Queens | Sudoku | Cryptarithmetic |
|----------|--------------|----------|--------|-----------------|
| Variables | Regions | Rows | Cells | Letters |
| Domain type | Finite, small | Finite, {1..n} | {1..9} | {0..9} |
| Arity | 2 (binary) | 2 (binary) | 2 (binary via pairwise) | Higher (column constraints) |
| Constraint graph | Planar, sparse | Complete K_n | Highly connected | Complex hypergraph |
| Search space | $k^n$ | $n^n$ | $9^{81}$ | $10^k$ |
| Solutions | Many | Few (n) | 1 (valid puzzle) | Usually 1 |
| Symmetry breaking | Useful | Useful | Essential | Essential |

### 3.4 Formal Definition of Node Consistency (Derivation)

**Definition:** A variable **Xᵢ** is **node-consistent** if every value in its domain **Dᵢ** satisfies all unary constraints on **Xᵢ**.

A CSP is **node-consistent** if every variable is node-consistent.

**Procedure to enforce node consistency:**

For each variable **Xᵢ**:
1. For each unary constraint **C** on **Xᵢ**:
2. For each value **v ∈ Dᵢ**:
3. If **v** does not satisfy **C**, then remove **v** from **Dᵢ**.

The result is a node-consistent equivalent CSP (same or smaller domains).

**Example (Map coloring with unary constraint):** Suppose "Western Australia cannot be Green":

$$D_{WA} = \{\text{Red}, \text{Green}, \text{Blue}\} \xrightarrow{\text{after NC}} D_{WA} = \{\text{Red}, \text{Blue}\}$$

New search space size: $3^6 \times 2^1 = 1458$ — a 33% reduction!

### 3.5 Formal Definition of Arc Consistency (Derivation)

**Definition:** A variable **Xᵢ** is **arc-consistent** with respect to **Xⱼ** if for every value **a ∈ Dᵢ**, there exists some value **b ∈ Dⱼ** such that **(a, b)** satisfies the binary constraint **Rᵢⱼ**. A CSP is **arc-consistent** if every variable is arc-consistent with respect to every other variable connected by a constraint.

**Symbolic form:**

$$X_i \text{ is arc-consistent with } X_j \iff \forall a \in D_i, \exists b \in D_j : (a,b) \in R_{ij}$$

**Algorithm to make Xᵢ arc-consistent with Xⱼ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{For each } a \in D_i: \\
&\quad \text{If } \nexists \, b \in D_j \text{ such that } (a,b) \in R_{ij}: \\
&\quad\quad \text{Delete } a \text{ from } D_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Important Caveat:** Arc consistency does **not** solve the CSP — it merely prunes the search space. A CSP can be arc-consistent yet have **no solution** (e.g., if any domain becomes empty) or **multiple solutions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation Note):** Students often claim "arc consistency guarantees a solution." This is **FALSE**. Arc consistency only simplifies domains; it does not prove existence or uniqueness of solutions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a CSP Solving System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Statement] --> B[Variable Identification]
    B --> C[Domain Specification]
    C --> D[Constraint Enumeration]
    D --> E[Constraint Graph Construction]
    E --> F[Consistency Preprocessing]
    F --> G{Node Consistent?}
    G -- No --> H[Prune Invalid Domain Values]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I{Arc Consistent?}
    I -- No --> J[Apply AC3 or AC4 Algorithm]
    J --> F
    I -- Yes --> K[Search Phase Backtracking]
    K --> L{Solution Found?}
    L -- No --> M[Backtrack and Try New Branch]
    M --> K
    L -- Yes --> N[Output Solution Assignment]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Constraint Graph for the Australia Map Coloring Problem

```mermaid
graph LR
    WA((WA)) --- NT((NT))
    WA --- SA((SA))
    NT --- SA
    NT --- Q((Q))
    SA --- Q
    SA --- NSW((NSW))
    SA --- V((V))
    Q --- NSW
    NSW --- V
    style WA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style NT fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style NSW fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

*Note: Tasmania (T) is isolated (no constraints) and is omitted for clarity from the main connected component.*

### 4.3 CSP Solving Flow — Backtracking Algorithm Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start] --> V1[Select Unassigned Variable]
    V1 --> O1[Ordering Heuristic MRV]
    O1 --> V2[Try Each Value in Domain]
    V2 --> C1{Value Consistent with Assignment?}
    C1 -- No --> V2
    C1 -- Yes --> A1[Assign Value to Variable]
    A1 --> FC1{Forward Checking Triggers?}
    FC1 -- Yes --> FC2[Prune Neighbour Domains]
    FC2 --> D1{Any Domain Empty?}
    D1 -- Yes --> UNDO1[Undo and Backtrack]
    UNDO1 --> V2
    D1 -- No --> V1
    FC1 -- No --> V1
    V2 -- No Values Left --> B1{All Variables Assigned?}
    B1 -- No --> UNDO1
    B1 -- Yes --> SOL[Return Complete Assignment]
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style SOL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style UNDO1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style FC2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

### 4.4 Hypergraph for a Ternary Constraint (Cryptarithmetic-style)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((X)) --- E1((Constraint C1))
    B((Y)) --- E1
    C((Z)) --- E1
    E1[Relation: X plus Y equals Z]
    style E1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

*This hypergraph node E1 represents a single ternary constraint involving all three variables simultaneously, illustrating higher-order constraints that are converted to binary form for the AC-3 algorithm in advanced topics.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024]**

**Q1. Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Constraint Satisfaction Problem is formally defined as a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where **X** is a finite set of variables, **D** is a set of domains specifying the allowed values for each variable, and **C** is a set of constraints restricting the values the variables can take simultaneously. A solution is a complete assignment of values to all variables that satisfies every constraint. **Example:** In the map coloring problem, the variables are the regions, the domain of each is the set of colors, and the constraints require that any two adjacent regions have different colors. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between unary, binary, and higher-order constraints with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Unary constraint** involves a single variable, e.g., "Variable X must be greater than 5." **(1 mark)**
- **Binary constraint** involves two variables, e.g., "X must not equal Y" in map coloring. **(1 mark)**
- **Higher-order (ternary or more)** involves three or more variables, e.g., "X + Y = Z" in cryptarithmetic puzzles. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem. List and explain the three components of a CSP with a formal mathematical representation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the map of Australia with 7 regions: WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T. Each region must be assigned one of three colors {Red, Green, Blue} such that no two adjacent regions share the same color. The borders are: (WA–NT), (WA–SA), (NT–SA), (NT–Q), (SA–Q), (SA–NSW), (SA–V), (Q–NSW), (NSW–V). Formulate this as a CSP, draw the constraint graph, and compute the total number of complete assignments before and after applying the constraint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 marks — Full Definition]

A CSP is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ **(1 mark)** where:
- **X = {X₁, X₂, ..., Xₙ}** is a finite set of **variables**. **(1 mark)**
- **D = {D₁, D₂, ..., Dₙ}** is a set of **domains**, where Dᵢ is the finite non-empty set of values for Xᵢ. **(1 mark)**
- **C = {C₁, C₂, ..., Cₘ}** is a set of **constraints**, where each Cⱼ is a relation over a subset of variables (the constraint's scope) specifying which tuples of values are permitted. **(2 marks)**
- A **solution** is a complete assignment of a value to every variable that satisfies all constraints. **(1 mark)**
- The objective is to find one or all such assignments. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** [7 marks — Full Step-by-Step]

**Step 1 — Variable Identification (1 mark):**
$$X = \{WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T\}$$

**Step 2 — Domain Specification (1 mark):**
$$D_i = \{R, G, B\} \text{ for every region } i$$

**Step 3 — Constraint Enumeration (1 mark):** Nine binary inequality constraints, one for each border pair, e.g., $C_{WA,NT}: X_{WA} \neq X_{NT}$, and so on.

**Step 4 — Constraint Graph (2 marks):** Already shown in Section 4.2 (7 nodes, 9 edges; T is isolated).

**Step 5 — Search Space Calculation (2 marks):**

Total complete assignments (no constraints):

$$\prod_{i=1}^{7} \vert D_i \vert = 3^7 = 2187$$

After enforcing all 9 inequality constraints, the standard result is **18** valid colorings, so the reduction is from **2187 to 18** — a **99.18% reduction** in the search space.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the concept of a constraint graph. Why is it useful in solving CSPs? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the 4-Queens problem as a CSP. Identify the variables, domains, and constraints. How many binary constraints does this CSP have? Show the constraint graph. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 marks]

A **constraint graph** is a graph G = (V, E) where each node represents a variable and each edge (or hyperedge) represents a constraint between the connected variables. **(2 marks)**
- **Usefulness:** It provides a visual and computational structure for the CSP, allowing graphical algorithms to propagate constraints and prune the search space efficiently. It exposes the topology (e.g., tree-structured CSPs are solvable in linear time). **(2 marks)**
- **Example:** For the map coloring of Australia, the 7 regions are nodes and the 9 shared borders are edges; the resulting graph is planar. **(2 marks)**
- Arc consistency algorithms like AC-3 operate directly on the edges of this graph. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Variables (1 mark):** $X = \{Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4\}$ where $Q_i$ is the column of the queen in row $i$.

**Step 2 — Domain (1 mark):** $D_i = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ for all $i$.

**Step 3 — Constraints (2 marks):**
- *Column inequality:* $Q_i \neq Q_j$ for all $i \neq j$
- *Diagonal inequality:* $\vert Q_i - Q_j \vert \neq \vert i - j \vert$ for all $i \neq j$

**Step 4 — Number of Binary Constraints (1 mark):** There are 4 variables, so the number of unordered pairs is $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ pairs. Each pair has **one** binary constraint combining both column and diagonal inequalities. Total: **6 binary constraints**.

**Step 5 — Constraint Graph (2 marks):** The graph is the **complete graph K₄** — 4 nodes, with every pair of nodes connected by an edge.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Key Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state "finite" domains** when defining a CSP — losing 1 mark. Always specify *finite discrete* domains for Module 4.
> 2. **Confusing constraint graph with a state-space search tree** — the constraint graph is a *static* representation of the problem structure, NOT the search tree generated by backtracking.
> 3. **Claiming arc consistency finds a solution** — it does NOT; it only prunes domains. Always clarify that final solution still requires search.
> 4. **Not computing the search space size** when asked for "total assignments" — examiners expect $\prod \vert D_i \vert$ as a numerical value.
> 5. **Mixing up "constraint scope" with "constraint arity"** — they are the same, but use the term "arity" in formal answers.
> 6. **Omitting the relation form** of constraints — for a 14-mark question, both the **logical form** (e.g., $X_1 \neq X_2$) AND the **relational form** (e.g., $R = \{(R,G), (R,B), \ldots\}$) should ideally be present.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ A CSP is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$: **Variables, Domains, Constraints**.
- ✅ **Solution** = complete + consistent assignment.
- ✅ **Unary constraint** = 1 variable; **Binary** = 2; **Higher-order** = 3+.
- ✅ **Constraint Graph** = nodes are variables, edges are binary constraints.
- ✅ **Search space size** = $\prod \vert D_i \vert$ (without any constraint enforcement).
- ✅ **Node consistency** = every variable's domain satisfies all unary constraints.
- ✅ **Arc consistency** = for every value in $D_X$, there exists a supporting value in $D_Y$. Prunes domains but does NOT solve the CSP.
- ✅ **Binary CSP** = all constraints have arity ≤ 2; **non-binary** = otherwise.
- ✅ **Map coloring, N-Queens, Sudoku, Cryptarithmetic** are the four canonical KTU examples — be ready to formulate any of them.
- ✅ **Intentional constraints** use formulas; **Extensional constraints** enumerate allowed tuples.
- ✅ **CSPs are declarative** — you state *what* must hold, not *how* to find a solution.
- ✅ **Constraint Optimization Problems (COP)** add a cost function to soft constraints — out of scope for this module, but mention if asked.
- ✅ **Tree-structured CSPs** can be solved in $O(nd^2)$ time — a key result in advanced topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for CSP: Backtracking and Constraint Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT613 - AI WITH PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for CSP: Backtracking and Constraint Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is formally defined as a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:

- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ is a finite set of **variables**.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ is a finite set of **domains**, where $D_i$ is the set of permissible values for variable $X_i$.
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\}$ is a finite set of **constraints** that restrict the combinations of values the variables can simultaneously take.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — CSP Techniques**
> Module 4 emphasizes two principal algorithmic strategies for solving CSPs:
> (1) **Backtracking Search** (a systematic depth-first search with intelligent pruning)
> (2) **Constraint Propagation** (using constraints to reduce variable domains *before* and *during* search).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Exam Seating Arrangement" Problem

Imagine you are the Chief Superintendent of Examinations at a KTU-affiliated college. You must:
- Assign **150 students** (variables) to **50 benches** (domain values) in a hall.
- **Constraints**: No two students from the same branch sit adjacent; left-handers must be on the left side; invigilator visibility must be maintained.

You try one student on bench 1. Then bench 2. Then bench 3. You hit a constraint violation on bench 4 — so you **backtrack** to bench 3 and try a different value. While placing students, you also keep a **"future-availability"** list — once bench 4 is filled by a CS student, you immediately remove the "CS-adjacent" restriction from neighbouring benches. That live pruning is **constraint propagation**.

## 1.3 Why CSPs Matter in AI

CSPs elegantly model a vast class of real-world problems including **map coloring, scheduling, Sudoku, cryptographic key assignment, resource allocation, route planning, and configuration of hardware/software systems**. Instead of reasoning about goals (as in classical search), CSPs reason about constraints — making them exceptionally efficient for combinatorial problems.

## 1.4 Binary vs. Non-Binary CSPs

- **Binary CSP**: Each constraint involves at most 2 variables (e.g., $X_i \neq X_j$).
- **Non-Binary (n-ary) CSP**: A constraint involves $n$ variables (e.g., "all-different" over 4 variables).

> [!NOTE]
> Any non-binary CSP can be converted into an equivalent binary CSP using the **hidden variable transformation**, which is essential for applying binary propagation techniques like AC-3.

## 1.5 Visualization of CSP Solution Process

The CSP solving strategy has three layers:

$$
\text{CSP Solution} = \underbrace{\text{Variables}}_{\text{What}} + \underbrace{\text{Domains}}_{\text{Where from}} + \underbrace{\text{Constraints}}_{\text{What rules}}
$$

Then:

$$
\text{Solution} = \underbrace{\text{Constraint Propagation}}_{\text{Pre-process}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Backtracking Search}}_{\text{Search}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Heuristics}}_{\text{Guidance}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Backtracking Search — The Core Algorithm

**Backtracking** is a depth-first search where variables are assigned one at a time, and constraints are checked incrementally. If a partial assignment violates a constraint, the algorithm **undoes** (backtracks) the most recent assignment and tries a different value.

### 2.1.1 Generic Backtracking Algorithm

```
BACKTRACKING-SEARCH(csp):
    return BACKTRACK(csp, empty assignment)

BACKTRACK(csp, assignment):
    if assignment is complete: return assignment
    var = SELECT-UNASSIGNED-VARIABLE(csp, assignment)
    for each value in ORDER-DOMAIN-VALUES(var, assignment, csp):
        if value is consistent with assignment:
            add {var = value} to assignment
            inferences = INFERENCE(csp, assignment, var, value)
            if inferences ≠ failure:
                add inferences to assignment
            result = BACKTRACK(csp, assignment)
            if result ≠ failure: return result
        remove {var = value} and inferences from assignment
    return failure
```

### 2.1.2 Properties of Backtracking

| Property | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Yes (for finite domains) | Guaranteed to find a solution if one exists |
| **Optimality** | No (in basic form) | Does not guarantee minimum-cost solution |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(d^n)$ worst case | $d$ = domain size, $n$ = number of variables |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | Recursion stack only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Plain backtracking explores a search tree of size $O(d^n)$. The goal of CSP techniques is to **prune** this tree aggressively — ideally, exponentially.

## 2.2 Variable & Value Ordering Heuristics

### 2.2.1 Minimum Remaining Values (MRV) — "Fail-First Heuristic"

Choose the variable with the **fewest legal values** remaining in its domain. The intuition: if a variable is about to fail, detect it early to prune the search tree.

$$
\text{MRV}(X_i) = \arg\min_{X_i \in \text{unassigned}} \vert D_i \vert
$$

**Tie-breaker — Degree Heuristic**: For variables with equal MRV counts, choose the one with the highest degree (most constraints on remaining unassigned variables).

$$
\text{Degree}(X_i) = \sum_{X_j \in \text{unassigned}, X_j \neq X_i} \mathbb{1}[\text{constraint}(X_i, X_j) \in C]
$$

### 2.2.2 Least Constraining Value (LCV) Heuristic

Given a chosen variable, choose the value that **rules out the fewest choices** for the neighboring variables.

$$
\text{LCV}(v) = \arg\min_{v \in D_i} \sum_{X_j \in \text{neighbors}(X_i)} \mathbb{1}[v \text{ eliminates value in } D_j]
$$

## 2.3 Constraint Propagation Techniques

### 2.3.1 Node Consistency

A variable $X_i$ is **node-consistent** if every value in its domain $D_i$ satisfies all unary constraints on $X_i$.

### 2.3.2 Arc Consistency (AC-3 Algorithm)

A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with $X_j$ if for every value $v \in D_i$, there exists some value $w \in D_j$ that satisfies the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$.

Formally:

$$
\forall v \in D_i, \; \exists w \in D_j \; \text{such that} \; (v, w) \in C_{ij}
$$

If $v$ has no such supporting value $w$, then $v$ is removed from $D_i$.

### 2.3.3 The AC-3 Algorithm (Mackworth, 1977)

```
AC-3(csp):
    queue = all arcs (X_i, X_j) in csp
    while queue is not empty:
        (X_i, X_j) = REMOVE-FIRST(queue)
        if REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
            if size of D_i == 0: return False
            for each X_k in NEIGHBORS(X_i) - {X_j}:
                add (X_k, X_i) to queue
    return True

REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
    revised = False
    for each x in D_i:
        if no value y in D_j satisfies constraint(X_i=x, X_j=y):
            delete x from D_i
            revised = True
    return revised
```

**Complexity of AC-3**: $O(cd^3)$ where $c$ = number of binary constraints and $d$ = max domain size.

### 2.3.4 Forward Checking

A simpler form of propagation. When a variable $X_i$ is assigned, **forward checking** immediately removes from the domains of all unassigned neighbors any value that is inconsistent with the assigned value.

| Technique | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Checking** | Detects failures 1 step ahead | Does not propagate further than neighbors |
| **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** | Detects failures deep in the tree | More expensive per iteration |

### 2.3.5 Path Consistency (PC-2)

A **2-variable path** through a third variable must be consistent. Path consistency ensures that for any pair $(X_i, X_j)$ and any intermediate $X_k$, if $(v, w)$ satisfies $C_{ij}$ and $w$ is consistent with some value of $X_k$, then the pair is path-consistent.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| $X = \{X_1, \dots, X_n\}$ | Set of variables | The unknowns to assign |
| $D_i$ | Domain of variable $X_i$ | Possible values for $X_i$ |
| $C_{ij}$ | Binary constraint between $X_i, X_j$ | Restricts $(X_i, X_j)$ pairs |
| $C_i$ | Unary constraint on $X_i$ | Restricts values of $X_i$ alone |
| **MRV** | Minimum Remaining Values | Fail-first variable selection |
| **Degree** | Highest constraints on unassigned | Tie-breaker for MRV |
| **LCV** | Least Constraining Value | Keep-options-open value |
| **AC-3** | Arc Consistency Algorithm 3 | $O(cd^3)$ time complexity |
| **FC** | Forward Checking | Removes inconsistent neighbors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: CSP techniques underpin **VLSI chip routing, compiler register allocation, airline crew scheduling, cryptography (key distribution), and configuration management tools** — making them indispensable in modern software engineering.

## 2.5 Intelligent Backtracking

Standard backtracking may "rediscover" the same conflict repeatedly. **Conflict-directed backjumping (CBJ)** jumps back to the variable that caused the failure, rather than the chronologically previous one. This avoids thrashing.

The **conflict set** for variable $X_j$ is the set of earlier-assigned variables connected to $X_j$ via constraints that are jointly responsible for $X_j$'s failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Map Coloring of Australia

Consider the classic Australia map-coloring CSP (Russel & Norvig):

- **Variables**: WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T (7 regions)
- **Domains**: $D_i = \{\text{red}, \text{green}, \text{blue}\}$ for all variables
- **Constraints**: Adjacent regions must have different colors.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Backtracking Execution with MRV + LCV

**Step 1**: Initial state — all domains are {red, green, blue}. All variables have MRV = 3.
Apply **Degree Heuristic tie-breaker**: SA has the highest degree (5 neighbors: WA, NT, Q, NSW, V).

**Step 2**: Assign SA = **red** (using LCV — red rules out fewest neighbor values).

**Step 3**: Apply **Forward Checking**. Remove red from domains of WA, NT, Q, NSW, V.

| Variable | Domain after FC |
|---|---|
| WA | {green, blue} |
| NT | {green, blue} |
| SA | {red} |
| Q | {green, blue} |
| NSW | {green, blue} |
| V | {green, blue} |
| T | {red, green, blue} |

**Step 4**: Recurse. Apply MRV — all unassigned non-T variables now have MRV = 2.
Apply Degree — Q has degree 3 (highest among the tied).

**Step 5**: Assign Q = **green**.

**Step 6**: Forward check — remove green from neighbors of Q: NT, NSW, SA (already assigned red, no effect), V.

| Variable | Domain after FC |
|---|---|
| WA | {green, blue} |
| NT | {blue} |
| Q | {green} |
| NSW | {blue} |
| V | {blue} |
| T | {red, green, blue} |

**Step 7**: Recurse. Apply MRV — NT has the smallest domain (size 1).

**Step 8**: Assign NT = **blue**.

**Step 9**: Forward check — remove blue from neighbors of NT: WA, SA (already red), Q (already green).

| Variable | Domain after FC |
|---|---|
| WA | {green} |
| NT | {blue} |
| SA | {red} |
| Q | {green} |
| NSW | {blue} |
| V | {blue} |
| T | {red, green, blue} |

**Step 10**: Recurse. WA domain = {green}. Assign WA = **green**.

**Step 11**: Forward check — remove green from neighbors of WA: NT (already blue, OK).

**Step 12**: Recurse. NSW domain = {blue}. Assign NSW = **blue**.

**Step 13**: Recurse. V domain = {blue}. Assign V = **blue**.

**Step 14**: Recurse. T has domain {red, green, blue}. T has no neighbors with assigned colors.
Assign T = **red** (arbitrary, all values are valid).

**Step 15**: All variables assigned → **SUCCESS** in **7 recursive calls**.

### 3.1.2 Resulting Assignment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WA} &= \text{green} \\
\text{NT} &= \text{blue} \\
\text{SA} &= \text{red} \\
\text{Q}  &= \text{green} \\
\text{NSW} &= \text{blue} \\
\text{V}  &= \text{blue} \\
\text{T}  &= \text{red}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Trace of AC-3 Algorithm

Let's trace AC-3 on the Australia map with all domains = {red, green, blue}.

**Initial Queue**: All directed arcs between neighbors.

$$
\text{Queue} = \{ (WA,NT), (NT,WA), (WA,SA), (SA,WA), (NT,SA), (SA,NT), (NT,Q), (Q,NT), (SA,Q), (Q,SA), (SA,NSW), (NSW,SA), (Q,NSW), (NSW,Q), (SA,V), (V,SA) \}
$$

**Iteration 1**: Dequeue (WA, NT). REVISE(WA, NT):
For each $x \in D_{WA} = \{R, G, B\}$, check if $\exists y \in D_{NT}$ with $y \neq x$. Since $|D_{NT}| = 3$, every $x$ has support. **No revision.**

**Iteration 2**: Dequeue (NT, WA). Same logic — no revision.

**... (similar for SA, Q, NSW, V arcs)** — because all domains are full, **AC-3 makes no revisions**.

This illustrates a key point: AC-3 is most effective **after partial assignment**, when domains are smaller.

### 3.2.1 AC-3 Trace After SA = red

Suppose we have already assigned SA = red. Domains become:
- $D_{WA} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{NT} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{Q} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{NSW} = \{G, B\}$, $D_{V} = \{G, B\}$, $D_T = \{R, G, B\}$.

Now queue = $\{ (WA,NT), \dots \}$. The propagation does nothing because no domain is empty. **AC-3 detects no inconsistency at this stage**, but combined with **forward checking during search**, it dramatically prunes branches.

## 3.3 Comparative Complexity Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{plain-backtrack}}(d, n) &= O(d^n) \\
T_{\text{backtrack + FC}}(d, n) &= O(d^n) \text{ but with much smaller constant} \\
T_{\text{AC-3}} &= O(c d^3) \\
T_{\text{min-conflicts}} &= O(n^2 d) \text{ (for local search variants)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of variables
- $d$ = domain size
- $c$ = number of binary constraints

## 3.4 Detailed Algorithm Steps for Backtracking + AC-3 Integration

**Algorithm**: `BACKTRACK(csp, assignment)` with AC-3 inference

**Step 1**: If assignment is complete, return assignment.

**Step 2**: Select variable $X_i$ using MRV (with Degree tie-breaker).

**Step 3**: Order values of $X_i$ using LCV.

**Step 4**: For each value $v$ in ordered domain:
  - (a) Add $X_i = v$ to assignment.
  - (b) Make a copy of all domains: $\text{domain\_copy} = csp.\text{domains}()$.
  - (c) Apply AC-3 to the modified CSP.
  - (d) If AC-3 succeeds (no empty domains), recurse: $\text{result} = \text{BACKTRACK}(csp, assignment)$.
  - (e) If result ≠ failure, return result.
  - (f) Otherwise, restore domains from `domain_copy` and remove $X_i = v$.

**Step 5**: Return failure.

## 3.5 Derivation of the MRV Theorem

**Claim**: Choosing the variable with the minimum remaining values (MRV) minimizes the expected search-tree size.

**Intuitive Proof Sketch**:

Let $p_i = \vert D_i \vert$ for unassigned variable $X_i$. The branching factor when choosing $X_i$ is $p_i$. The expected total search-tree size is bounded by:

$$
E[\text{size}] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

This product is **minimized when each $p_i$ is small and the assignment order follows ascending $p_i$** (greedy by MRV). Therefore, MRV minimizes expected work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Source**: This analysis is foundational in CSP theory (see Mackworth, 1977; Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*, 4th ed., Chapter 5). MRV is also called the "fail-first" heuristic because it triggers early backtracking on variables most likely to fail.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CSP Solving Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CSP Input Variables Domains Constraints] --> B[Initial Constraint Propagation AC-3 Node Consistency]
    B --> C{Domains Empty}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Return Failure No Solution]
    C -- No --> D[MRV Variable Selection Degree Tie Break]
    D --> E[LCV Value Ordering]
    E --> F[Assign Variable equals value]
    F --> G[Constraint Check Local Consistency]
    G --> H{Constraint Satisfied}
    H -- No --> I[Undo Assignment Backtrack]
    I --> E
    H -- Yes --> J[Forward Checking Remove Inconsistent Neighbor Domains]
    J --> K{Any Domain Empty}
    K -- Yes --> I
    K -- No --> L[Recursive AC-3 Propagation]
    L --> M{All Variables Assigned}
    M -- No --> D
    M -- Yes --> N[Return Complete Assignment Solution]
```

## 4.2 CSP Search Tree with Pruning (Australia Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root Start] --> S1[SA red MRV Degree]
    S1 --> S2[Q green MRV LCV]
    S2 --> S3[NT blue MRV Domain 1]
    S3 --> S4[V blue Only Valid]
    S4 --> S5[NSW blue Only Valid]
    S5 --> S6[WA green Only Valid]
    S6 --> S7[T red Any Valid]
    S7 --> X[Solution Complete Assignment]
    S2 -. failed alternative .-> F1[Q blue Pruned by FC]
    F1 -. dead end .-> Z[Backtrack to SA]
```

## 4.3 AC-3 Algorithm Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CSP Definition] --> B[Initialize Queue All Arcs]
    B --> C[Pop Arc Xi Xj]
    C --> D[REVISE Xi Xj]
    D --> E{Domain Xi Reduced}
    E -- Yes --> F{Domain Xi Empty}
    F -- Yes --> FAIL[Return False Inconsistent]
    F -- No --> G[Add Arcs Xk Xi for all neighbors Xk]
    G --> C
    E -- No --> C
    C -. Queue Empty .-> SUCCESS[Return True Consistent]
```

## 4.4 Heuristic Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[CSP Solver] --> V[Select Variable]
    V --> MRV[Apply MRV Min Domain Size]
    MRV --> TIE{Tied Variables}
    TIE -- Yes --> DEG[Apply Degree Heuristic Max Constraints]
    TIE -- No --> ORD[Proceed]
    DEG --> ORD
    ORD --> VAL[Order Values]
    VAL --> LCV[Apply LCV Least Constraining]
    LCV --> ASSIGN[Assign Variable]
    ASSIGN --> PROP[Constraint Propagation]
    PROP --> FC[Forward Checking]
    PROP --> AC[Arc Consistency AC3]
    FC --> REC[Recursive Search]
    AC --> REC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note**: These flowcharts depict the **functional architecture** of CSP solving — showing how variables, domains, constraints, heuristics, and propagation interact. They are not physical schematics but logical flow representations suitable for algorithmic analysis in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). List any three real-world problems that can be formulated as CSPs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) is defined by a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ where:
- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ is the set of variables,
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ is the set of domains (each $D_i$ is the set of possible values for $X_i$),
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m\}$ is the set of constraints that specify allowable combinations of values.

**Three real-world CSP examples** (any 3, 1 mark each):
1. **Map coloring** — coloring a map such that no two adjacent regions share the same color.
2. **Sudoku** — filling a 9×9 grid such that each row, column, and 3×3 box contains digits 1–9 exactly once.
3. **N-Queens** — placing N queens on an N×N chessboard such that no two queens attack each other.
4. **Job-shop scheduling** — assigning jobs to time slots subject to resource and precedence constraints.
5. **Cryptarithmetic puzzles** — assigning digits to letters such that a given arithmetic equation holds (e.g., SEND + MORE = MONEY).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Explain the Minimum Remaining Values (MRV) heuristic for variable selection in CSP. Why is it called the "fail-first" heuristic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Minimum Remaining Values (MRV)** heuristic selects the unassigned variable with the **fewest legal values remaining in its domain** to assign next.

$$
\text{MRV}(X_i) = \arg\min_{X_i \in \text{unassigned}} \vert D_i \vert
$$

**Why "fail-first"?** (2 marks)

It is called the **fail-first heuristic** because by choosing the variable most likely to fail (smallest domain), the algorithm detects **infeasibility early** in the search tree. Early detection allows the algorithm to prune large subtrees, avoiding wasted computational effort. It also ensures that if a constraint forces an early failure, we discover it before investing effort in less constrained branches.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Define the terms *node consistency*, *arc consistency*, and *path consistency* in the context of constraint satisfaction problems. Explain the AC-3 algorithm with its pseudocode and analyze its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following CSP:
- Variables: $X, Y, Z$
- Domains: $D_X = D_Y = D_Z = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
- Constraints: $X < Y$, $Y < Z$, $X + Z = 6$

Solve this CSP using **backtracking search with forward checking and MRV heuristic**. Show the complete trace, including the backtrack points if any. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Node Consistency (1 mark)**: A variable $X_i$ is node-consistent if every value in its domain $D_i$ satisfies all unary constraints on $X_i$.

**Arc Consistency (2 marks)**: A variable $X_i$ is arc-consistent with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $x \in D_i$, there exists some value $y \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint $C_{ij}(x, y)$ is satisfied. If no such $y$ exists, then $x$ must be removed from $D_i$.

$$
\forall x \in D_i, \exists y \in D_j \text{ such that } (x, y) \text{ satisfies } C_{ij}
$$

**Path Consistency (1 mark)**: A pair of variables $(X_i, X_j)$ is path-consistent with respect to a third variable $X_k$ if, for every pair of values $(x, y)$ satisfying $C_{ij}$, there exists a value $z \in D_k$ such that both $C_{ik}(x, z)$ and $C_{jk}(y, z)$ hold. Path consistency is stronger than arc consistency and considers 3-variable interactions.

**AC-3 Algorithm Pseudocode (2 marks)**:

```
AC-3(csp):
    queue = all arcs (X_i, X_j) in csp
    while queue not empty:
        (X_i, X_j) = REMOVE-FIRST(queue)
        if REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
            if size(D_i) == 0: return False
            for each X_k in NEIGHBORS(X_i) minus {X_j}:
                add (X_k, X_i) to queue
    return True

REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
    revised = False
    for each x in D_i:
        if no value y in D_j satisfies constraint(X_i = x, X_j = y):
            delete x from D_i
            revised = True
    return revised
```

**Time Complexity Analysis (1 mark)**: AC-3 runs in $O(c \cdot d^3)$ time, where $c$ is the number of binary constraints and $d$ is the maximum domain size. This is because there are at most $2c$ arcs in the queue, each arc is processed at most $d$ times (each time the domain reduces), and REVISE takes $O(d^2)$ time.

### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Initial State** (0.5 marks):

| Variable | Domain |
|---|---|
| $X$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |
| $Y$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |
| $Z$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |

**Step 2 — Apply AC-3 / Initial Constraint Propagation** (1 mark):

From $X + Z = 6$: valid pairs $(X, Z)$ are $(1,5), (2,4), (3,3), (4,2), (5,1)$. All values 1–5 remain possible in $D_X$ and $D_Z$ since each appears in at least one valid pair.

**Step 3 — MRV Selection** (0.5 marks): All domains equal (size 5). Choose $X$ (any will do).

**Step 4 — Assign $X = 1$** (0.5 marks). Apply forward checking:
- From $X < Y$: $Y$ must be $> 1$, so $D_Y = \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$.
- From $X + Z = 6$ with $X = 1$: $Z = 5$, so $D_Z = \{5\}$.

| Variable | Domain |
|---|---|
| $X$ | $\{1\}$ |
| $Y$ | $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |
| $Z$ | $\{5\}$ |

**Step 5 — MRV Selection** (0.5 marks): $Z$ has smallest domain (size 1). Assign $Z = 5$.

Apply FC: $Y < Z = 5$, so $D_Y = \{2, 3, 4\}$.

**Step 6 — Assign $Y$** (0.5 marks). $D_Y = \{2, 3, 4\}$. Choose $Y = 2$.

Apply FC: $X < Y$ means $1 < 2$ ✓. All constraints satisfied.

| Variable | Value |
|---|---|
| $X$ | 1 |
| $Y$ | 2 |
| $Z$ | 5 |

**Verification** (1 mark):
- $X < Y$: $1 < 2$ ✓
- $Y < Z$: $2 < 5$ ✓
- $X + Z = 6$: $1 + 5 = 6$ ✓

**Solution found**: $(X, Y, Z) = (1, 2, 5)$ with no backtracks needed. **Other valid solutions**: $(1, 3, 5), (1, 4, 5), (2, 3, 4), (2, 4, 4)$... actually recheck: $(2,4,4)$ fails $Y<Z$; valid ones include $(2, 3, 4), (2, 4, 4)$ invalid, $(2, 4, 4)$ — $Y<Z$ means $4<4$ false. So with $X=2, Z=4, Y<4, Y>2$, $Y=3$. $(3, 4, 3)$ etc.

**Final answer (one valid solution)**: $X=1, Y=2, Z=5$.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain **backtracking search** for constraint satisfaction problems. With a suitable example, illustrate how the **MRV and LCV heuristics** improve the efficiency of the search. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **AC-3 algorithm** to the following graph coloring problem with 4 variables:
- Variables: $A, B, C, D$
- Domains: $D_A = D_B = D_C = D_D = \{\text{red}, \text{green}, \text{blue}\}$
- Constraints: $A \neq B$, $B \neq C$, $C \neq D$, $A \neq D$, $A \neq C$

Trace the AC-3 execution step-by-step and show the final domains after AC-3 termination. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Backtracking Search Definition (2 marks)**:

Backtracking search is a **depth-first search algorithm** for solving CSPs. Variables are assigned sequentially, and the algorithm checks each assignment against the constraints. When a partial assignment violates a constraint, the algorithm **backtracks** to the most recent variable and tries a different value. Backtracking forms a search tree where each level corresponds to one variable's assignment.

**Pseudocode (2 marks)**:

```
BACKTRACK(csp, assignment):
    if assignment is complete: return assignment
    var = SELECT-UNASSIGNED-VARIABLE(csp)  // Uses MRV
    for each value in ORDER-DOMAIN-VALUES(var, csp):  // Uses LCV
        if value is consistent with assignment:
            add {var = value} to assignment
            inferences = INFERENCE(csp, var, value)  // Forward Checking
            if inferences ≠ failure:
                result = BACKTRACK(csp, assignment)
                if result ≠ failure: return result
            remove {var = value} and inferences from assignment
    return failure
```

**MRV & LCV Illustration with Example (3 marks)**:

Consider a simple CSP:
- Variables: $A, B, C$
- Domains: $D_A = \{1, 2\}$, $D_B = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $D_C = \{1, 2\}$
- Constraints: $A \neq B$, $B \neq C$

**Without heuristics**, backtracking explores: A=1→B=1✗→B=2→C=1✗→C=2✓ (find). It explores 5 nodes.

**With MRV**: Choose $A$ first (domain size 2, tied with $C$; by Degree: $B$ has degree 2, $A$ has degree 1, $C$ has degree 1; tie between $A$ and $C$). Let us choose $A$. Try values in **LCV order** (least constraining first).

For $A = 1$: How many values of $B$ remain? $B \neq 1$, so $D_B = \{2, 3\}$ — 2 values remain (highly constraining since $B$ loses more for $A=2$). So **LCV prefers $A = 2$** (which only eliminates one value from $B$). Try $A = 2$ first.

For $A = 2$: $B \in \{1, 3\}$, $C \in \{1, 2\}$. Try $B = 1$: $C \in \{2\}$ (only $C \neq 1$). Solution: $A=2, B=1, C=2$ in **3 nodes** instead of 5.

**Improvement**: MRV + LCV reduces explored nodes by **~40%** in this small example; savings compound exponentially on larger CSPs.

### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Initial State** (0.5 marks):

| Variable | Domain |
|---|---|
| $A$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $C$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |

**Adjacency (constraint) list**:
- $A \rightarrow B, C, D$
- $B \rightarrow A, C$
- $C \rightarrow A, B, D$
- $D \rightarrow A, C$

**Initial Queue** (0.5 marks): All directed arcs — 8 arcs total:
$(A,B), (B,A), (A,C), (C,A), (A,D), (D,A), (B,C), (C,B), (C,D), (D,C)$

**AC-3 Execution** (5 marks):

| Iteration | Arc Processed | REVISE Result | Queue After |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(A, B)$ | No revision (each $x \in D_A$ has support in $D_B$) | 7 arcs left |
| 2 | $(B, A)$ | No revision | 6 arcs left |
| 3 | $(A, C)$ | No revision | 5 arcs left |
| 4 | $(C, A)$ | No revision | 4 arcs left |
| 5 | $(A, D)$ | No revision | 3 arcs left |
| 6 | $(D, A)$ | No revision | 2 arcs left |
| 7 | $(B, C)$ | No revision | 1 arc left |
| 8 | $(C, B)$ | No revision | 0 arcs left |

Since all domains start at full size (3) and no individual domain becomes empty after any revision, **AC-3 makes no revisions on this unassigned CSP**.

**Final Domains after AC-3 Termination** (1 mark):

| Variable | Domain |
|---|---|
| $A$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $C$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{R, G, B\}$ |

**Conclusion** (0.5 marks): AC-3 alone **cannot reduce domains** when all variables have full domains and no domain becomes empty. AC-3's power emerges **during search** (after partial assignments), where it propagates constraints from assigned variables. To solve this CSP, we must combine AC-3 with backtracking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing AC-3 with Forward Checking**: Forward Checking only checks the **immediate neighbors** of the assigned variable; AC-3 propagates constraints **transitively** across the constraint graph.
> 2. **MRV ≠ Random variable selection**: Students often forget that MRV requires a **Degree tie-breaker** when domain sizes are equal. Failure to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Complexity notation error**: Writing $O(d^n)$ for AC-3 is wrong; AC-3 is $O(c d^3)$.
> 4. **Forgetting to restore domains**: When backtracking undoes an assignment, all domain reductions must be reverted. Students often miss this, leading to incorrect traces.
> 5. **Mixing up LCV with MRV**: LCV is a **value-ordering** heuristic (applied after the variable is chosen), while MRV is a **variable-selection** heuristic (applied first).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CSP Techniques**

- ✅ A CSP is the triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$: variables, domains, constraints.
- ✅ **Backtracking** is depth-first search with constraint checking and undoing of failed assignments.
- ✅ **Backtracking complexity**: $O(d^n)$ worst case without heuristics.
- ✅ **MRV (Minimum Remaining Values)** selects the variable with the smallest current domain — fail-first heuristic.
- ✅ **Degree Heuristic** breaks MRV ties by selecting the variable with the most constraints on unassigned variables.
- ✅ **LCV (Least Constraining Value)** chooses the value that rules out the fewest neighbor values.
- ✅ **Node Consistency**: all values satisfy unary constraints.
- ✅ **Arc Consistency**: $\forall x \in D_i, \exists y \in D_j$ supporting the constraint.
- ✅ **AC-3 Algorithm** has time complexity $O(c \cdot d^3)$ and uses a queue of arcs.
- ✅ **Forward Checking** is a weaker form of propagation — only immediate neighbors.
- ✅ **Path Consistency** extends arc consistency to 3-variable interactions.
- ✅ **Intelligent Backtracking (CBJ)** jumps back to the variable causing the failure, not the chronologically previous one.
- ✅ **Combining backtracking + MRV + LCV + AC-3** yields the strongest practical CSP solver.
- ✅ **CSP applications**: map coloring, Sudoku, scheduling, VLSI design, cryptographic key assignment, resource allocation.
- ✅ **Binary CSPs** are convertible to/from non-binary CSPs using the **hidden variable transformation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip**: For 14-mark questions, always provide a **complete trace** with a domain table at each step. Examiners award partial credit for correct intermediate steps even if the final solution is wrong — but only if the trace is clearly shown. Practice the Australia map example until you can reproduce it in under 10 minutes.
